“Fairy tales are 
more than true – 
not because they 
tell us dragons 
exist, but because 
they tell us 
dragons can be 
beaten.” G. K. Chesterton

3
The Fairytale of 
the Child Saviour
Once upon a time... the world of fairytales was on the verge of ending, forsaken by 
children who grew up too fast and forgot the value of old stories. Within the void left by 
failing stories, darkness and oblivion blossomed. All the inhabitants of fairytales wept with 
fear at the long-dreaded end.
“ All it would take is one last reader. One single young heart willing to believe in us still, ” 
sighed the weary Fairytale King.

4
But there was a Child who still believed in fairytales. Alone and abandoned by everyone, the 
Child found shelter in those old stories. Within was protection from the world outside, too real 
and too cruel. Lost among myths and tales, he forgot the pain of a family unable to give him the 
childhood he deserved. “I love all fairytales full of heroes and villains to defeat, ” said the Child. 
“Why doesn’t anyone write new fairytales? If only I knew how, I would do it, ” the little dreamer 
kept repeating. 
The Fairytale King noticed the little dreamer, for if even a single reader 
was ready to believe in fairytales, the whole world would continue to do so. 
Thus the Child became the Saviour. From his dreams, fairytales drew new life. 
As a reward, the Fairytale King granted the Child Saviour a wish.

5
“Thanks to you we are remembered. I 
trust you will find in your heart the 
proper reward to ask for. ”
For a long time, the Child 
searched his heart for the most 
beautiful wish he could think 
of. However, hidden in his 
heart’s innermost corner, 
he found his own shadow. 
The One Who Plots in 
the Shadows emerged 
from the future, 
driven by rejection, 
But every light needs its shadow.
So it was that the villains became good, the heroes became evil.
And all fairytales shattered.
whispering in the Child Saviour’s 
mind a malicious intention. The 
Child Saviour truly believed 
that it was a good idea. 
Innocent, he said, “I would give 
new life to the rogues, a chance at 
redemption for every character 
who has been relegated to the 
role of villain for centuries. ”

6
Introduction
 
Broken Tales tells dark stories, reinterpreting 
fairytales from an upside-down perspective. Players will 
become part of the Order, a secret group operating on 
behalf of the Papacy to investigate supernatural events 
beyond human comprehension. Moving through an 18th 
century Europe imbued with mysticism and horror, the 
main characters of Broken Tales are nothing less than the 
former antagonists of fairytales, thrown into a new world 
resulting from the wish of the Child Savior.
The Game System
Broken Tales uses a role-playing rule system called Monad 
Echo. The system aims to be fast, encourage storytelling, 
and avoid pauses while playing. All this, while allowing 
Players a great degree of freedom. 
This rulebook is based on the Monad System, a universal 
role-playing system created by The World Anvil collective. 
Monad Echo is not a lite version of the Monad System, but 
rather a totally new system that shares terminology and 
action resolution mechanics with the original system while 
focusing on a more narrative and cinematic style. 
Already two other games featuring Monad Echo have 
been published in Italian: Evolution Pulse Rebirth and  
Valraven: The Chronicles of Blood and Iron. If you like the 
mechanics and dynamics showcased in this manual, know 
that you will find them in these games as well, adapted to 
the narrative and gameplay features of each setting. 
From now on we will refer to Monad Echo as “the Broken 
Tales system” or more simply “the system” . 
Glossary of Game 
Terminologies
Additional Successes: Further Successes to be added 
to the Base Successes, obtained by spending Soma, rolling 
dice or due to the effect of Gifts. 
Agenda: Description of an NPC’s purpose and goals.
Base Successes:  The number of successes a Hunter 
starts with when facing a Position or Defense check.
Defense Check: The game mechanic by which a Hunter 
reacts to the actions of an NPC or a Threat, managed by the 
Storyteller.
Dark Presence: NPC related to the world of fairytales 
and reborn in their “broken” version in the world of Broken 
Tales.

7
Descriptor: A short sentence de-
scribing a salient characteristic of a 
Hunter, NPC, or aspect of the world 
of Broken Tales. 
Exchange: A short time unit defin-
ing the order of actions for each par -
ticipant in a conflict situation. 
Experience Points: Abbreviated as 
XP, it’s the numerical value that indi-
cates the experience gained by a Hunt-
er during their adventures. 
Failure: Indicates that a Hunter’s 
action has failed because of an unsuc-
cessful Position or Defense Check.
Gift: A special ability of a Hunter, 
NPC, or Threat that activates particu-
lar effects in the game.
Hunter: A classic fairytale villain re-
born as an agent of the Order.
Interlude: A specific moment 
during a Session when Hunters catch 
their breath, spend XP , and recover 
Soma and Wounds.
Keywords: Single-themed words 
that help Players to focus on narrative 
details in a direct way, stimulating the 
imagination and helping everyone in-
volved create interesting details on the 
fly.
NPC: Acronym for Non-Player Char-
acter. All non-protagonist characters, 
fantastic creatures, Dark Presences, 
and in general any character managed 
by the Storyteller. NPCs are divided 
into three types: Main, Minor, and 
Dark Presences.
Opposition Level: Shortened as OL, 
it’s the difficulty value associated with 
an NPC or Threat.
Outcome: Indicates a good result in a 
Position or Defense check and can be 
of three types: with Cost, Standard, or 
with Increment.
Player: Any participant in a Session 
of Broken Tales. One Player must take 
the role of Storyteller, often the one 
who has read this book in full. All the 
other participants will play a Hunter.
Position Check: The game mechan-
ic by which a Hunter tries to succeed 
at actions they’ve described in the 
narrative.
Penalty: Value to be subtracted from 
Base Successes, derived from NPC 
Gifts.
Scene: The time unit during which a 
Session is played.
Scenario: A complete Broken Tales 
story that reinterprets and “breaks” a 
classic fairytale.
Session: A single evening/afternoon 
of play.
Soma: A value that quantifies the in-
ner strength and ability to tap into the 
inner energies of a Hunter. Spending 
Soma can grant automatic positive 
Outcomes during Checks or activate 
some Gifts.
Threat: A generic term for an ob-
stacle or complex situation that has 
its own Descriptor and an OL, which 
could oppose a Hunter.
Wounds: Represents the ability of a 
creature (Hunter or NPC) to resist any 
source of adversity, whether physical 
or mental.

8
About Role-
Playing Games
A role-playing game is a recreational 
activity where all participants are involved 
in a conversation aimed at creating a 
story. In our case, the main characters 
are the Hunters and the world in which 
they move is the Broken Europe of Broken 
Tales. Imagine you are helping to write a 
screenplay or novel, in which the plot is 
created in real time by the players at the 
table, each making contributions.
There are two well-defined roles during a 
game:
Player – the one who moves and makes a 
single Hunter act, equipped with a Sheet. 
A Player is one of the main characters 
of the Scenario and the narrated events 
revolve around them. The narrative 
authority of a Player is focused on their 
Hunter. They will have to decide how to 
act and react to everything the Storyteller 
will introduce during a Session.
Storyteller – this is the role that most 
closely resembles a film director. The 
Storyteller brings the Broken Tales 
Scenarios to life, describing locations and 
people, as well as reacting to the Hunters’ 
decisions. The Storyteller has a different 
set of duties than the other Players but 
isn’t solely responsible for how a Session 
unfolds. Simply put, the rules provide 
them with tools and procedures to manage 
the conversation from an alternative point 
of view.
To recap
The Storyteller introduces the Hunters 
to scenes and situations from the chosen 
Scenario. Players narrate the exploits 
of their Hunters and how they react to 
situations. When the conversation reaches 
a point where the outcome is uncertain, 
it’s time for a Position or Defense Check to 
determine the Outcome or Failure of the 
Hunters’ actions. The conversation then 
resumes its flow until the next Check. 
This pattern will help create a story in 
which each Player at the table features 
as a protagonist. The goals are chosen by 
the Hunters and the themes are expressed 
in the Scenarios by the Storyteller. When 
combined, Players and Storytellers create 
a unique narrative in the Broken Tales 
style.
Playing materials
To play, you will need what we listed 
below. Some material will need to be 
printed or procured. Hobby and board 
game stores might be helpful. Get:
 ◆ Some common 6-sided dice. Each 
Player can have their own set for 
convenience. The Storyteller never 
rolls dice and doesn’t need a backup. 
 ◆ One Hunter’s Sheet for each Play-
er, excluding the Storyteller. Plus, a

9
printout of a Bookmark for each Hunter.
 ◆ Pencils and erasers to write on the Bookmarks and 
keep track of the Hunters’ progress throughout the 
game. 
 ◆ Markers of any kind, such as small colored stones or 
tokens, are useful to quickly keep track of each Hunter’s 
Tokens and Soma Points, even if not essential.
Reality and fiction
The setting of Broken Tales presents an alternative 
Eighteenth century strongly inspired by historical Europe. 
Grafted onto this backdrop are several very different 
realities and fantasies, such as imaginary kingdoms and 
supernatural creatures, that strongly differentiate Broken 
Tales from a specific historical context. This game presents 
not just another time, but an original world, pervaded 
by darkness, where you can play your Broken Tales. 
Storytellers and Hunters will be free to sketch details of 
locations. As a general reference, it may be useful to know 
that the year 1780 is the starting point of the Core Book 
Scenarios’ hypothetical timeline.
Game Modes
Broken Tales is a modular game, and can adapt to the needs 
of your playgroup. The fastest way to experience the game 
(useful also for demos and one-shots) is to select one of 
the ready-made Scenarios and pre-generated Hunters and 
start playing right away. Each Scenario is designed to be 
played dynamically. Events will move along in response 
to the Hunters’ actions, making each Session unique. Y ou 
won’t find a classic pre-written storyline. The Storyteller 
can have fun, moving Events and NPCs along, reacting 
to the Hunters’ actions, and creating an emergent plot. 
Alternatively, each Scenario offers a Scene Tree structure, 
allowing the discovery of the Scenario’s plot through a 
series of linked Scenes, in a more classic, linear fashion. If 
you or other players are new to the game, you might want 
to try that first.
Of course, it’s also possible to play longer Campaigns 
through a series of linked Scenarios. Freely moving along 
the map, Hunters will interact with an interconnected 
world, deciding at each turning point what to do and which 
locations to explore.
In The Broken Ones expansion, sold separately, you’ll also 
find the rules for playing as ordinary humans and a variant 
for solo play.

10
Rules For Hunters
The rules for playing a Hunter are presented entirely on the 
following pages. These same rules can also be found on the 
Player’s Sheet. If you plan to be the Storyteller instead, read 
the dedicated section in this chapter and all the in-depth 
rules in the following chapters.
What’s on the Hunter’s Sheet:
 ◆ Under the Hunter’s name, a brief history explaining 
who they are and why they joined the Order.
 ◆ Descriptors indicate what the Hunter does best and 
what their flaws are. Incorporating a Descriptor into 
the narration of a Hunter’s actions allows a Position 
or Defense Check to start from 3 Base Successes. 
Getting in trouble during the narrative, thanks to the 
Descriptor’s Downside, allows you to check them off  
and gain XP .
 ◆ Gifts are powerful special abilities that only Hunters 
of the Order possess. They can be used whenever the 
opportunity arises, following the rules and limitations 
of the Gift itself.
 ◆ The Dark Ego represents their essence and bond 
to the fairytale world, what the Hunter was before 
their Breaking Point. Y ou can activate the Dark Ego 
whenever the Activator’s condition is triggered, or 
when the Hunter suffers or decides to inflict 1 Wound 
on themselves. The effect of the Dark Ego persists 
throughout the course of the Scene.
 ◆ The equipment indicates what gear the Hunter 
possesses and can use in order to perform their mission 
on behalf of the Order.
 ◆ The Soma value represents the Hunter’s inner strength 
and ability to tap into the true essence of a Broken Tale. 
Spending Soma increases the chances of a positive 
Outcome in any Position and Defense Check, or allows 
the activation of certain Gifts. 
When the Storyteller asks for a Position or 
Defense Check…
Check if one of the Hunter’s Descriptors can help them in 
the action being performed. If so, the Hunter starts with 3 
Base Successes. If no Descriptor is relevant, only 1 Base 
Success is obtained. It’s possible to obtain Additional 
Successes (for example, as a result of a Gift) or Penalties  
(to be subtracted from the total), to modify the number of 
Base Successes. Once this is done, the number of Successes 
is compared with the NPC’s or Threat’s Opposition Level 
declared by the Storyteller. There are 3 Opposition Levels: 
EASY 3, MEDIUM 5, and HARD 7. 
For an action to have a positive Outcome, you must obtain 
a total number of Successes (given by the sum of Base 
Successes and Additional Successes) that is at least equal to 
the Opposition Level you are facing. 
To Get the Additional Successes you need to...
 ◆ Spend Soma from the Hunter’s reserve to obtain 1 
Additional Success for each point.
 ◆ Decide to roll one or more 6-sided dice. Y ou gain 1 
Additional Success for each die rolled. However, if even 
a single die rolls a 1, the entire Check fails. All other 
results (2-6) count as Additional Successes.

11
How many dice can be rolled?
Rolling dice is a gamble. Y ou can roll as many dice as you 
wish to reach the Outcome you desire. However, each 
additional die rolled increases the chances of rolling a 1, 
even one of which results in a Failure. Even when aiming 
for the best Outcome (Outcome with an Increment) the 
total number of Successes needed will never exceed the 
Opposition Level + 2. 
Count total Successes...
If it’s a draw with the Opposition Level you’ll get an 
Outcome with a Cost. The Hunter gets what they want 
but at a cost chosen by the Storyteller. If it makes sense, 
the Hunter can also inflict 1 Wound. In a Defense Check 
the incoming problem is avoided, but with a complication 
decided by the Storyteller.
By scoring 1 point above the Opposition Level you’ll get 
a Standard Outcome. The Hunter gets what they want. 
In a Defense Check, the problem is handled without any 
complications. If it makes sense, the Hunter can also inflict 
1 Wound.
By scoring 2 points above the Opposition Level you’ll 
get an Outcome with an Increment. The Hunter succeeds 
better than expected and gains an Increment in addition to 
what they wanted. The Storyteller decides on the additional 
effect or, alternatively, the special Increment of one of the 
Hunter’s Gifts can be activated. In the case of a Defense 
Check, the problem is avoided perfectly and an Increment 
is also obtained. If it makes sense, the Hunter can also 
inflict 1 Wound.
A Wound is inflicted…
As an effect of a failed Defense Check or as a Cost for an 
action. Each Wound must be described and annotated on 
the Bookmark. The Wound narratively affects what the 
Hunter can do and the Opposition Level of what they face. 
Whenever you suffer 1 Wound, you can activate your Dark 
Ego power for the duration of the Scene. A Player may 
decide to voluntarily inflict a Wound on the Hunter to 
instantly activate their Dark Ego.
When they suffer a third Wound, the Hunter is out of the 
game or dead. The Storyteller will reveal their fate according 
to the situation.
Once upon a time…
If during the Scenario the Hunter gets into trouble and 
highlights the Downside of one of their Descriptors, the 
corresponding box must be marked. During an Interlude, 
each box marked is converted into 1 XP .
Declaring an Interlude...
If Hunters need to catch their breath, a Player can ask 
the Storyteller for an Interlude  between Scene changes. 
During the Interlude, it is possible to recover 1 Wound 
or, alternatively, recover 2 Soma. Each Hunter can ask a 
question of another Hunter to learn more about their past.

12
During an Interlude XP may be spent to obtain additional 
effects:
 ◆ Recover 2 Soma for each XP spent.
 ◆ Recover 1 Wound for each XP spent.
 ◆ Evolve a Descriptor into a Specialization. For the rest of 
the Scenario, this will grant 1 Additional Success in each 
Scene in which it is used for the first time (1 XP).
 ◆ Obtain a Scenario Gift for the duration of the Scenario 
(1 XP).
Each Interlude, however, represents a use of time. Remember 
that the Scenario, and the NPCs, may continue to act even 
when the Hunters aren’t paying attention.
Rules for the 
Storyteller
Broken Tales is designed to be a modular, easy-to-play role-
playing game. For Storytellers who already have experience 
with role-playing games, all the basic rules are presented 
in this section. In the section Further Details on Game 
Rules (page 17) you will find all the rules explained with 
examples, to clear up doubts about the system and on how to 
handle a game of Broken Tales.
Choose how to play the Scenario
Unlike other RPGs, Broken Tales doesn’t have Scenarios 
with a pre-written plot, but instead cultivates an “emergent” 
narrative. What happens in the Scene will always be the 
outcome of the Hunters’ actions and this, combined with 
the Storyteller’s choices regarding the NPCs, will generate 
situations with unpredictable outcomes. 
An emergent narrative means approaching the game with the 
intention of being surprised by it, reacting on the fly while 
being guided by the rules of the game and the Scenario’s 
information.
As mentioned earlier, a more classical approach is also 
possible. Each Scenario comes with a Scene Tree that will 
allow you to explore one of the possible plots in a more linear 
fashion. A Scene Tree may include forks and variations, but it 
will always give the Storyteller clear directions on what clues 
to bring into play and how to move the plot forward. 
There is no right or wrong way to play Broken Tales. Each 
group can decide how to approach it based on their own 
taste and desire to improvise or, rather, live out a more 
guided story.

13
The Storyteller’s Job
The Storyteller’s job is to manage the game, the Scenario, 
and all the NPCs that the Hunters will encounter. Each 
Broken Tales Scenario provides all the information needed 
to move a plot forward while adapting continuously to 
the Hunters’ decisions and the Storyteller’s personal taste 
in puppeteering the many side NPCs. After the Starting 
Situation, this outline should be followed to run the Session:
1. The Storyteller asks the Hunters where they want to 
go and what they want to do.
2. The NPCs’ Agendas and Scenario Events should be 
consulted frequently. If something has changed and it 
makes sense in the narrative, the Storyteller will have 
“off-stage” NPCs acting or entering the Scene.
3. Each new Scene must be contextualized by the Story-
teller with a Place, a Time, an Initial Situation, and 
any further Information.
4. After a brief introduction of the Scene, it’s the Hunt-
ers’ turn to decide what to do.
5. During the Scene, if the outcome of an action is un-
certain or collides with an NPC/Threat, the Storytell-
er should ask for Position and Defense Checks. Once 
the Check is resolved, it’s again the Hunters’ turn to 
describe what they do.
6. When the Hunters’ stated goal in the Scene is 
achieved or can no longer be achieved, the Scene 
starts over from step 1.
NPCs’ Agendas
To manage the game flow, each Scenario presents a series of 
Agendas, goals that the Scenario’s NPCs try to accomplish. 
An Agenda can progress in two ways: when it makes sense 
for it to happen in the narrative or during an Interlude 
(because time continues to pass while the Hunters rest). The 
Agenda is the most important reference for the Storyteller 
when deciding how to handle the events of the Scenario. It 
describes what that specific NPC wants regardless of the 
Hunters’ actions. Consulting NPC Agendas can lead to 
spontaneous situations without having to plan Scenes in 
advance.
Scenario’s Events
Events are brief descriptions of something that will happen 
if certain conditions are met. They’re sudden twists in the 
Scenario, shaking things up for the Hunters. When the 
conditions described in the Event occur, the Storyteller 
should include it in the Scenario as soon as possible.
Scenes and Interludes
Each Scenario is developed through Scenes, which are 
roughly comparable to scenes in a movie. It is usually clear 
when one ends and a new one begins. Each Scene must have 
a Place, a Time, an Initial Situation , and possibly some 
Information the Storyteller can use as a starting point. It’s 
the Storyteller’s job to manage Scenes and decide when to 
move on to the next one.    
Interludes are special Scenes that Hunters can ask for 
between the action to catch their breath. Interludes are calm, 
restful (such as dinner at the inn), and always take time. In 
addition to recovering Soma and Wounds, as indicated on 
the Player’s Sheet, an Interlude has two additional effects 
on the Scenario:
 ◆ Hunters can ask another Hunter a question about their 
past. This is a good way to reveal something and dig 
deeper into each background story. Players are free to 
invent whatever they want to characterize their Hunter 
but their answer should remain relevant to the question 
they have been asked.
 ◆ Time doesn’t stop during an Interlude, and the 
Storyteller must consider how to advance the Scenario’s 
Agendas. They must point out to the Hunters that the

14
Scenario goes on without them. Asking for too many 
Interludes can lead to things getting out of control!
Handling the Position Checks
When a Hunter performs any kind of action, the Storyteller 
needs to decide if they want to ask for a Check. In making 
this decision, it’s vital to keep all relevant Descriptors in 
mind.
 ◆ If the Hunter acts to obtain something but might fail 
due to an NPC or a Threat
Then, it’s time for a Position Check. This applies to any 
action in which the Hunter is the active party. Ideally, the 
Hunter would narrate a detailed, dynamic action such as, 
“I run up the wall and leap off, cutting with my sword at 
the giant’s head.” Not every action requires a Check, just 
those that face opposition that could prevent the Hunter 
from succeeding.
 ◆ If the Storyteller’s narration includes circumstances 
that may injure or inconvenience the Hunter
Call a Defense Check instead. This applies to any reaction 
in which the Hunter is a passive party. For example: 
“You’re jumping from roof to roof, but the rain made the 
tiles slippery, do a Defense Check against OL Medium 5. ” All 
Defense Checks represent a Hunter’s reactions, attempts to 
avoid a potential problem. A Defense Check is used when 
the Storyteller is the one taking the floor, presenting a 
Threat or an NPC as an obstacle the Hunter must overcome.
The Storyteller never rolls dice. Instead, they ask for Position 
Checks when a Hunter acts. The Storyteller also asks for a 
Defense Check when Scenario NPCs and Threats attempt 
to oppose the Hunters. Not every Hunter will find the same 
situation challenging, so the Storyteller may only ask some 
Hunters to perform a specific Check, remembering to keep 
in mind the Descriptors of each Hunter, NPC, or Threat 
involved. 
If the Scene evolves into a conflict, you must keep tabs on 
who has acted and who hasn’t, until all participants in the 
Session have had a chance to do something. This rotation 
system is called Exchange.
Depending on the outcome of the Position and Defense 
Checks, a Cost or Increment may be generated. The 
Storyteller will decide its nature.
 ◆ A Cost is some kind of narrative disadvantage, such 
as employing more time to perform the action or 
consuming a useful resource to do it. Possible Costs are: 
You retrace your steps or move away from a potential goal 
you intended to achieve, or you hesitate, avoid danger, 
but can’t get past what you’re trying to overcome / You 
get wounded or waste a lot of energy, suffering 1 Wound 
/ You lose, break, or drop something, or waste more 
resources than expected / Your opponent can easily move 
away from you and you can’t do anything to prevent it / 
You’re forced to make promises to successfully conclude 
the negotiation / You reveal something important about 
yourself or an ally / You lose much more time than 
expected / You let your guard down or are caught off  
guard, and receive 1 Penalty on your next Check / What 
you obtain is not whole or is missing parts.

15
Alternatively, or if no ideas spring to mind, you may assign 
1 Penalty to the Hunter’s next action, or inflict 1 Wound.
 ◆ An Increment is some kind of narrative advantage, 
such as spending less time performing the action or 
getting into a better position during an attack. Possible 
Increments are: You can maneuver around your target, 
stay engaged with it, and move in a direction of your 
choosing, or you can easily move away from a Threat 
without impediments / You can catch something on the 
fly / If an enemy is moving, you can force them to stop 
by blocking their path / You can provide 1 Additional 
Success to your next action or that of your ally, to 
be spent in the immediate future / You get the best of 
the negotiation, impress or fascinate someone, or are 
otherwise memorable / You can learn a secret, details of 
a scandal, or a technique from a subject or situation / 
You succeed better than expected, it takes you less time, 
or you get more beneficial results / You succeed and you 
can also help someone who is facing the same problem as 
you, benefitting their narrative position.
As an alternative, if no ideas spring to mind, you may 
assign 1 Additional Success to your next action or that of 
an allied Hunter.
The Opposition Level of Opponents 
and Threats
Any element of the Scenario can be an NPC or a Threat (a 
natural element, a magical effect, or anything other than an 
NPC). Both are always characterized by a Descriptor and 
an Opposition Level and, in some cases, by one or more 
Gifts. NPCs are also divided by Category, which indicates 
their importance in the game.
 ◆ The Descriptor: is a short narrative quote describing 
the NPC or Threat. It is used to handle the narrative 
and to modify the Opposition Level by +1 if the Hunter 
is taken by surprise or has no means to oppose what 
the Descriptor says. If the Hunter uses it to their 
advantage, it will lower the Opposition Level by 1.
 ◆ The Opposition Level (OL): is the numerical value 
to be achieved or surpassed in a Position or Defense 
Check.
EASY 3 / MEDIUM 5 / HARD 7 
 ◆ Gifts: are special effects and powers. Each Scenario will 
feature those specific to relevant NPCs and Threats.
 ◆ The Category: indicates the importance of an NPC in 
the narration and a sensible range of Wounds they can 
suffer.
Minor NPC: from 1 to 3 Wounds (generally 1).
Main NPC: from 1 to 6 Wounds (generally 3).
Dark Presence: from 1 to 6 Wounds (generally 3).

16
Managing Information
Broken Tales Scenarios will often be investigations 
that Hunters perform on behalf of the Order. With 
this in mind, it does not make sense to ask for 
Position Checks to find clues that, if undiscovered, 
would stall the situation. In general, when a Hunter 
is looking for information, ask yourself:
 ◆ Does one of the Hunter’s Descriptors justify 
the knowledge of the information? If so, the 
Hunter will get it.
 ◆ Does it take time to get the information? 
The Storyteller communicates this drawback 
to the Hunter, who will decide what to do.
 ◆ Is getting the information dangerous for the 
Hunter? The Storyteller introduces an NPC/
Threat into the Scene.
 ◆ Does the Hunter need external help to get 
the information? The Storyteller will reveal 
who can provide it. This can often lead to a 
new Scene.
This is all you need to get started with the game. 
In the Further Details on Game Rules chapter on 
page 17, you will find several examples for each 
mechanic presented in these pages and the rules 
for creating NPCs and Threats.

17
Further Details on 
Game Rules
In this section, we’ll explore further the rules we’ve already briefly discussed in 
the sections devoted to Hunters and Storytellers. If you’re not familiar with role-
playing games, it can be a good idea to read everything at least once. Y ou will better 
be able to understand the application of rules, after seeing some practical examples 
explained. For those who already have experience with other games based on Monad 
Echo, this section is a useful reference when in doubt.
 
The Conversation
A game of Broken Tales is structured as a conversation between all 
Players, in which the Storyteller presents fictional situations and 
the other Players react with their Hunter’s choices. When the 
consequences of a Hunter’s choice are uncertain, the Storyteller 
asks for a Position Check to determine the outcome. At this point, 
the Hunter’s action either succeeds or fails, and the conversation 
resumes. The Storyteller narrates the action’s result, together with 
the Hunter’s Player. Play continues until the next Check and so on 
throughout the Session.
The Storyteller never performs Checks on the 
behalf of NPCs and Threats.

18
Check Type When is it done? What are the Check 
criteria?
Who 
narrates the 
outcome?
What are the 
mechanical 
consequences?
Position 
Check
The Hunter is actively trying 
to position themselves in a 
certain way within the story, 
and the outcome is uncertain.
The Storyteller requests the 
Check, based on the Hunter’s 
narrative and what they’re 
trying to achieve. 
The Storytel-
ler describes 
the outcome.
The action continues 
with one of three possible 
positive Outcomes or a 
Failure. The Hunter expe-
riences the consequences 
of the action.
Defense 
Check
The Hunter is the target of 
an action by someone or 
something (NPC or Threat) 
attempting to obstruct them.
The Storyteller requests the 
Check based on the actions 
or triggers of NPCs or 
Threats. 
The Hunter 
narrates the 
outcome.
The Hunter suffers any 
effects and play continues. 
The Hunter cannot achie-
ve anything that is not 
related to avoiding danger.
Making Decisions While Narrating
The game flow is simple and fast. It is interrupted only for 
Position and Defense Checks. With this in mind, it is very 
important to have a clear idea of who can determine what, 
and the duties of the Hunters and the Storyteller in telling 
the story.
How and when does a Hunter act? How much can they 
risk in terms of resources and consequences in the 
narrative?
This is the domain of the Players. The Storyteller cannot 
impose actions or make decisions for a Hunter.
How dangerous is an action and when is a Position and 
Defense Check required?
Making this decision is up to the Storyteller. A Hunter may 
try to steer the narrative towards one of their Descriptors 
to improve their chances of obtaining 3 Base Successes. 
However, it will always be the Storyteller who decides 
whether or not to call for a Check. Only the Storyteller 
knows the Descriptors and other details of NPCs and 
Threats, and they’re the only one at the table who always 
knows everything that is happening.
When a setting/environmental element acts directly 
against one or more Hunters, the Storyteller may request 
a special Position Check called a Defense Check. As 
with a normal Position Check, the Hunter will always 
be confronted with the Opposition Level declared 
by the Storyteller; this time, however, the Player will, 
with the help of the Storyteller, narrate the outcome.  
Once the Check is resolved, the narration resumes as usual.

19
Which Scenario developments affect the way Hunters 
behave? What will trigger XP accumulation thanks to a 
Descriptor’s Downside? 
Players have authority in these situations. Each of them 
has Descriptors on their Sheet and can decide when to 
create a problem for themselves, putting the Downside in 
the spotlight. Doing so will allow them to check off the 
Descriptor, which will later translate into XP .
The Basic Mechanics
The main mechanics of Broken Tales revolve around 
Position and Defense Checks. But what are they exactly?
The Position Check represents an action taken by the 
Hunter, along with the purpose they are trying to achieve, 
in order to metaphorically gain a beneficial “position” 
within the storytelling. Anything a Hunter declares they 
will do that does not have an obvious outcome may require 
a Position Check. Defense Checks are a special type of 
Position Checks. If the Storyteller declares that a Hunter 
faces a challenge, it will be up to the Player to describe 
what they will do to avoid it. Their success or failure in a 
Defense Check determines their new place, or position, in 
the narrative.
Position Check
The Storyteller asks for a Position Check when a Player’s 
narration causes their Hunter to interact with an NPC, 
another Hunter, or a Threat, and the outcome of that 
interaction is uncertain.
When in doubt, follow the storytelling because the same 
action can lead to different Checks and outcomes based on 
the context. 
Narrating in detail what the Hunter does is essential for the 
Storyteller who:
 ◆ Starts with the story when determining what kind of 
Check is needed (if any).
 ◆ Will evaluate if one of the Hunter’s Descriptors is 
relevant to the action.
 ◆ Will decide the possible consequences of a positive 
Outcome or how the narrative will proceed in the 
event of a Failure.
 ◆ Will determine whether to change an NPC’s Opposition 
Level or Threat by +1 or -1, answering the question: 
“Has the NPC/Threat Descriptor been taken advantage 
of, or countered?”
 ◆ Will determine if a Player can roll a Position Check to 
see if their action succeeded, or if a Defense Check is 
needed first.
The Hunter always has the right to review an action before 
deciding whether to roll the dice or invest Soma. The 
Storyteller has the duty to clearly explain the consequences 
of what is about to happen, without stating the unknown 
Descriptors of the opponents or adversities. What a Hunter 
knows and what they don’t know can vary in relation to 
their Descriptors. Some may have the skills to sense threats 
that others would normally underestimate.
In fact, the Hunter’s intentions will often collide with 
unexpected situations. When changing circumstances 
might affect a Hunter’s action, a Defense Check may be 
needed to see how they are affected. If successful, they may 
be able to then roll the Position Check for their action. 
Again, it is up to the Storyteller to make the situation clear 
and ask the Hunter what they intend to do in light of how 
events are unfolding.

20
Example: 
Garou the Old Wolf is exploring the area around the town 
of Durfort. Meanwhile, his companions are having a 
conversation with the noble Dubois, hoping to get a grasp 
of what is happening in the Red Hood Iskra Scenario. 
The Storyteller warns Garou of the presence of a rapidly 
approaching pack of wolves. No Check is required because 
it is a piece of information Garou knows (as explained on 
page 16) and in addition, Garou has the Gift Find Them 
Forever After, which allows Garou to perceive noises and 
smells before anyone else. Safely among the foliage, Garou 
prepares an ambush for the new arrivals, determined to make 
immediately clear that this is now his territory. The wolves 
arrive a few meters from the hiding place and Garou decides 
to attack, jumping on them. However, the Storyteller points 
out that wolves have a very acute sense of smell (as reported 
by their Descriptor) and therefore the surprise effect could 
fade before Garou manages to bite one. At this point, the 
Storyteller requires a Defense Check to see if the Hunter will 
really be able to perform the surprise attack. Garou agrees to 
take the risk and does the Check. If not, Garou would have 
remained in hiding waiting for a better opportunity.
Once the situation is clear and defined, we move on to the 
resolution sequence.
The Storyteller:
 ◆ Identifies whether to determine the positive 
Outcome or Failure of an action during the game.
 ◆ Declares the basic Opposition Level of the Threat/
NPC. 
 ◆ Determines whether to raise or lower the Opposition 
Level by 1 , according to the current narrative and 
Hunter’s behavior (knowing or not) in relation to 
what is happening. A Hunter often does not know the 
Descriptors of a Threat (but can discover them using 
special Gifts, Descriptors taken advantage of, or as the 
result of previous actions).
For a Check to have a positive Outcome, a Hunter has 
to accrue a total of Successes equal to or greater than the 
Opposition Level declared by the Storyteller.
The Hunter:
 ◆ Checks if one of their Descriptors is relevant to the 
declared action and confirms it with the Storyteller. 
If so, they start with 3 Base Successes, otherwise with 
only 1.  
 ◆ Adds up any Additional Successes and subtracts any 
Penalties. Each Penalty removes 1 Success.
 ◆ Decides what kind of Outcome to aim for, depending 
on the compromises they are willing to make and the 
expected results of the action. Matching the Opposition 
Level with Successes leads to an Outcome with a 
Cost, exceeding it by 1 is a Standard Outcome, and 
exceeding it by 2 is an Outcome with an Increment 
(see following pages). It is never possible to aim for a 
higher result than an Outcome with an Increment.
 ◆ May decide to spend Soma from their supply to get 1 
Additional Success per point spent. 
 ◆ Can roll as many dice as they want, all at once, getting 
1 Additional Success for each result between 2 and 6.

21
How to read the dice
If 1 is rolled: Failure.
Any result between 2-6: Success.
 A single 1 (Failure) causes the entire Check 
to fail. 
Automatic positive Outcome
It may happen that the sum of Basic Successes + Additional 
Successes reaches or exceeds the Opposition Level 
determined by the Storyteller. In this case, the action 
is already successful. Which Outcome is reached
 will be determined as explained in the third point of The Hunter 
paragraph.  
Check example: 
Continuing with the example on page 20, Garou is facing 
a pack of four wolves. Through a Defense Check, Garou 
has approached the enemies from behind, taking them by 
surprise.
Garou’s Descriptors tell us that he is a fierce and powerful 
predator, and that is definitely relevant to what he is doing. 
Therefore, Garou receives 3 Base Successes.
The Storyteller declares that the Opposition Level of the wolves 
is Medium, which is equal to 5. However, the Storyteller 
decides to lower it to 4 because the wolves’ Descriptor specifies 
that they obey only their Alpha Greskar or Iskra, and neither 
of them is present in the Scene.
Garou has 3 Base Successes and needs at least 1 Additional 
Success to reach the Opposition Level. This is the minimum to 
get an Outcome with a Cost.
Now the Player can decide if they want 2 Additional Successes 
to reach a Standard Outcome (1 Success over the Opposition 
Level) or 3 Additional Successes to aim for an Outcome with 
an Increment (2 Successes over the Opposition Level). Garou 
doesn’t mind a Standard Outcome: he now has to choose how 
to get the 2 missing Additional Successes to achieve it.
As the last step, Garou decides to exchange 1 Soma for a 
Success, and then roll 1 single die. The die comes up a 3: 
Success. Garou gets a Standard Outcome, which is up to the 
Storyteller to describe.
Let’s see all the possible results of Garou’s Check:
Rolling even a single 1 on the dice makes the action fail.
In this case, the action fails: if the Hunter’s goal is to 
somehow avoid a Wound, they will suffer it. The narration, 
however, goes on anyway. Whether the Hunter wants it or 
not, something changes, and, in the case of a Failure, it does 
so in a way that affects them negatively.
The Storyteller describes the result: Garou hurries to avoid 
alerting the wolves, but this makes the attack hasty and 
inaccurate. The bite misses its mark as the other wolf dodges 
the attack and turns around, ready to face the threat.

22
DEFENSE CHECK
By obtaining a total of Successes equal to the Opposition 
Level: the action would result in an Outcome with a Cost.
The Hunter gets what they want but at a Cost chosen by the 
Storyteller from the list (page 14), in line with what is 
happening. As a result of the Outcome, if the Hunter’s goal 
is to harm a target in some way, 1 Wound can be inflicted.
The Storyteller describes the result: Garou sinks his fangs into 
the throat of one of the wolves, which, being able to suffer 
only 1 Wound, is instantly killed. Unfortunately, as the 
animal falls to the ground, it drags Garou with it, throwing 
the Hunter off balance. The Storyteller assigns 1 Penalty to 
Garou’s next Check.
By obtaining a total of 1 Success above the Opposition 
Level: the action would result in a Standard Outcome.
In this case, the Hunter gets what they intended and, if 
consistent, can inflict 1 Wound on one or more targets.
The Storyteller describes the result: Garou sinks his fangs into 
the throat of the enemy, which, being able to suffer a single 
Wound, is killed instantly.
This is exactly what happened in the example on page 20. 
By obtaining a total of Successes 2 points over the 
Opposition Level: the outcome is an Outcome with an 
Increment.
The Hunter gets what they have set out to do and an 
Increment, an additional effect increasing the range of the 
action itself. The Increment is provided by the Storyteller 
in relation to the situation and the objectives of the Hunter, 
choosing from the generic list (page 15). Alternatively, 
if the Hunter’s Gifts grant special Increments, they may 
choose to activate them.
The Storyteller describes the result: Garou sinks his fangs into 
the throat of the enemy, which, being able to suffer a single 
Wound, is killed instantly. Garou sprints past the opponent’s 
body and pushes the remaining wolves along a narrow path 
to prevent being surrounded, as stated by the Player at the 
start of the attack. The Storyteller will therefore neither 
be able to exploit the narrative advantage of Garou being 
outnumbered for the wolves’ next action, nor their Gift which 
confers advantages to them when they outnumber a prey.

23
Defense Check
A Defense Check is basically a Position Check which 
represents an obstacle to the Hunter’s intentions. When a 
Threat or NPC acts on a Hunter, the Storyteller asks the 
Hunter for a Check to see how well they can handle the 
problem. Often a Defense Check is self-evident, such as 
when an enemy responds by attacking a Hunter during a 
fight. Other times it will be unexpected, for example when 
a Hunter declares an action and the Storyteller brings into 
play elements that have been underestimated or hidden.
A Hunter’s action may require a Defense Check before a 
Position Check can be attempted. In that case, the Storyteller 
decides whether to allow them time to review the action, 
examining the Descriptors involved and establishing 
whether the Hunter has the opportunity to become aware 
of the threat in time.
After Garou’s attack, the wolves cannot use their numbers 
to overwhelm the enemy. Garou recounts how he used the 
narrow path to force the wolves to face the Hunter one at 
a time. The Storyteller declares that the Opposition Level 
drops from 5 to 4 as the opponents fail to use their Descriptor 
Efficient as a pack. It will always be Garou to make the 
Defense Check, and once again will be able to count on 3 
Base Successes thanks to his Descriptors, deciding what kind 
of Outcome to aim for. In the same situation, if Garou had 
declared to dodge the blow rolling away from the enemy, he 
would have ended up in a small clearing behind his back. In 
this way, unknowingly, Garou would have put himself in the 
perfect position for the enemy to bring into play their Gift, 
which increases their Level of Opposition by 1 when they are 
numerically superior.
The same action and the same situation can have very 
different outcomes based on the Hunter’s narrative: 
evaluating whether a threat is perceived or not makes 
the difference between presenting the Check directly or 
giving the Hunter the opportunity to review their action. 
Has Garou seen the enemy pack fight before? Has the 
Hunter experienced their fighting style and knows how 
they exploit their numerical superiority? Or does Garou 
have a Descriptor that indicates the ability to understand 
the enemy’s strategy? In these cases, the Storyteller should 
allow Garou to review his action.
Other Rules on Position and 
Defense Checks
Let’s discuss some additional elements to consider when 
calling Position and Defense Checks.
A Player describes how the Hunter does 
something
During the game, narration is continuous. Every Hunter 
must be aware that, if there are no obstacles or a Position 
Check is successful, they will get exactly what they have 
set out to do. One’s intent must be clear while narrating, 
describing what they do concretely and not what they could 
possibly do. In combat, “I try to hit the enemy” is the wrong 
way to state intent; “I hit the head of the pirate” is much 
more precise and useful for fictional positioning.
The big difference between the two statements is that 
the first one is too general and doesn’t tell us anything, 
making any Outcome bland and leaving all the burden of 
the consequent narrative to the Storyteller. The Player’s 
intentions could be misinterpreted and the action would 
likely lead to a less entertaining narration. The second 
DEFENSE CHECK

24
statement, however, makes the facts clear: for the Storyteller, 
it will be easy to narrate the result of “hitting a pirate on the 
head” and also to pick any Cost, Increment, and what type 
of Wound to inflict in case of success. 
Everything starts from the narration, 
everything goes back to the narration
The most important question to keep in mind during the 
game is: what is happening in the Scene? Broken Tales starts 
with the narrative premise that each Player describes what 
their Hunter does, while the Storyteller responds through 
NPCs and situations. There are no subsystems: when an 
action can have an interesting or uncertain outcome, you 
make a Check. Take into account what was narrated before 
asking for the Check. What is happening in the Scene will 
lead to a whole series of considerations:
 ◆ What prompted the Check? Are we narrating a violent 
action towards someone? Or a stealth maneuver to 
sneak away unseen? Is the action free and unimpeded 
or does it put the Hunter at risk of having to make 
a Defense Check first? What is happening in the 
narration is essential to understanding what will be 
achieved with the Check. Always remember that if an 
action is successful and it makes sense in the context, 
the target could suffer 1 Wound. 
 ◆ How do you manage the Check? Does the Hunter 
bring into play a relevant Descriptor? Does the NPC 
or Threat involved bring a Descriptor into play as well? 
What Gifts are used? It is always through the narrative 
that we will understand “how” the action is done.
 ◆ What’s the result? How does the Hunter’s narrative 
position change after the Check? The result is the 
most important part because it will always cause some 
change to the ongoing Scene.
Dodging a rifle shot by throwing yourself to the ground 
is probably the simplest solution, but even in the case of 
a positive Outcome, it will result in you ending up on the 
ground. 
A nearby enemy could take advantage of this and force your 
Hunter to do a Defense Check while you’re attempting to 
get up. Blocking the bullet with an improvised shield will be 
very dangerous and obtaining an Outcome with a Cost could 
mean suffering a Wound, but will leave your Hunter standing 
and ready to react.
When many subjects are involved in 
a Scene, it is good to establish turns 
dynamically
When Hunters are alone or in situations where they 
have full control and time to plan their moves, they can 
freely decide how and when to act. On the other hand, if 
there’s a conflict situation involving NPCs, the Storyteller 
must always divide characters into two factions. After 
determining the first to act, each faction will then make 
each of its members act in turn until all of the Hunters and 
prominent NPCs have acted at least once.
This system is called Exchange and it establishes a 
“narrative order. ” It is used by the Storyteller to keep the 
spotlight equally divided between the parties but does not 
have to follow all the contenders in the same way.
What matters during an Exchange is that Hunters and 
prominent NPCs have time to act and turn the tide in 
their favor, while everything else remains active, but in the 
background.

25
After a Position or Defense Check, the 
narration always continues
After a Check, the situation must  evolve in some 
way, for better or worse. This is a fundamental step. 
A Position Check always moves the situation forward 
far enough, even if by just a few moments, to ensure 
that the previous status is changed. This applies both 
to any type of positive Outcome, as well as to a Failure. 
Failing can mean many things. Sometimes, you can 
get what you want and still fail. The Player narrates 
how. A Position Check to push someone off a cliff can 
be resolved in many ways, one of them being that the 
target is pushed but takes the Hunter down with them!
Hunter vs Hunter
Broken Tales assumes that a conflict between two 
Hunters should be resolved mainly between Players, 
with a spoken mediation between intent and outcome. 
However, it could happen that one Hunter acts to harm 
another, generating either a Position or Defense Check. 
In this case, the Storyteller determines who is acting 
(the Active Hunter), and who is at the receiving end of 
the action (the Passive Hunter). The Active Hunter will 
then have to make a Check against an Opposition Level 
of either Easy 3 (if the Passive Hunter has no relevant 
Descriptors) or Medium 5 (if the Passive Hunter has a 
relevant Descriptor). The Passive Hunter cannot spend 
Soma to increase their Opposition Level.
Dividing storytelling duties
Broken Tales puts all the choices in the Storyteller’s 
hands, who then decides Costs, Increments, and 
if Wounds are inflicted. This is intended to avoid 
interruptions in the game flow and avoid delays due to 
indecision, which can be a frequent problem in some 
other games. Once you are familiar with the rules, feel 
free to decide how to divide the duties of narrating Outcomes, 
Costs, Gifts, choices and Descriptor uses between Storyteller 
and Hunters to match your playstyle.
Descriptors
They represent the salient narrative features of a Hunter, an 
NPC, or any other element of the setting. Descriptors help 
Players in interpreting the Hunter and the Storyteller in 
establishing areas of competence during the game. Another 
effect of the Descriptors is to grant bonus XP if they caused a 
Hunter trouble during a Scene.
Each Hunter Descriptor is made up of two parts. One is positive 
and describes the Hunter’s abilities and strengths. The second, the 
Downside, highlights a flaw or weakness.
Baba Yaga the Child Witch possesses the Descriptor I am very 
skilled in reading people. That is why the Order has chosen 
Strength in numbers?
If a group of four Hunters faces a pack of twelve wolves, 
the Storyteller will not have the enemies act twelve times. 
Instead, the wolf pack has the same number of actions as the 
Hunters, four in this example. The purpose of the Exchange 
isn’t to put pressure on the Hunters with multiple Defense 
Checks, but to create a tense situation. An overwhelming 
number of enemies is already a decisive narrative advantage. 
They can surround the Hunters (forcing them to succeed in 
a Defense Check to move), take secondary actions, and so 
on. Some Gifts increase the Opposition Level of opponents 
based on their number, which can also be a good way to 
emphasize their superiority.

26
me as leader of the Hunters. I love manipulating people 
and I often do it merely to amuse myself. This Descriptor 
tells us a lot about our Hunter. Yaga is good at reading others 
and plays a major role among the Hunters. At the same time, 
Yaga cannot contain her manipulative nature and will tend 
to abuse it even without any real need, creating unnecessarily 
complicated schemes to reach her goal.
In this case, there is a noticeable difference between a 
Hunter, NPCs, and Threats. The protagonist of the game 
is defined by three complete Descriptors (positive part + 
Downside), while NPCs and Threats have only one, not 
necessarily with a Downside.
Additional considerations:
 ◆ For everything that is not a Hunter, a Descriptor is 
nothing more than a narrative sentence the Storyteller 
can use to manage that particular story element. The 
Descriptor of an NPC or a Threat may have a Downside 
or not. The important thing is that the Descriptor is 
narrow enough to clearly signal how and when it is 
relevant in a Check, and what tactics can be applied 
against it or in its favor.
 ◆ Some Gifts create a temporary Descriptor. In that case, 
the Gift description and its importance in the narrative 
will indicate the Descriptor’s specifics.
 ◆ Descriptors created by Wounds will be mainly of 
a negative nature. This doesn’t mean that a Hunter 
cannot exploit them to their own advantage, but that 
they will be more of a problem than a resource.
Gifts
Gifts are special skills and abilities that give Hunters and 
NPCs depth. Every Gift has a full description of its effect, 
which clarifies when and why it can be used in the game. As 
with Descriptors, Hunters and NPCs use Gifts in different 
ways.
On the Hunters’ side, Gifts influence different game 
situations, from obtaining circumstantial bonuses to 
recovering Soma.
Generally speaking, NPCs and Threats have Gifts that have 
simpler effects than the Hunters’ . On pages 29, 32 and  
73 you’ll find all the expanded rules to create Gifts for 
Threats, NPCs and Hunters.
Gifts are by far the most design-heavy elements to create for 
Storyteller and Players, as they must be balanced following 
an often-subjective evaluation.
The golden rule when creating a Gift is to start by describing 
what happens in the narrative when the Gift is used.
This means setting aside bonuses and game effects so 
that you can prioritize a narrative description over the 
mechanics. It is good to be exhaustive, describing how the 
Gift is activated, if it requires special precautions, and what 
happens when the owner shows it off. On page 73 you 
will find a series of Archetypal Effects and Costs for the 
Hunters’ Gifts which, when combined, will allow you to 
build a full-fledged Gift.
GIFTS

27
Ask yourself:
 ◆ If you had to describe in simple words what a Gift  
does and when it can be used, how would you 
describe it?
 ◆ Which of the mechanics available on pages 75 or 
81 best suit what you described?
Always ask yourself the questions in this order!
Having a clear understanding of what happens in the 
narrative is key to deciding which Effects and Costs to 
apply. In Broken Tales, Hunters and their opponents are 
often exceptional people. There’s nothing wrong with 
giving them Gifts that grant incredible abilities. The only 
real way to create a balanced Gift is to remain consistent 
with the backstory of the Hunter/NPC.
needs to be increased or lowered.
It is important to clarify that Wounds represent the NPC’s 
“plot armor” that keeps them active during the Scenario, not 
just their physical stamina.
 ◆ Any NPC who represents a minion or doesn’t have 
special abilities should have 1 single Wound. Even when 
facing many of these opponents, Hunters can defeat them 
with a single hit.
 ◆ Minor NPCs should have 3 Wounds, so they can 
withstand some of the Hunters’ attacks and have their say 
before being knocked out.
 ◆ Anything between 3 to 6 Wounds is indicated for three 
categories of NPCs. First, are the Main NPCs of the 
Scenario, who play a prominent role in the narrative. 
Dark Presences and particularly dangerous enemies, 
who even by themselves can be a match for a group of 
Hunters, can have a larger Wound pool. A single Exchange 
is probably not enough to defeat an opponent with 5 or 
6 Wounds, thus giving the Storyteller the opportunity to 
present an extended challenge.
James the Swordsman is engaged in a deadly duel with the 
Masked Swashbuckler (a Dark Presence inspired by Puss in 
Boots). After a failed Defense Check, James is slashed in his 
right leg by his opponent, causing a Wound. The Storyteller 
gives it the Descriptor: Slash in the leg . As this is the first 
Wound suffered, James is still in the game, but from now on 
declaring maneuvers that require quick movements or running 
away could require a Check.
When a target receives its last Wound, its fate is decided by the 
Player who inflicted the Wound, in accordance with what has 
been narrated up to that point. This means that suffering the 
last Wound does not always mean dying or being incapacitated 
permanently. Wounds can also be psychological trauma such 
as fear or loss of confidence. They can also be debilitating 
statuses such as fatigue, confusion, and exhaustion. The result 
Wounds
All creatures have a pool of Wounds, an indicator of their 
ability to withstand injury and trauma. In terms of play, a 
subject’s pool determines how many Wounds they can take 
before being knocked out or, worse, killed. The number of 
Wounds an NPC can suffer is established according to their 
type.
Every Wound suffered must be outlined with a Descriptor, 
which represents its effects on the narration. This will 
become an additional element for the Storyteller to evaluate 
when, during Checks, the Opposition Level of something 
GIFTS

28
of a Position or Defense Check may have the additional 
effect of causing a Wound.
The Astonishing Pied Piper Without A Name enters an 
underground catacomb, where an evil spirit rages and 
whispers mad words in the ears of mortals. Determined to 
smother the ravings of the spirit with his music, the Pied 
Piper brings his flute to his lips and breathes a sweet melody. 
The Storyteller then asks for a Position Check to determine if 
the Piper’s melody will really soothe the restless soul. Playing 
the flute, the Pied Piper hears the ghost’s whispers and is lost 
in a nightmare of dying, devoured by a mob of hungry rats. 
This vision shakes the Hunter, who will suffer the Wound 
Terrified by mice.
Recover or exhaust Wounds
Although it may not seem like it at first glance, Wounds are 
a full-fledged resource, useful both for absorbing damage 
and for activating the effects of various Gifts. A Wound is 
easy to suffer, but it’s just as easy for a Hunter to recover.
 ◆ At the discretion of the Storyteller, at the end of each 
Scene where there is an obvious moment of rest (such 
as a night of sleep), each Hunter will recover 1 Wound.
 ◆ During an Interlude, each Hunter can choose to recover 
1 Wound for free (instead of recovering Soma), and by 
spending XP , heal additional Wounds.
 ◆ For the NPCs, on the other hand, the Storyteller simply 
evaluates whether the subject has had the opportunity 
to recover from the damage suffered and, if so, restores 
all their Wounds. 
Equipment and 
possessions
In Broken Tales, everything a Hunter needs and can be 
justified by their Descriptors will be in their possession. 
As a general rule, owning something simply expands the 
Hunter’s storytelling possibilities, allowing to declare 
actions that wouldn’t otherwise be possible.
The Astonishing Pied Piper Without A Name always has the 
trusty trained mouse named Hamelin nearby. The Pied Piper 
can employ his little friend for various tasks, or even complex 
ones, such as exploiting a crack in the wall to retrieve the key 
of the Hunters’ jail cell.
Weapons and Armors
Weapons and armor have no damage modifiers or 
protection values; this does not make them less effective. 
The true value of a good weapon is represented by the 
narrative opportunities that it will give to its owner. In 
game terms, this means that the Storyteller will evaluate 
the usefulness of the chosen equipment every time they call 
for a Check. Whenever possible, it is important to detail 
an NPC’s weaponry in its Descriptor. A good alternative is 
to focus on the fighting technique, allowing the Player to 
decide what equipment is appropriate.
Consider the following:
 ◆ A pack of hungry wolves will be at its strongest when 
exploiting their numerical superiority, while it will be 
less effective in the absence of the alpha wolf.
 ◆ A skilled swordsman will be a relentless foe in single 
combat but be at a disadvantage if thrown into the 
chaos of a large-scale confrontation.

29
 ◆ The Beast that longs for the flesh of James the 
Swordsman will be able to crush a peg-legged pirate 
but will be easy to circle and backstab.
These considerations will lead the NPC’s Opposition 
Level to go up or down by 1, making it more or less lethal 
depending on the situation and giving a relative meaning 
to weapons and armor. In addition to the modifier, always 
keep in mind what leads to a Position and Defense Check 
during the narrative. Charging an enemy with a loaded 
pistol will result in a Defense Check for the Hunter before 
they can attempt a Position Check to conclude the attack.
Wealth and Possessions
In the Broken Europe of the eighteenth century, trade is 
highly developed, but most Hunters show no interest in 
worldly riches. The Broken Tales system does not assign 
a precise value to every piece of equipment, and for this 
reason, there is no real need to keep track of treasures 
or anything else, if not for the weight they can have on a 
narrative level. When a Hunter is preparing to obtain or 
seek some material goods, the Storyteller must consider 
two elements: 
Are there any Hunter Descriptors that justify that 
specific possession or that give the Hunter the possibility 
to obtain it?
For Garou, the Old Wolf, getting horses and supplies for a 
long journey could be tricky. All his Descriptors lead back to 
a wild wolf who, even when in a human form, possesses no 
wealth. Regina the Thief of Hearts, on the other hand, will 
only need to make her requests known to some “friends” in 
the city and receive what she wants in a short time.
Is the object of the search easy to find or is it a rare and 
precious commodity? 
In the first case, they get it. In the second case, the Storyteller 
will reveal information on how to obtain it in the story. The 
Hunter will then have to actively work to get their hands 
on it. 
Threats
The term Threats refers to features of the game world such 
as special places, natural threats, and all those situations in 
which the Hunters can be in danger but are not directly 
facing an NPC. A drawbridge of a haunted castle or the 
illusion of a mighty witch are both excellent examples of 
a Threat. 
To create a Threat, the Storyteller must establish three 
points:
 ◆ Create a Descriptor that indicates the characteristics of 
the Threat. 
 ◆ Establish a Threat Opposition Level. 
 ◆ Optional: Give the Threat a Gift to represent a special 
ability.
Threats are a way to formalize potential Position or Defense 
Checks and a good way for the Storyteller to keep track of 
what may happen during the Session. Often, a Threat will 
be created on the fly as a direct result of a Hunter action and 
will not require additional work for the Storyteller.

30
NPCS
Sun Wukong the Amazing Monkey is chasing a mysterious 
killer that the Hunters have caught red-handed at the crime 
scene. The mysterious, black-cloaked figure leaps swiftly 
across the rooftops of Prague, almost as if it were flying. Sun 
Wukong runs after them by jumping from roof to roof. It is 
night and it is raining, so a Defense Check will be needed to 
keep Wukong from losing track of the target.
It is good practice to establish a Descriptor for each Threat, 
in order to have a narrative reference that describes the 
possible result of a Hunter’s current course of action.
Threat Gifts are simpler than those of the Hunters, as they 
serve to represent a particular effect that activates when the 
Hunter interacts with the Threat. The best way to create a 
Threat Gift is to describe what happens in the story when 
the Hunter suffers the effects of the Threat. Unlike the 
Hunters’ Gifts, which are a balancing act, a Threat’s Gift  
allows you to have fun creating effects to make the Scenario 
more interesting.
In the wolves’ lair, it will not be easy for the Hunters to move 
around without being spotted. The Gift Defense Lair  (page 
124) might be the most suitable because it forces intruders 
to succeed at a Defense Check every Scene to avoid detection. 
On the other hand, if the wolves in our story have pups to 
defend, the Gift Duty Bound  (page 33) will be more 
relevant to what we imagine could happen in the narrative 
when the Hunters enter the lair. By describing what we 
imagine and want to happen in the story, it becomes very 
easy to detail the full effects of the Gift.
Below you will find a list of generic Gifts, to use as a basis 
for creating Threats on the fly.
 ◆ The Threat Gifts always must clearly state in the text 
what activates them.
 ◆ Unless there are valid narrative justifications, there is 
no reason why a Threat Gift should not affect NPCs 
as well.
Generic Gifts
This list of Gifts represents generic features adaptable to 
every type of Threat.
Adverse: The Threat consumes the power of those who 
approach. A Hunter loses 1 Soma at the start of each Scene, 
while an NPC lowers their Opposition Level by 1 on the 
first action taken. 
Clock: The Threat accumulates Tokens of a certain type (see 
page 76), in relation to the actions of the Hunters or the 
passing of time. Once you have reached a predetermined 
number of Tokens, activate a Special Event listed in the 
Gift’s description. This is a good way to give importance to 
the passage of time, or the accumulation of resources and 
information during exploration. 
Create Effect: When the Threat activates, it creates a 
Descriptor representing how the situation is affected and 
changed by the Threat.
Damage: When the Threat is triggered, it inflicts 1 Wound 
of a sort linked to the type of Threat. This Wound is in 
addition to any Wound inflicted on the Hunter because of 
a Failed Check.

31
Disturbance: The proximity of the Threat weakens a 
certain ability, such as magic in a holy place. Anyone near 
the Threat receives 1 Penalty (-1 to the Opposition Level for 
NPCs) when using abilities that are affected by the Threat’s 
power. 
Fulcrum: The proximity of the Threat enhances a certain 
ability, such as magic in a place charged with arcane energy. 
Anyone near the Threat receives 1 Additional Success (+1 
to the Opposition Level for NPCs) when using abilities that 
are affected by the Threat’s power.
NPCs categories
In this part, we will explain how to create NPCs. Broken 
Tales allows you to come up with NPCs very quickly, 
defining only a few necessary features. The following are 
the recommended guidelines. The Storyteller can always 
decide to change one or more variables to best characterize 
whoever they have in mind. For example, nothing prevents 
you from assigning a Major NPC an Opposition Level of 
Easy 3 to represent a person who is socially influential, but 
totally unable to handle other situations, despite the rule 
of thumb stating that a Major NPC will have a Medium 5 
Opposition Level.
NPCs
The first step is to decide what kind of NPC should be 
created.
Main NPCs
These are the real antagonists of the Scenario or, more 
generally, characters with an Agenda who will have a 
strong impact on events. Main NPCs must have the skill 
and influence to move the story in their favor or to be an 
interesting challenge for the Hunters.
Minor NPCs
These are the people surrounding the Main Scenario NPCs. 
A Minor NPC is almost never going to be a big challenge 
for a Hunter and should be considered more of a link 
between Scenario situations and Major NPCs. Given their 
varying importance, a Minor NPC may not even have an 
Agenda. In this way, their actions will be easier to manage 
and connect to the Main NPCs and Hunters.
Dark Presences
Dark Presences are NPCs who, like the Hunters, come from 
the world of fairytales and have been “broken” by the wish 
of the Child Savior. Dark Presences feature an extra sub-
item, called Type, providing a general reason why the Dark 
Presence is in the Scenario or in the world of Broken Tales. 
Dark Presences enjoy a slightly more complex creation 
system, illustrated on page 41.
