NPCs Gifts
The Gifts of NPCs are simpler than those of Hunters, as 
they grant bonus effects in very specific areas and almost 
never have an activation cost. The best way to create an 
NPC Gift is to describe what happens in the story when 
the NPC uses their Gift. Unlike the Hunters’ Gifts, which 
need a more careful balancing act, with NPC Gifts you can 
indulge in creating the most imaginative effects to make 
each NPC unique and interesting.
Example: an NPC linked to the Vampire myth could have 
the Immortal Gift, which makes it impossible to kill except 
by some specific means described in the Gift itself. How 
immortality manifests is determined by what we imagine 
the character to do in the narrative. Will they fall under the 
blows they have suffered, turning into a cloud of smoke or 
will they simply ignore any Wound that is not inflicted by 
something they’re vulnerable to? Or will their body regenerate 
instantly in front of the incredulous eyes of those present? By 
describing what we imagine and want to see in the narrative, 
it becomes easy to detail the full effects of a Gift.

33
Below, you will find a list of generic Gifts to use as a basis 
for creating NPCs on the fly. Y ou can also freely draw from 
the Hunters’ Gifts and adapt effects to fit the NPC if they are 
fairly powerful. Often, however, a Gift meant for a Hunter 
will require some modification to be suitable for an NPC.
 ◆ NPCs have no Soma, so Gifts that require a Soma cost 
to activate must change so that their use is limited to 
one or more times per Scene.
 ◆ NPCs do not score any Additional Successes or 
Penalties. These effects must be converted into an 
equivalent modifier (+1/-1) that can be applied to the 
NPC’s Opposition Level.
 ◆ Many NPCs have few Wounds and paying a Wound to 
activate a Gift can be a difficult cost to bear. In these 
cases, it is best to make the activation of the Gift free 
the first time in each Scene the NPC uses it.
Generic Gifts
This list of Gifts represents generic features adaptable to all 
types of NPCs and creatures. 
Ally / Minion: The NPC has a minion, animal, or some 
kind of servant ready to lay down their lives for them. The 
Ally / Minion is another NPC who will do their best to help 
the owner of this Gift and, in case of danger, can shield their 
patron and take 1 Wound in their place.
Armored / Thick-Skinned: The NPC can take 1 addi-
tional Wound or ignore a certain type of Wound such as 
being immune to fire.
Destined / Duty Bound:  The NPC will not be easily 
swayed. Once per Scene, the NPC can ignore or interrupt 
the effect of a Gift that is used against them.
Determined / Devoted: Religious belief or iron determi-
nation means the NPC always stays calm. Any attempt to 
persuade or manipulate the NPC through words or a power 
related to a Broken Tale receives 2 Penalties.
Expert / Skilled:  Whoever possesses this Gift increases 
their Opposition Level by 1 when they do something they 
are masterful at. This specialization must be indicated in 
the Gift description.
Foul Play / Poisoner:  Wounds this NPC inflicts are 
made more serious by a dangerous poison. Wounds 
inflicted in this way cannot be recovered with an Interlude 
as usual, but only through the use of a specific antidote / 
medication or at the end of a Scenario.
Gang / Herd: Whoever possesses this Gift increases their 
Opposition Level by 1 when acting against a target if they 
have the support of one or more companions.

34
Intuitive / Persuasive:  The NPC is good at reading 
whoever they face. During a social interaction, the NPC 
can understand one of the Descriptors of the person they 
are talking with. A Hunter can make a Defense Check to 
avoid revealing anything about themselves.
Sworn Enemy / Weapon Master: The NPC is a dan-
gerous opponent in combat and, once per Scene, can inflict 
1 additional Wound when an attack hits.
Versatile / Resourceful:  The NPC has a second De-
scriptor representing a very useful personal resource, such 
as great wealth or a special combat technique. The first time 
this is shown off in a Scene, the NPC can impose 1 Penalty 
on any Defense Check.
Agenda
Main NPCs always have an Agenda, which establishes 
their way of acting and helps the Storyteller manage 
their behavior during the game. Each time an Agenda is 
exhausted, either in success or failure, the Storyteller must 
evaluate whether to write a new one. Agendas are very 
important, because they give purpose and depth to NPCs 
in a quick and functional way, suggesting their responses to 
the Hunters’ actions during the Scenario.
On page 93, you will find tables and guidelines for the 
creation of NPCs Agendas.

35
The Storyteller’s Role
This chapter will examine Storyteller’s role, and 
provide useful tips for managing Sessions and Scenes. The 
role of the Storyteller differs from that of other Players 
in terms of duties and narrative authority over the game 
world. Where the Hunters are the protagonists of Broken 
Tales’ stories, the Storyteller is responsible for presenting a 
world that is coherent and managing everything that is not 
the direct prerogative of a Hunter. The Storyteller makes 
decisions for NPCs, determines when Scenes open and 
close, and generally looks at the game with a different eye. 
The role of the Storyteller is equivalent to that of a movie 
director, who, however, does not have an immutable script. 
Instead, they shape what could happen if the Hunters 
do not intervene, and improvise with a certain degree of 
freedom. In this way, each Scenario will be unique and 
tailored to each group.
How the Storyteller acts during a 
Session
The game is a conversation where the Storyteller presents 
various situations and the Players deal with them by 
narrating how their Hunters act. For a Player, storytelling 
duties are all related to their Hunter and to some information 
about their past. For the Storyteller, however, the spectrum 
of agency is much broader. 
But how, then, does the Storyteller act during a Session?
Giving life to Broken Europe
Broken Tales’ setting is a version of eighteenth-century 
Europe where rural landscapes are still predominant. The 
Hunters often travel to small villages and abandoned places, 
where the Broken Tales can find fertile ground in which 
to act away from the gaze of ordinary people. The Broken 
Tales’ existence is an anomaly. At the same time, they need 
contact with people and reality to pursue their goals. This 
duality will inevitably create conflicts and problems. It is up 
to the Storyteller to bring out these elements, which form 
the basis of the events of Broken Tales’ stories, in play. The 
key principles of the setting are:
 ◆ The world of Broken Tales, Broken Europe, is 
a massive fairytale that encompasses all other 
fairytales within it. This concept will be explained 
better when we discuss the Campaign (page 243). For 
now, it is good to keep in mind that Broken Europe 
is a fictional world that lays its foundations in the 
real historical context of the era, and then corrupts 
everything with out-of-the-box characters, magic, and 
a strong horror vibe.

36
 ◆ The stories the Hunters live out will almost always 
take place in a rural setting. The section on Broken 
Europe on page 103 will provide useful information 
to manage and present a plausible eighteenth century. 
In this context, the Storyteller’s prerogative must be 
to break the fairytales, place them in this plausible 
context, and give each character a purpose and a reason 
to use the powers that distinguish them.
By choosing or creating a Scenario
The Hunters are part of the Order and intervene when 
there is a cry for help against the darkness. The Storyteller 
decides which Scenario to present from those found in this 
book or creates one from scratch. In the case of a Campaign, 
the Storyteller can manage everything as explained on page 
243.
By shaping the initial situation of each Scene
This is a great power: the Storyteller decides how a Scene 
begins and who will be there. They describe places and 
situations, and only when the Scene is clearly understood 
by everyone will they leave the stage to the Hunters. After 
the first Scene, it will be easier to set up the following 
ones, because in most cases it will be the Hunters who will 
specify a new objective. The Storyteller will simply have 
to decide what happens between one Scene and the next, 
and how the new situation begins. Between two Scenes, 
the Storyteller must keep track of what is happening and 
possibly “move” the NPCs, if they have already appeared 
in a previous Scene, according to their Agenda. There is 
no predefined style regarding how to set up a Scene. The 
Storyteller introduces everything that seems relevant to the 
situation and goals of the Hunters, asking questions of the 
Players to understand how the story should unfold.
By managing information
The Storyteller knows many secrets and will often see all 
the mysteries of the Scenario clearly from the beginning. 
Their task is to disclose information according to the 
questions and actions of the Hunters. Since the Storyteller’s 
goal is for the Hunters to advance from Scene to Scene 
towards the conclusion of the Scenario, they should never 
deny information, as explained on page 16. This does not 
mean helping the Hunters or offering NPCs with ready-
made solutions. Hunters must always be at the center of the 
Scene, while NPCs must act in response to their actions, 
with information acting as the glue between the two.
Considering when to call a Check
The last important responsibility of the Storyteller is 
to manage the transition from narration to resolution 
through Position and Defense Checks. As explained in the 
rules, the Storyteller must always be certain of a Hunter’s 
intent when evaluating whether to request a Position 
Check. Broken Tales allows you to act on the granularity 
of events, “zooming in” on a particularly important scene 
and splitting it into multiple “close-up shots” , or keep a 
“wide shot” to solve a macro-situation in one go. The most 
striking example of this granularity can be found during 
fight Scenes: all the parties involved will act to harm the 
opponents on multiple actions and, most likely, failure will 
have painful consequences. In a conflict, the Hunters will 
describe action after action and generate many Checks. 
Conversely, an action to infiltrate a location protected by 
guards could be resolved with a single Position Check 
made by the Hunter leading the raid. 
As a general rule, before requesting a Check, the 
Storyteller must ask themselves the following question: 
Does Succeeding or Failing the Check have an interesting 
narrative outcome? 
If the answer is yes, then it’s okay to call a Check.

37
By fostering a safe and inclusive gaming 
environment
Role-playing is an activity that can easily expose delicate 
issues, offending the sensibility of some Players. Broken 
Tales is no exception and the themes of the Scenarios can 
be difficult to deal with for some people. Each Scenario 
includes a series of Keywords useful for getting a quick 
understanding of the situations that could arise during the 
game. In this way, the Storyteller will be able to evaluate 
what to offer to the table and whether or not to list the 
Keywords to the Players. It must be noted that Keywords 
might reveal characters and situations, spoiling some 
aspects of the plot.
Another good way to handle difficult issues is to modify 
the Scenario based on Keywords. If the story includes 
the Keywords Sect and Manipulation, the Storyteller 
can modify those elements in the plot, transforming the 
members of the sect into something else. For example, a 
gang at the service of the villain. 
The best advice, however, remains to speak and listen to 
each Player and be accommodating to their sensitivity. 
Often, simply hinting at what happened in a given situation 
and not dwelling on the details is a good way to run the 
Scenario as it’s written without impacting the fun factor at 
the table.
Finally, even if it may seem trivial, it is good to remember 
that Broken Tales is a dark horror game with a focus on 
the distortion of traditional fairytales, switching the roles 
of hero and villain. Approaching the game with appropriate 
expectations is essential to avoiding nasty surprises. After 
all, you wouldn’t go to the movie theater to see a scary 
movie if you hated the horror genre, right?
Tips for proper game 
management
The following is a series of good procedures that can be 
useful to the Storyteller and clear up any doubts about 
various game situations.
Establish the initial description of the Scene
Take into consideration the place, the time of day, and other 
characters present. The Hunters see the world through the 
Storyteller, who filters what has yet to be discovered from 
what has already been revealed. For this reason, you can 
provide whatever information may be useful when the 
Scene opens. After the initial description, ask the Hunters 
questions to dispel doubts and better specify all the required 
details.
Manage NPCs Agenda
Agendas are essential for keeping track of the opponent’s 
possible actions in the Scenario. Checking the Agenda of 
everyone involved in the Scene, and what those encountered 
in previous Scenes might do, is a good way to clarify NPC 
motivations and have them act consistently.
Ask questions and build on the answers
Questions are a great resource. Through a question, the 
Storyteller can better understand the intention of a Hunter 
or detail a Scene and a place. Whenever they deem it useful, 
it is good for the Storyteller to ask questions and build on 
the answers given by the Hunters.

38
Give every Hunter the spotlight
The protagonists of the stories are the Hunters. Everything 
starts with them and from them come the decisions that 
will bring the Scenario to its conclusion. From a Hunter’s 
sheet, you can glean many elements useful in building an 
interesting Session. Gifts also reveal a lot about a Hunter’s 
abilities. Presenting situations where the Gifts can stand out 
is a good way to give a Player a chance to take action.
Interpret the rules and manage Position 
Checks
The Storyteller has the last word on the interpretation of a 
rule. This does not mean handing judgments from on high 
as a superior and unfailing authority. The dialogue between 
Players is essential, but it makes sense to save discussions of 
rules for after the Session when it won’t stop the game. The 
most “critical” mechanic to manage is understanding when 
to call for a Check. This is because, as already mentioned, 
the same action can be divided into more or fewer Position 
and Defense Checks to better highlight the drama. This 
decision is up to the Storyteller, as Players will instinctively 
try to perform as few Checks as possible. After all, making 
a Check means risking Failure or losing precious Soma 
points. A good way to handle the breakdown of a situation 
is to ask often: What are you doing now? This simple 
question stops your description of the action and shifts the 
responsibility to the Players: the narrative has entered a 
new phase.
Manage the results of the Hunters’ actions
The result of the Hunters’ actions is a vital element of the 
game. The Storyteller describes the results of a Position 
Check, but with the goal of staying true to what the Hunter 
expects to achieve. It is simple and easy to work from the 
intent of the Players. Isn’t that what motivated a certain 
action? From this point of view, the Increments must 
confirm the Hunter’s success and the Costs must temper 
the success of an action that has, in any case, achieved the 
desired result. 
The real reason the Storyteller manages Costs and 
Increments is to make the game run smoother. Otherwise, 
the narrative would stop with each Check to allow the 
Hunter to choose an effect from a list. There is no good 
reason not to give a Hunter what they want if they already 
have a clear idea of how their success can become even 
cooler.
Stay anchored to the story as it’s played 
and not to whatever you have prepared in 
advance
Even if you have an understanding of Scenario development 
and detailed descriptions of NPCs and situations, the game 
will suffer if you focus on what you expected of the Hunters 
and not what they actually do. Scenarios are designed with 
an open structure and the Agenda of the NPCs is intended 
to make it easy to quickly come up with a consistent 
reaction based on the Hunters’ moves. By default, the plot 
is not decided by the Scenario but created directly at the 
table. If a game group prefers a more classic structure, each 
Scenario provides a Scene Tree to follow that will help you 
set up a game more focused on story discovery and less tied 
to improvisation.

39
If necessary, pause the game and ask other 
Players for assistance
The Storyteller does not have to fear stalemate situations. 
Sooner or later, it will happen that the Hunters make a 
decision so unusual as to undermine all expectations about 
what could happen. In these moments, pause the game and 
make it clear that you need a moment to rearrange your 
ideas. The Storyteller is not an entertainer and doesn’t owe 
the Players any more than the opportunity to have fun. 
While the Storyteller thinks, Players can take a break or 
organize themselves to play a Memory Scene. For example, 
two Hunters can play through a flashback in which they 
confront each other in an argument, or in which NPCs are 
present, perhaps interpreted by the other Players. Players 
will kill time and have the chance to bring the Downside 
into play and accumulate some precious XP , while the 
Storyteller prepares for a return in style.
Do not call Position Checks ahead of time
While Position and Defense Checks look similar, they are 
actually very different. A Position Check transports the 
Hunter into a new narrative phase where the Hunter’s Player 
gains the authority to decide whether to do something  and 
why, taking a calculated amount of risk. Defense Checks, 
on the other hand, are born as a side effect of the Hunter’s 
action, and therefore are the ace up the Storyteller’s sleeve 
to respond to actions and place the Hunters in danger.
Don’t make the Hunters look incompetent
Make the Hunters’ failures interesting moments, rather 
than humorous sketches where a Hunter fails miserably. 
Even the Descriptors’ Downsides are 
excellent narrative hooks that can 
put the Hunters on the spot. Use 
them to create an exciting game 
where Hunters are the heroes.

40
Don’t pressure yourself
Whenever possible, boil things down to two factions, 
review Agendas between one Scene and the next to evaluate 
how to move the NPCs, and take enough time to set the 
stage for upcoming events. The Storyteller shouldn’t be in a 
hurry to throw everything in the face of the Hunters right 
away. It’s best to focus on a single Scene and a few NPCs to 
better keep track of the situation. The more elements in the 
spotlight, the more the Hunters will have distractions and 
things to reflect upon, significantly slowing down the pace 
of the game.
Make fights dynamic
Even if the rules feature the Exchange system, the narrative 
of Broken Tales must always remain in the foreground and 
account for the Hunters’ tactical choices. 
A fight is always part of the story and will be more 
interesting if the actions of different participants in the 
Scene are handled with more flexibility, compared to the 
classic procedure of attacking someone only when it’s your 
turn. It is vital to remember that every NPC has an Agenda 
that clearly determines what they want to achieve. Rarely 
will that goal be fighting to the death against the Hunters. 
Suddenly running away, overturning a wardrobe to block 
someone, or setting fire to some part of the setting to make 
it more dangerous, are all legitimate actions to be processed 
first in the narrative, and only then by the rules. 
Similarly, a fight does not end when all of the enemy’s 
Wounds are depleted, but when the narrative makes it 
clear that one of the two sides has won. The simplest rule 
for handling a fight is to ask “what would this NPC do with 
their action now, to fulfill their Agenda?”, and then narrate, 
consequently, what happens in the fiction. The application 
of the rules with Check, Gifts, and other mechanics, will 
simply be a result of the choices made in the narrative.
Join the dots
A Scenario features a set of NPCs, Places, and possible 
situations to create a connected macro-plot, but one 
that will inevitably be tested by the actions and powers 
of the Hunters. This is a good thing. They are the heroes 
of the story and they are the ones who possess Gifts and 
abilities that can make a difference. The Storyteller’s job 
is to rearrange the Scenario after every Scene, having fun 
working out the NPCs’ moves in relation to those of the 
Hunters. Pursuing the Agenda of the NPCs, the Storyteller 
must play like the others to see how it ends, not pigeonhole 
the story in a pre-written path. Each Scenario in this book 
can have an infinite number of very different endings – it 
makes no sense to limit this creative potential. Perhaps 
the most important task of the Storyteller is to use their 
resources jointly with the Hunters, without ever worrying 
that the story will take an unexpected turn.

41
The creation process for Dark Presences follows the 
same pattern as that for Hunters. It requires you to push a 
fairytale character to their Breaking Point, then give it new 
life in the world of Broken Tales. Dark Presences are for all 
intent and purposes NPCs with a background connected 
to the original fairytale, but aren’t specific to a Scenario. 
Within the world of Broken Europe, a Dark Presence can 
take on a life of its own. This makes them versatile figures, 
which the Storyteller can drop in any Scenario or Campaign 
to assist or oppose the Hunters.
Every Dark Presence must be born 
from a fairytale
The starting point is deciding which fairytale character to 
“break” in order to create their new version in the world of 
Broken Tales. 
 ◆ Any source for the fairytale will do, whether it’s a book 
or just a website.
 ◆ Based on the original story, you have to decide what 
kind of Dark Presence you want to create.
An Opponent is a Dark Presence meant to be an enemy. 
Their Gifts will mainly be skills that put the Hunters 
in trouble. NPCs with a mercenary or hired assassin 
background would make excellent Opponents, as would 
anyone unburdened by scruples.
An Ally is a Dark Presence aiding the Hunters. Their Gifts 
will primarily serve a support function. Hunters will be 
able to invoke or activate them in time of need. Allies are 
often Order-bound NPCs – that’s why they are ready to 
serve the Hunters.
Finally, a Neutral Dark Presence is a type of NPC with a 
personal goal unrelated to the Order or what the Hunters 
do. These NPCs can be both Allies or Opponents of the 
Hunters, depending on their Agenda.
By following the following steps, it will be easy 
to decide the inclination of a Dark Presence.
Dark Presences

42
Step 1 Bring the character 
to their Breaking Point 
Underline and take notes on all the interesting words or 
phrases in the original fairytale. These will become the 
Keywords. Keywords are narrative flags useful in detailing 
all aspects of a Dark Presence. For some words or phrases, 
the meaning of a Keyword should be flipped. This step is 
necessary to reach the Breaking Point and transform the 
classic character into its distorted version. 
It’s now time to detail a story and give a background to the 
Dark Presence. For historical notes and context, use the 
Europe of 1780 as a reference. However, there is no need to 
limit yourself to historical truth. 
Step 2 Assign the 
characteristics 
We start by defining the Dark Presence’s Descriptor. A 
Descriptor consists of a couple of sentences that must 
summarize what the Dark Presence does and what 
capabilities they have. As with a Hunter’s Descriptor, 
the Dark Presence Descriptor can have a downside. The 
Descriptor must make it clear who the Dark Presence is in 
a few sentences.
We then establish how many Wounds the Dark Presence 
can suffer, starting from 1 to a maximum of 6. Keep in 
mind that the number of Wounds is not an indication of 
the NPC’s power, only of their narrative “stamina” . An NPC 
can be easy to knock out (1 Wound), but very capable and 
powerful (Opposition Level 7 Hard). Generally speaking, 
the average Dark Presence has 3 Wounds. 
Finally, you have to decide on an Opposition Level (Easy 
3, Medium 5, Hard  7). This is a general indicator of the 
NPC’s strength. Medium 5 is the “standard” level for the 
Dark Presence, Easy 3 is for less capable NPCs, while Hard 
7 is only for the most powerful and dangerous subjects.
Step 3 Create the Gifts
Each Dark Presence must have at least two Gifts that 
represent abilities, powers, or something special they can 
do. Keywords will be of great help in staying consistent. 
The basic rule is to describe what  happens during the 
narration when the Dark Presence uses their Gift. Using 
the description can clarify what the mechanical effect of the 
Gift should be.
 ◆ Gifts for Opponents must be aimed at fighting or 
disrupting the Hunters. 
 ◆ Gifts for Allies must be formulated so that Hunters can 
activate them in order to obtain a supporting effect. 
It is also often useful to assign an Archetypal Cost (as 
explained on page 81) to balance the Gift’s potential 
benefit to the Hunters.

43
A Gift Example for an Ally Dark Presence: Sinbad
Gift - Traveler of a Thousand Places: Sinbad has seen the whole world and much 
of what the Broken Tales hide. In the course of a Scene, a Hunter can invoke his help 
to obtain information or create an additional Descriptor that represents a specific 
area of Sinbad’s knowledge. But the old sailor is an eccentric type, and to provide his 
help he first must be entertained. The Storyteller will evaluate the current situation 
and Sinbad’s level of boredom. A Hunter can capture Sinbad’s interest with creative 
actions, as long as they’re appropriate to the current Scene.
Step 4 Agenda
The last step is to establish an Agenda for the Dark Presence, which 
is a short sentence that determines a motivation for their actions. 
The Agenda must be contextualized with the character’s story 
or the Scenario in which the Dark Presence will be used. If 
the Dark Presence comes from a fairytale from which one 
of the Hunters was drawn, it’s possible to detail a Special 
Agenda representing some connection between the 
two.
Note: Some of the Special Agendas in the following 
pages refer to Hunters featured in The Broken Ones 
expansion book.

44
Ambrose the Sorcerer 
known as Old Merlin
Inspired by Merlin from the Arthurian Myth (Neutral)
Deep mysteries still surround wielders of magic and their secret, 
centuries-old struggle for control of the arcane world. Some legends tell 
of the two most outstanding practitioners of arcane arts: Baba Yaga, the 
witch forced into the body of a child, and Ambrose, constrained into 
the body of a tired old man. Under the false name of Merlin, Ambrose 
has been pulling the strings of humanity’s destiny for centuries. He 
pretends to be of service to those who need him most, while in truth his 
actions only strengthen his position. Many seek the old hermit Merlin for 
advice or help, unaware that the only support he dispenses is that which 
supports himself. With each new petitioner, the ancient sorcerer grows 
ever closer to absolute power.
Descriptor: I am an ancient sorcerer, my body is weak, but my magical 
power is unmatched.
1 Wound 
Opposition Level: Hard 7 
Agenda: Pretending to be a wise old man who is always ready to 
help others with the proper advice / Gaining personal advantage by 
manipulating others. 
Special Agenda (Baba Yaga):  I both respect and hate Yaga. 
Hers is the only power that can stand against mine. While the laws of 
magic prevent me from killing her directly, nothing prevents me from 
maneuvering her into danger. And if she perishes due to her own 
foolishness, all the better.

45
Special Agenda (Morgana of the Lake):  I fear the 
power of the greatest sorceress of all time. Thanks to an 
agreement with Baba Yaga, we have sealed the memory of 
young Morgana. I will do whatever it takes to keep her from 
awakening.
Note: Morgana of the Lake is one of the Hunters featured 
in The Broken Ones expansion book.
Special Agenda (Marina the Sea Explorer): I respect 
Marina as a skilled witch and I seek her aid against Baba 
Yaga. Obviously, once my great enemy is defeated, I will have 
to make sure that Marina does not take her place.
Gift – Ancient Sorcerer:  Merlin’s power is incredible, 
achieving practically anything he can think of through the 
use of magic. Those who rely on his advice will be able, once 
per Session, to activate the magic power that the wizard has 
infused within them. The magical effects must be decided 
at the time that Merlin blesses the character by defining an 
appropriate Descriptor such as: Taking flight like a bird  or 
Having skin as hard as a stone. Whoever carries Merlin’s 
magic can activate its power once, obtaining the Descriptor 
for the entire Scene. Once the Scene resolves the magic is 
drained from the player and cannot be activated again.
Gift – Wise Advice:  Merlin will never intervene 
personally but can appear in a dream or vision to his 
protégés. Once per Scenario, a Hunter can spend 1 Soma 
to summon Merlin’s help. The sorcerer will appear with 
helpful information or to bless the character with one of 
his spells.
Frog Face
Inspired by The Frog Prince (Neutral)
Once upon a time, the heir of a noble family was born 
deformed. His face was broad and covered with pustules, his 
eyes huge and yellow, and his skin a frog-like green. Destroyed 
by grief, the young man’s mother wished to keep him hidden 
from the world until he came of age. However, his father’s 
untimely death made him the new head of the family. Locked 
in a world of fairytales and lies by his mother, the young scion 
was convinced that he was the victim of a curse that only a 
kiss from someone truly in love could break. For years, Frog 
Face tried to find love and break the spell, receiving in return 
only rejection and contempt. On the verge of madness, Frog 
Face decided that if he couldn’t have true love, he would take 
someone else’s. Since that day, Frog Face travels accompanied 
by his trusty servant Heinrich. The two spy on people and 
when they find a couple in love, they kidnap one of the two 
lovers to snatch a kiss. This perpetuates a vicious circle of hope 
and disappointment that always culminates in the killing of 
those who, in the eyes of Frog Face, do not nurture a love pure 
enough to break the curse.
Descriptor: I am a wealthy, deformed noble, using my 
resources to achieve my purposes.
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Easy 3
Agenda: Find someone truly in love and force them to kiss 
me to regain a normal appearance.

46
Gift - By Any Means: Frog Face’s only desire is to break 
the “curse” that afflicts him. When he is trying to obtain 
something that obviously brings him closer to his goal, his 
Opposition Level becomes Hard 7.
Gift - True Love’s Kiss:  After years of experimental 
medicine, Frog Face’s saliva has acquired strange attributes. 
Whoever is kissed or licked by Frog Face will go into a daze 
for the rest of the Scene, lowering their Opposition Level 
by 2 (in the case of an NPC) or receiving 2 Penalties at 
each Check (in the case of a Hunter). A Hunter can make a 
Defense Check to resist the effects of True Love’s Kiss.
Heinrich the Trustworthy
Descriptor: I am the old family coachman, the only one 
who knows the truth about my master. Over time, the grief 
for his fate has driven me mad.
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Medium 5
Agenda: To help my lord realize his crazy dream, preserving 
him from a truth he would not accept.

47
Once upon a time, there was a young 
girl who lived in the Far East. When 
they drafted her old father for war, 
the young woman pretended to be a 
boy to take his place, as women were 
not allowed to be soldiers at the time. 
Under the false name of Hua Mulan, 
the young warrior won every battle 
and brought glory to her army, but 
when her true identity was discovered, 
envious generals took advantage of it to 
accuse her of deception and betrayal. 
Branded and exiled, the warrior fled 
her home, eventually reaching Europe 
after a long and wandering journey. 
Tired and embittered at finding the 
same sort of injustice wherever she 
went, Hua Mulan decided she would 
run no more. She made a disguise of her 
ancestral armor, hiding her face behind 
the features of a snarling demon. Under 
the name of the Sword Demon, the 
warrior now wanders around Europe, 
wielding her blade against everything 
that represents the status quo, such as 
the Order itself.
Descriptor: I am a skilled warrior 
and have won a thousand battles. I hate 
injustice and I know that the way of the 
sword is the only answer.
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Hard 7
Agenda: Punishing injustice and 
righting wrongs using the only tongue 
the vile can understand – the language 
of the blade / Deep in my heart I hope 
to find someone who can show me what 
true justice is.
Gift - Legendary Warrior:  The 
Sword Demon’s prowess is unmatched. 
During the course of a Scene, an 
enemy will not be able to use the same 
Gift twice against the Sword Demon. 
Having seen them in action once, 
the Demon will always find a way to 
anticipate the enemy and counter 
their strategy.
Gift - The Demon Blade:  Any 
edged weapon wielded by the Sword 
Demon becomes lethal. With her 
weapon, the Demon can cut through 
solid objects, stop arrows, and even 
dispel magic. Thanks to her iron will, 
the Sword Demon can defend herself 
against any type of attack, canceling 
the narrative advantage given by any 
associated Descriptors.
Hua Mulan the 
Sword Demon     
Inspired by the legend of Hua Mulan 
(Opponent)

48
The Periwinkle Peer
Inspired by Prince Charming (Opponent)
Once upon a time, there was a handsome nobleman born 
the heir to a wealthy family. The Young Lord was famous 
for his prized, periwinkle blue cloak, which would catch 
every eye whenever he entered a room. But despite everyone’s 
admiration and every young woman claiming him as a lover, 
the lordling was obsessed with only one thing: perfection. The 
Young Lord’s parents were the only ones who knew of their 
son’s bedeviling compulsion, which in the past had 
driven him to kill his brother, executed for the 
crime of being a better horseman. Blinded by love, 
the Young Lord’s parents pushed him into a 
military career. The Young Lord rose through 
the ranks quickly, driven as he ever was to 
excel. Then, he learned of sordid intrigues 
that could be used to garner preferment 
and promotion. His madness 
resurfaced, eventually driving him to 
murder once more. A wanted man 
and a deserter, today the Young 
Lord wanders endlessly, always 
looking for challenges to prove 
his superiority, putting himself 
at the service of anyone who 
praises his perfection.
Descriptor: I am the best 
at everything I do and I must 
win every challenge. I do not 
excel in science and academic 
knowledge, but no one must 
discover this weakness of mine. I prefer to show off my 
physical prowess and skill.
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Medium 5 
Agenda: I must prove my perfection, even if it means 
drowning doubters in blood.
Gift - Obsession for Perfection:  The Periwinkle Peer 
draws strength from the drive to win every challenge. When 
an enemy succeeds in a Defense Check against his action, 
the Peer gets +1 to his Opposition Level for the rest of the 
Scene. This bonus is cumulative. Once per Scene, when the 
Peer receives his final Wound, he will mock his enemy 
in a fit of rage and grit his teeth to keep from being 
knocked out, immediately recovering 1 Wound.
Gift - Divine Charm:  The Periwinkle Peer 
possesses good looks and charm out of 
the ordinary. With his mere presence, 
he is able to beguile anyone. During 
a Scene in which he is present, the 
Storyteller can create an Agenda 
for a Minor NPC that describes 
their infatuation with the Peer. 
This Agenda is more important 
than all of the NPC’s past ties, and 
also any pacts they might have 
struck with the Hunters. The Peer 
can also influence Major NPCs (at 
the Storyteller’s discretion) or a 
Hunter. In this case, the Player will 
create a Descriptor that represents

49
how The Periwinkle Peer impressed the Hunter. This 
new Descriptor lasts until the next Interlude and can be 
marked as usual to get XP .
The Ancient Enemy
Inspired by the Dragon (Neutral)
Once upon a time, there lived an ancient and mighty 
creature who had been a witness to the world’s unfolding 
since time immemorial. Frightened mortals called it Draco, 
fearing its proud bearing and immeasurable power. But 
despite his fearsome appearance, Draco wasn’t evil, just 
curious. In hopes of creating another of his kind, as he 
had watched countless mortals do, Draco laid an egg once 
every century. Each time, evil and unscrupulous men took 
possession of it before it could be hatched, leaving him 
alone. After a thousand years, despair stole Draco’s ability 
to lay more eggs. The bitter truth was, to lay an egg was to 
condemn it to an evil fate. Even today, it is said that Draco 
keeps waiting for a person with a pure soul, able to show 
kindness and compassion enough to warm his heart, thereby 
giving him hope for the future. Meanwhile, the lying legends 
of a great dragon, an ancient Enemy of mortals, have 
spread, reinforced by self-serving tales grown into myths 
and superstitions. More and more fools seek glory by setting 
out in search of the Ancient Enemy, invoking the grace of 
God’s protection, to slay the legend once and for all.
Descriptor: I am an ancient and powerful creature. 
My body was formed in the fires of creation and common 
weapons can do nothing against me.
6 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Hard 7 
Agenda: To live in solitude and avoid contact with 
humans, even if deep in my heart I still want to believe that 
there is a mortal worthy of my respect.
Special Agenda (St. George the Dragon Slayer):  I 
hate St. George because he is a symbol of those who judge 
others blindly, overawed by faith. I want to humiliate him 
and make those who believe in him see St. George as the 
lowest of the low.
Gift - Colossal Body:  Draco possesses a colossal body 
and is capable of flying at great speed. During a Scene 
or Exchange, he can act 3 times instead of once: with his 
powerful bite, with his front legs, and with his long tail. 
Draco’s strength is so great that each of his actions inflicts 
1 additional Wound. Finally, Draco can spit flames from 
his mouth once per Scene, making an area attack in front 
of him, engulfing everything in flames.
Gift – The Power of the Myth:  Draco is one of the 
most powerful beings of any myth and, in the course 
of a Scene, he can use the following powers at will: 1. 
Shapeshifter: Take on the appearance of an ordinary 
human or an animal.  2. Magicbreaker:  Immediately 
cancel the effect of a spell or arcane object that has been 
used in his presence (the user can attempt a Defense 
Check to avoid the effect of this power). 3. Gift of the  
Dragon: Shed a piece of his body, such as a scale or a tooth, 
which can be used to forge objects with extraordinary 
powers.

50
The Wild Swans Gang
Inspired by the Wild Swans (Opponent)
Once upon a time, there were eleven bold brothers, sons of an amiable 
nobleman. All were deeply attached to their younger sister Elisa and treated 
her like a queen. When the father remarried, the new wife, jealous of all the 
attention that the family reserved for Elisa, made a pact with Morgana, the 
famous witch, to cast a powerful curse. The brothers were turned into swans 
and Elisa was blamed for it. Accused of being a sorcerer and cast out by 
her father, Elisa wandered for months looking for a new home. Finally, she 
reached another region, where she was welcomed by a local noble who soon 
fell in love with her beauty. Elisa began living happily, regularly meeting her 
brothers, who in the meantime had been looking for a way to break the curse 
cast upon them. Elisa started to study magic to help them, but this attracted 
the attention of the prelate of the country who, fearing the worst, requested 
the Order’s intervention. Upon reaching Elisa’s presence, the Hunters noted 
her propensity for magic and took her away, turning her over to the papal 
inquisitors. When the eleven brothers found out, they cried bitter tears for 
their sister’s fate and swore revenge against the Order, waging war in the hope 
of finding Elisa alive.
Descriptor: We are a band of brothers and we specialize in fighting the 
Order’s emissaries. Thanks to the curse that has been inflicted on us, we can 
transform into swans.
1 Wound (for each of the 11 gang members)
Opposition Level: Medium 5

51
Agenda: Defeat the Hunters of the Order and find Elisa / 
Save Elisa from the clutches of the Order, even if it means 
destroying it.
Special Agenda (Morgana of the Lake):  We learned 
that everything that happened to us is the fault of Morgana 
the Witch, so we want to kidnap and force her to save Elisa 
with her magic.
Note: Morgana of the Lake is one of the Hunters featured 
in The Broken Ones expansion book.
Gift – Change into a Swan:  A member of the Wild 
Swans Gang can transform into a swan at will. The 
transformation is immediate. When it happens, it gives him 
the new Descriptor: I am a wild swan – I can fly well, but 
I maintain my human consciousness. The gang member’s 
clothing, items, and other possessions are never lost during 
the transformation.
Gift – The Swans Gang:  When a member of the gang 
acts and has at least one partner to support him, he 
increases his Opposition Level by 1. If at least 3 members 
focus on supporting the same action, whoever performs it 
increases the Opposition Level by 3 points.
The Silent
Inspired by The Little Mermaid (Opponent)
Once upon a time, there was a young Mermaid who was 
captured by a fisherman. Sensing her worth, the greedy 
fisherman gave the Mermaid as a tribute to the local 
nobleman. He displayed her as an exotic animal, fascinating 
everyone, but particularly the nobleman’s son. Over time, the 
young man began to look at the Mermaid with different eyes, 
and his curiosity turned into passion. The two consummated 
a forbidden desire, which led the young Mermaid to feel 
sincere love for her prince. The feeling was, unfortunately, not 
reciprocated. As soon as the opportunity arose, the nobleman’s 
son left the family home to get married. The Mermaid was 
sold to a circus, destined to end her days as a sideshow 
monster frightening fools with her misery. The meeting with 
a mysterious witch named Marina changed her destiny. In 
exchange for her voice, she obtained a shapeshifting potion. 
“Be careful, ” said the witch. “Do you really wish to give up 
what you are to pursue a fleeting love?” The Mermaid didn’t 
think twice about it and drank the potion to become fully 
human. She returned to her prince only to find that he barely 
remembered her and their time together. The Mermaid’s 
heart darkened. On a full moon night, she drank the blood of 
her lost love, and with it that of his entire new family.
Descriptor: I am an unscrupulous killer, I hate humanity 
and the body to which I am condemned. My voice is reduced 
to a faint whisper, which weakened my Siren powers but did 
not take them away.
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Medium 5

52
Agenda: I work for anyone who can afford to pay with 
magic and arcane items, which I intend to use to become a 
Mermaid again.
Special Agenda (Marina the Sea Explorer):  I hate 
Marina for what she did to me. Even though she warned me 
about what would happen, her power can ruin people’s lives. 
I want her to make me a Mermaid again before I kill her.
Gift - The Siren’s Whisper: The Silent has given away 
her voice, but she can still whisper. Any opponent acting 
against the Silent must pass a Defense Check before taking 
their action, or be dazed and miss the opportunity to act. 
A target cannot be affected by this Gift more than once per 
Scene.
Gift - The Siren’s Charm:  The Silent possesses an 
otherworldly beauty that can awaken the lustful desire 
of those around her. Once per Scene, if the Silent wants, 
every subject in her presence will receive the Descriptor: 
I desire the Silent with all my heart and would do anything 
to have her. A Hunter can make a Defense Check to resist. 
Particularly powerful NPCs, at the discretion of the 
Storyteller, may not succumb to the Siren’s Charm.

53
The Wise Fox
Inspired by The Golden Bird (Ally)
Since time immemorial some have set aside the wise words 
that advised hard work and responsibility, preferring fast 
and easy rewards. When the lessons of the wise are 
ignored, pain is the teacher of last resort, bringing 
with it the awareness that every mistake has a price. 
A benevolent and kind Spirit knew this well and 
for centuries had tried to teach mortals in order 
to spare them the pain of a harsher lesson, only 
to see them inevitably choose the less sensible 
path. Eventually, the Spirit decided to help those 
in need, but only if they could meet his price. 
In this way, he thought, his help would be 
more appreciated by humans, who value 
something more the more it costs them.
Descriptor: I am an immortal spirit 
incarnated in the body of a talking fox 
and I have always watched over human 
affairs.
6 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Hard 7
Agenda: To help the humans I deem 
deserving while asking a fair price in 
exchange.
Gift - A Good Advice:  Once per 
Scene, the Wise Fox can appear to 
someone who has done a good deed, to 
bring advice, an object, or information that will be decisive 
for the current Scene. Before dispensing his help, however, 
the Fox will ask for a price to be paid. Select between: 1. 
Spilled Blood: Whoever benefits from the advice must 
self-inflict 1 Wound by shedding blood as a symbol of 
commitment. 2. Good Action: Whoever wants to receive 
the Fox’s help will first have to perform the requested 
action. This action, although apparently absurd, can 
be contextualized by the Storyteller in a future 
Scenario faced by the Hunters. The effort 
required must include difficult or dangerous 
situations to overcome. 3. Demonstrate 
Commitment: To receive the benefit of the 
advice, a Hunter has to go through an entire 
Scene without using Gifts.
Gift - Offer Help:  Once per Scene, the 
Wise Fox can appear and help one 
who previously paid the cost he 
required. A Hunter may spend 1 
Soma to get advice from the Wise 
Fox, thus receiving 2 Additional 
Successes on a Position Check.

54
Fire Eater the Puppeteer
Inspired by Pinocchio (Neutral)
Once upon a time, there was a skilled puppeteer obsessed with 
the idea of creating the perfect puppet. “If only my puppets 
looked like real humans, ” he said. “Then my puppet shows could

3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Medium 5
Agenda: Listening intently to the personal stories of those I 
meet and judging who deserves to join my puppet collection 
/ Treating everyone equally, innocents and murderers alike,

Special Agenda (Carlo the Living Puppet):  I’m always 
looking for the perfect puppet. Once I find it, I will make it

Note: Carlo the Living Puppet is one of the Hunters featured 
in The Broken Ones expansion book.

55
Gift - The Puppets’ Rite:  Fire Eater can animate a 
Puppet by harnessing the soul of a dying person within 
it. From that moment on, the Puppet will be linked to 
its creator until he decides to free it. A Puppet has the

Descriptor: I’m an animated puppet and I do

1 Wound 
Opposition Level: Easy 3
Gift - Puppet:  At zero Wounds a Puppet will shatter,

return to action. The Descriptor of each Puppet must 
be completed with a feature related to what they were in 
life. Acting on that part of the Descriptor can awaken the 
dormant consciousness of the harnessed soul, allowing the 
Puppet to act independently of its master.
Gift - Master of Puppets:  Fire Eater is always 
surrounded by at least 6 Puppets. In his presence, increase 
each Puppet’s Opposition Level by 1. When he would suffer 
1 Wound, Fire Eater can sacrifice a Puppet instead.
The Masked
Swashbuckler
Inspired by the Puss in Boots (Opponent)
Once upon a time, there was a cunning Cat who made his

day on, his only master would be money.

human thanks to a magical mask. I put myself at the service

lives to get the better of every problem.

56
3 Wounds 
Opposition Level: Medium 5
Agenda: To serve my customer without asking questions / 
Mocking those who trust others too much.
Gift - Nine Lives Like a Cat: The Masked Swashbuckler

The item is disposable and the Storyteller must

Gift - Cat Mask:  Thanks to his magical 
mask, the Masked Swashbuckler can freely 
transform between human and cat, taking 
advantage of this to approach his enemy. 
The transformation is instantaneous, but it 
cannot be done in front of witnesses.

57

Inspired by the Evil Orc (Ally)

69
The Hunter’s 
Creation
Step 1 – Collect sources of 
inspiration
Each Broken Tales’ Hunter is derived from a fairytale villain. 
The first step will therefore be to decide which villain to 
“break” and which source to draw inspiration from. It is 
important to choose a fairytale, an essay, or another source 
that speaks of the villain in its most classic incarnation, 
trying to avoid modern takes. This is because fairytales and 
classic stories feature characterizations very much in line 
with the Broken Tales mood, without the adjustments for 
modern sensibilities that are quite common with the more 
recent versions. This step is a good opportunity to do some 
research and maybe discover the dark sides of a fairytale 
you thought you knew!
An important note: in the text, the term “fairytale” also 
includes many classic stories such as The Wonderful Wizard 
of OZ or Peter and Wendy, which are not technically real 
fairytales. They are, however, stories that have entered the 
collective imagination in much the same way. In truth, 
Broken Tales draws from every source including fairytales, 
folklore, and classic stories, encompassing them all in the 
term “fairytale” for practicality.
Creating your own Hunter takes some time, but 
it’s also very satisfying. Broken Tales provides a large 
cast of ready-made Hunters which can be used to get 
started right away. However, if the Players’ intention is 
to tackle a Campaign, creating Hunters from scratch 
can be a good option. Of course, nothing prevents you 
from mixing things up! Maybe some people in your 
group will create brand new Hunters, while the others 
use one of those we provide with the game.
The Hunters’ creation does not simply consist of 
describing the protagonists on paper. Use the steps in 
this chapter as a guide, both for breaking the story of 
an iconic villain, and creating the mechanics to describe 
their powers. This process is called Bringing a Villain 
to their Breaking Point: that’s how they’ll be reborn as 
a Hunter in the world of Broken Tales.

BREAKING POINT
70
Analyzing an entire text can provide a lot of material, but it 
is a rather long process. For this reason, a valid alternative 
is to do some simple online research. The web is full of sites 
that summarize and analyze classic stories, and Wikipedia 
itself can be a good starting point.
Reading the story, we’ll underline or jot down interesting 
words, phrases, or entire passages. These elements will 
become Keywords. As explained in the rules section, 
Keywords will serve as a guide and inspiration in working 
out everything related to a Hunter.
Example: The Hunter we’re going to create is inspired by 
Captain Hook from the novel Peter and Wendy. This choice 
will allow us to illustrate the complete creation process and 
at the same time show how easy it is to create an alternative 
Hunter to the “canonical one” included in the ready-made 
Hunters’ sheets (James the Swordsman, in this specific case).
To streamline the process, we’ll use Wikipedia as inspiration.
After a careful reading of the page, these are the Keywords 
that we marked:
Infamous / Bad gentleman / Loves music and poetry / 
Challenges Peter Pan to a    final  duel. When beaten, Peter 
Pan, goaded by Hook himself, kicks the Captain off the ship 
and right into the crocodile’s gaping mouth / Hook is not 
his real name / Ruthless pirate captain, sometimes seems 
compassionate / It saddens him to know that the Lost Boys 
hate him, even though he has earned their hatred / Hook 
is described as “cadaverous, ” with blue eyes and long, dark 
curls of hair that look like “black candles” when viewed from 
a distance / Barrie later wrote of him: “In a word, the most 
handsome man I have ever seen, although, at the same time, 
slightly disgusting. ”
Step 2 – Tell the Hunter’s 
backstory and bring them to their 
Breaking Point
In this section, we need to describe how our villain is reborn 
in the world of Broken Tales, taking on a role and finding 
their purpose within the Order. We will write a short story, 
trying to integrate all relevant Keywords. Y ou don’t have 
to use them verbatim: synonyms or phrases derived from 
Keywords will also work. 
In this short backstory, we’ll focus on detailing these points:
 ◆ Who is the Hunter and what do they do in the Order?
 ◆ What role do they play and how do they live in Broken 
Europe?
 ◆ What is the bond they maintain with their fairytale 
counterpart?
 ◆ What is their Breaking Point , or flipped details, 
from the classic fairytale that makes this Hunter 
original?
To accomplish all that, we’ll flip one or more parts of the 
original story to separate the Hunter from their classic 
identity as a villain. This inversion will serve to distinguish 
them from the original in their objectives or actions. 
The best way to do this is to think about one of the most 
distinctive characteristics of the villain and make it a flaw (if 
it isn’t one already) or change its connotation completely...
Some examples:
 ◆ Garou the Old Wolf joined the Order so as to escape the 
many humans chasing him. The strong and overbearing 
wolf of many fairytales undergoes the treatment he has 
always reserved for others.
 ◆ Baba Yaga is a child witch and no longer the classic old

BREAKING POINT
71
hag that everyone fears. Trapped in the body of a little 
girl, no one really takes her seriously. She’s relegated to a 
secondary role when all she would like to be is respected 
and feared. At least, until she shows off her power.
 ◆ James the Swordsman is a gentleman who fights with 
honor and respect . He let his guard down against a 
mysterious boy and lost a hand for it . He suffered the 
archetypical low blow that the original Captain Hook 
inflicted on his enemies. As a man of honor, James would 
never dare to do such a thing.
 ◆ The Astonishing Pied Piper Without A Name lives only 
for music and has committed various crimes in its name. 
Money and punishing others are things that don’t interest 
him.
Example: Let’s tell the story and take Captain Hook to his 
Breaking Point.
I am a fearsome smuggler  and the sea has always been my 
home. With my trusty crew, I have traveled along the English 
coasts transporting all kinds of merchandise. We were 
outlaws in the eyes of fools frightened of the word pirate. 
During one of my raids, I rescued a strange boy from the sea. 
Despite my instincts telling me otherwise, I decided to bring 
him on board. Never was a choice regretted more. A curse 
hung over the young man: the sky darkened and a terrible sea 
monster struck my ship. I fought until the end when its giant 
jaws closed over me. I survived, or at least my body survived. 
My heart stopped beating and with it, my body began the 
slow process of decay. 
Immortal but doomed to ruin, I entered the Order in the 
hope of finding a remedy for the spell that afflicts me and 
which the mysterious boy passed on to me.
For this story we used these keywords:
 ◆ Fearsome Smuggler (Infamous). 
 ◆ A Strange Boy / Terrible Sea Monster / I Fought until 
the end (He challenges Peter Pan to a final duel. When 
beaten, Peter Pan, goaded by Hook himself, kicks the 
Captain off the ship and right into the crocodile’s gaping 
mouth.)
 ◆ Trustworthy crew / Pirate / On my ship (Ruthless pirate 
captain, sometimes seems compassionate).
 ◆ My body has started a slow process of decay (Hook is 
described as “cadaverous, ” with blue eyes and long, dark 
curls of hair that look like “black candles” when viewed 
from a distance).
Hook’s Breaking Point is that he has lost his great challenge 
and with it his obsession with defeating Peter Pan. Now 
he is a walking corpse, but he would like to continue to be 
considered the infamous gentleman he always was.
Step 3 – Detail the Hunter 
Descriptors
In this step, we will define the three Hunter’s Descriptors. 
Descriptors must cover:
 ◆ Who is the Hunter and what do they do in Broken 
Europe or at the service of the Order?
 ◆ What is the Hunter’s most distinctive asset/feature?
 ◆ What is the bond they maintained with their fairytale 
counterpart?
The simplest way to create a valid Descriptor is to start from 
one of the Keywords and use it as a guide for answering the 
questions given for each category, so as to set up an excellent 
starting point to inspire you. A Hunter’s Descriptor must 
have both a positive part (useful/signals a resource) and

72
a negative part (the Downside ) that will provide XP if 
brought into play. In this first step, however, it is good to 
focus only on the first part of the Descriptor. Once you have 
defined all three Descriptors, it will be easier to analyze 
them and find good Downsides.
A Descriptor must be written in the first person, 
in the Hunter’s own words.
To keep the Descriptors consistent, start with one of the 
following active phrases: I know/I have something, I use 
something, I get something, I own something, I am something, 
I want/I will be something.
Descriptors have several functions in the game:
 ◆ They outline the background, behavior, and 
appearance of a Hunter, helping the Player to focus 
their interpretation. 
 ◆ They can be invoked while narrating a Position or 
Defense Check to get 3 Base Successes instead of just 1.
 ◆ At the end of each Scene, if a Descriptor has caused 
the Hunter troubles, it can be checked off and then 
converted into 1 XP during the next Interlude. 
 ◆ They help the Storyteller and the Players understand 
the Hunter’s skills and what they are capable of 
doing. Descriptors are the basis from which to 
determine if a Position Check is necessary, what results 
can be obtained, and what information the Hunter has 
about a menace they are facing.
In a Campaign, during an Interlude at the end of each 
Scenario, it will be possible to change or evolve a Hunter’s 
Descriptor according to what happened at the table. The 
creation of the Descriptors is a very important step, but 
don’t stress about it. As a Player, focus on what you like and 
consider consistent with the story of your Hunter, without 
thinking too much about the practical applications in-
game. The adventures faced by your Hunter will lead them, 
over time, to change and make their Descriptors more 
relevant to the path they have taken.
Example: Descriptors of the revived Captain Hook.
The first Descriptor describes who the Hunter is, what role 
they play in the Order, and, in general, what do they do in 
the world of Broken Tales.
 ◆ I am a fearsome and merciless ship captain and with 
my crew of criminals and smugglers I have carried out a 
thousand raids. I’m always afraid of a mutiny and that’s 
why I don’t trust others.
This Descriptor highlights that Hook is a skilled ship 
captain and that his crew is a useful and active resource. 
Unfortunately, Hook does not trust anyone and his paranoia 
will surely lead him to hesitate at a critical moment.
The second Descriptor details one or more resources, 
characteristics, or special abilities that define what the 
Hunter can do.
 ◆ Leadership requires decisiveness and charisma, traits I 
possess in spades, which is also why I win every duel. 
But I am a gentleman and I often spare my opponent to 
prove my superiority.
This Descriptor represents Hook’s abilities as a duelist 
and commander, especially when it comes to a challenge. 
However, our captain will let his guard down if a challenge 
is going his way...

73
The third Descriptor will be linked to the Hunter’s Dark 
Ego, detailing the most powerful Gift.
 ◆ I have an immortal, albeit repulsive , body. My corpse-
like appearance inspires fear in my opponents. Despite 
it, I love elegant clothes and comfortable, refined places.
This Descriptor portrays Hook as undead, able to continue 
even after suffering the worst injuries. His corpse-like 
appearance scares mortals, making him even more 
fearsome. Unfortunately, Hook can no more escape 
his former life than he could surrender its pleasures. 
We can’t wait to see him in action at some party, as he 
awkwardly tries to pass for a distinguished nobleman! 
Creating 
Gifts
Gifts are unique abilities that make the Hunter special 
compared to ordinary humans. On a mechanical level, 
Gifts are the Player’s way of defining the Hunter’s powers 
and abilities, which can significantly impact the narrative. 
Gifts are divided into two categories:
Normal Gifts: The first two Gifts of each Hunter. They 
are generally composed of 2 Archetypal Effects and 1 
Archetypal Cost.
Dark Ego Gift:  The third and most powerful Gift of any 
Hunter. It can contain any number of Archetypal Effects, 
but always requires the Archetypal Cost Activator, a 
Descriptor that describes what the Hunter must do or what 
has to happen for the Gift’s power to trigger. Alternatively, 
the Dark Ego Gift can be activated when taking a Wound, 
even a self-inflicted one, to represent the effort necessary to 
unleash its power.
Describing what the Gift does in the story
The first step in creating a Gift is to describe what happens 
in the Scene when the Hunter uses it. Based on the linked 
Descriptor, the Keywords and what we would like the 
Hunter to do, we need to explain what the Gift does in 
order to have a foundation from which to process all 
mechanical effects.

ARCHETYPAL EFFECTS
74
These are the options at our disposal to find a base Gift to 
build upon:
 ◆ Find a relevant Gift from a Hunter sheet. All 
ready-made Hunters are built to bring a specific 
characterization into play. For this reason, they are 
often the best starting point for a custom Gift.
 ◆ Find a relevant Gift from one of the Order’s 
Treasures (read more abouth them in The Broken 
Ones expansion). Like those of the Hunters, they are 
designed to represent iconic powers and abilities.
 ◆ Add 2 Archetypal Effects and 1 Archetypal Cost 
together (presented later in the chapter). Each Effect 
grants a special rule that can be readjusted to the 
Hunter’s backstory.
The number of Archetypal Effects is not limited in the case 
of the Dark Ego Gift, and the Activator is extrapolated as a 
Descriptor from the Gift’s effects. 
It is essential to describe what the Gift does in the story 
before settling on what it will do at the rules level. By 
visualizing what happens when a Hunter uses their Gift, it 
will be very easy to sift through similar Gifts from other 
Hunters, Archetypal Effects, and Costs to make the right 
choices.
Example: All ready-made Hunters’ Gifts are created on this 
principle.
The Descriptor says: I am a sorceress and my magical powers 
allow me to do whatever I want. I need the right amount of 
time to cast my spells. Hence, we decide that Baba Yaga can 
literally get what she wants with magic, but each spell must 
have its own magic formula and a physical object to use as a 
focus. The Archetypal Effect is Manipulating Reality and as 
we have formulated it, Baba Yaga can literally do anything 
she wants. From the description, it is clear that the use of an 
object can be associated with the Archetypal Cost Sacrifice , 
to which we add Soma Expenditure to limit the power of the 
Witch.
The Gift becomes, therefore: When you want to achieve 
something, you may use witchcraft. Choose what you want 
with a sentence like: I want to fly on that old broom or I 
want to look like a demon in the eyes of those fools. Your 
spell must be focused on an object in your possession, such as 
a broom to fly or a mask you can wear to look like a demon. 
Pay a cost from 0 to 2 Soma, decided by the Storyteller, to 
activate your spell for the duration of a Scene. Remember 
that you can’t ask for someone’s sudden death, but you can 
inflict Wounds with your actions through magic.
     
In the case of Babai the Judge, we imagined the Hunter with 
a large, dark body that blends into the surrounding shadows. 
Babai can scare anyone and only the bravest can hold his 
searching gaze. His Gift has become: You are like a sinuous 
shadow that can reach anywhere. During a Scene, you can 
arrive at a location by covering any distance and overcoming 
any obstacle if one of your allies or an NPC calls your name. 
When you manifest your true nature, everyone who witnesses 
it will be frightened and will not act against you unless you 
threaten them first. The Storyteller may ask you for a Position 
Check to frighten particularly powerful characters. Receive 1

ARCHETYPAL EFFECTS
75
Additional Success on all Defense Checks you make against the 
actions of someone who is now fearful.
Because this is Babai’s Dark Ego Gift, we can use as many 
Archetypal Effects as we want. In his case, they are: Freedom of 
Movement to move in the shadows, Manipulate NPCs for the 
fear Effect, and 1 Additional Success to strengthen the potency 
of the Gift, as we have specifically said that only the bravest 
dare to threaten Babai.
Keep in mind the fundamental rule for creating 
a Gift: the narrative coherence of the Gift’s effect is more 
important than how strong the Gift is. Don’t concern yourself 
with Gifts that are too strong or too weak, but rather focus 
on Gifts that are consistent with the narrative characteristics 
of the Hunter, making sure that they add something to their 
story.
After playing for a while, it’s always possible to modify the 
Gift, or adjust the total Soma pool available to a Hunter, to 
calibrate the availability of a Gift that either is too strong or 
rarely used. It might take a few Sessions of experimentation, 
but each Player must be aware that arriving at the ideal 
version of the Gift may take time and the Storyteller may have 
to evaluate what happens in the game. If a Gift makes sense 
with 3 Archetypal Effects instead of 2, no problem: just add 1 
Archetypal Cost or subtract 1 from the Hunter’s Soma value. 
If the choice of the Archetypal Effect is based on narrative 
coherence and not on the quest for powerful combinations, 
your games will improve.
All the Gifts of ready-made Hunters are created on these 
principles and are great study material for understanding the 
creation process, as well as reasons for making exceptions 
and minor deviations from the standard rules.
Archetypal Effects
Archetypal Effects are mechanical bonuses representing what 
happens when the Gift is brought into the game.
In some Gifts, two or more Archetypal Effects are added 
together to achieve the intended narrative outcome. The 
most iconic example is magic, with which a Hunter can 
Manipulate Reality, Inflict Wounds, and Create Descriptors. 
As already mentioned, the priority is always to create a Gift  
related to the Hunter’s backstory, limiting any potential 
power by adding Archetypal Costs to either the same Gift or 
to the Hunter’s other Gifts. Baba Y aga, the Child Witch is a 
great example of how to tie together three Gifts to make her 
Magic Gift very powerful.
1 Additional Success
Effect: +1 Additional Success to the Hunter Checks in a 
particular situation.
Keywords: expert / advantage / focused skill / above average.
Tips: This is the standard Effect to complete a Gift in 
the absence of other ideas, or to emphasize a particular 
specialization of the Hunter. It is very easy to connect 
1 Additional Success to a potential action generated by 
the other Archetypal Effect of the Gift, and this is a great 
reinforcement to characterize what the Gift does.

76
2 Additional Successes
Effect: +2 Additional Successes to the Hunter Checks in a 
particular situation.
Keywords: master / best / lifelong experience / Unparalleled.
Tips: This Effect is great for highlighting an area where the 
Hunter excels more than anyone else. The advantage of 2 
Additional Successes is palpable. For this reason, it is best 
to tie this Gift to Costs that limit its use to very specific 
narrative situations. Another recommended Cost is Check 
Limit, binding the Gift to Position Checks or Defense 
Checks.
3 Additional Successes
Effect: Grants +3 Additional Successes to a single Hunter’s 
Check.
Keywords: master stroke / unleashing power / superiority 
/ targeted action.
Tips: This Effect is great for a one-time power burst and 
sets up an action that the Hunter will rarely fail. This Effect 
is indicated for Gifts that generate fiction in which the 
Hunter is confident when displaying their skill. 
Additional Action
Effect: The Hunter has the possibility to act 2 times 
consecutively in an Exchange, to act 1 extra time during an 
Exchange, or to perform secondary actions.
Keywords: rush / quick / support / hit and run.
Tips: This Effect is used to create moments where the 
Hunter moves to a better position after acting or is able to 
perform multiple actions at the same time. If the description 
of the Gift involves multiple actions such as hitting and 
running, this Effect is best suited to add an extra maneuver. 
Having additional actions is an Effect that ties in with Costs 
requiring the investment of something: Soma, Wounds, or 
Increments are great ways to represent the effort required.
Advantage Token
Effect: The use of the Gift grants the Hunter 1 or more 
named Tokens linked to the Gift itself. The Hunter can then 
spend these Tokens to create another bonus Archetypal 
Effect.
Keywords: strategy / power charge / accumulation / 
combination.
Tips: This Effect represents a mix of various powers that 
need to be themed on the Hunter’s abilities. It is a great 
Effect for creating combinations, where the Hunter does 
something in the fiction to accumulate Tokens that will be 
useful in the future.
Allies
Effect: One or more NPCs are in the Hunter’s service. Each 
has a distinctive Descriptor and will do their best to help 
the Hunter. The Hunter can also sacrifice or use an ally to 
obtain additional effects.
Keywords: animal companion / followers / minions / gang.
Tips: This Effect encompasses all of the Hunter’s possible 
connections to other NPCs traveling alongside them. 
Followers, trusted animals, or silent guardians are all 
grouped under this Effect. When creating a Gift with this 
Effect it is good to think about what the Hunter can do if 
their Allies are eliminated or put out of action so that the

77
Gift doesn’t become useless during a Scenario. A great 
additional Effect can be the ability to suffer a Wound in 
place of the Hunter.
Automatic Successful Outcome
Effect: A single Check performed by the Hunter is 
automatically a Successful Outcome, with the player 
selecting one of the three types. Alternatively, a Failed 
Check can turn into a Successful Outcome.
Keywords: determination / flipping the situation / 
infallible / aiming at the goal.
Tips: This Effect can be very powerful and must therefore 
be linked to one or more Costs limiting its use, such as a 
time limit or a very specific Narrative Limit. In general, 
it is not very exciting if the Hunter always succeeds at 
the right time through the use of the Gift. It’s more fun 
if a specific situation such as anger or pride leads them to 
excel at any cost, thus affecting their behavior in the story.
Create a Descriptor
Effect: The Hunter can create a Descriptor that allows 
them to power up or extend their abilities. The Descriptor 
affects the Hunter, modifying their abilities.
Keywords: impose / manipulate / manage / create.
Tips: This Effect is one of the most versatile and can be 
used in any Gift where the Hunter creates something to 
use to their advantage. Magic is the most obvious example, 
but the ability to adapt to the situation or have many 
resources at your disposal are also excellent examples. 
Create a Descriptor can be too versatile of an Effect, so it’s 
recommended to tie it strongly to a theme that highlights 
the background and characteristics of the Hunter.
Dampen
Effect: For one action, the Hunter is able to prevent an 
enemy from using a Gift, or lower their Opposition Level 
by 1 or more.
Keywords: exterminator / specialized against a threat / 
hatred / revenge.
Tips: This Effect allows the Hunter to make enemies less 
powerful and must be linked to some knowledge or to an 
emotion that, in the fiction, makes them fundamentally 
opposed to that specific threat. 
Disguise
Effect: During the course of a Scene the Hunter can 
appear different from what they really are, or make sure 
that they are not noticed by others when entering or 
exiting the Scene. No one can prevent them from doing so. 
Keywords: underestimating / shadow / masquerading / 
disguising.
Tips: This Effect is useful if the Hunter needs a free hand 
without attracting attention. At the same time, it can also 
represent individuals who enjoy behavioral freedom on a 
social level. A good Cost related to this Effect is Reaction 
to an Event, as it allows you to set a condition (such as a 
certain behavior or the activation of other Gifts) to reveal 
the Hunter’s deception.

ARCHETYPAL EFFECTS
78
Freedom of Movement
Effect: Allows the Hunter to move freely, ignoring 
environmental and enemy restrictions, such as a Defense 
Check to avoid interference. 
Keywords: escape routes / disappear / appear / hunt.
Tips: This Effect can allow the Hunter to move or follow a 
target without performing Defense Checks. It is optimal for 
elusive characters or as an additional Effect of an Outcome 
with an Increment.
Get XP
Effect: The Hunter receives 1 or 2 XP for achieving a 
particular type of action related to their story and personal 
goals. In general terms, performing this action gives inner 
strength to the Hunter.
Keywords: reward / goal / improve / willpower.
Tips: This Effect is perfect as a complement to some Gifts 
as it does not provide a direct bonus that can throw the 
story out of balance, instead offering a long-term benefit 
for the Hunter. However, it is good to keep the story of the 
Hunter in mind and consider the motives that drive them 
to improve or to learn from mistakes.
Inflicting a Wound
Effect: This allows you to inflict 1 additional Wound on a 
target or inflict 1 Wound by simply activating the Gift.
Keywords: mighty / anger / targeted / specialized. 
Tips: This Effect is great for giving the Hunter offensive 
power and making them feel more dangerous. Given the 
small pool and their importance, it is good to limit the 
possibility of inflicting additional Wounds with a dedicated 
Cost, such as requiring an Outcome with Increment or, 
even better, imposing a Narrative Limit. The Gift’s Effect 
can also indicate a power that the Hunter manifests and 
that inflicts 1 Wound on potential targets, independently 
from the Hunter’s action.
Interruption
Effect: This allows you to interrupt an opponent’s action to 
activate a special effect or act before them.
Keywords: lightning-fast / feint / foreshadowing / 
bodyguard.
Tips: This Effect allows the Hunter to act outside of his 
turn in an Exchange and to interrupt what is happening in 
the story. It is a good way to highlight a Hunter’s predictive 
and threat-management skills, allowing them to stand 
between the danger and another subject or to activate an 
Effect that is triggered in relation to a specific event. 
Manipulate NPCs
Effect: The Hunter can impose conditions and behaviors 
on the NPCs they interact with. By default, the Effect is 
automatic on Minor NPCs and may require a Position 
Check for Major NPCs and Dark Presences.
Keywords: charm / fear / charisma / trust.
Tips: This Effect represents a Hunter’s innate charm or 
social skills. As it can be very useful in social contexts, it is 
good to limit the Effect with Costs that mark its use as Once 
per Scene or take into account that the Hunter will activate 
it whenever they can.

ARCHETYPAL EFFECTS
79
Manipulating Reality
Effect: The Hunter can decide on an Effect and make it 
real. The Effect can vary based on context and encompass 
several other Archetypal Effects, such as Reshaping the 
Environment or Inflicting a Wound. It is usually good to 
specify what can be done and what falls within the limits 
of the Gift, such as affecting or not affecting living beings.
Keywords: magic / arcane power / supernatural item / 
supernatural. 
Tips: Like Create a Descriptor, this Effect allows the 
Hunter to apply the Gift directly to the narrative without 
going through a Position Check. It is advisable to tie this 
Effect to Costs that require the investment of resources, 
such as Soma or Wounds.
Multiple Benefits 
Effect: A list of minor advantages (from 2 to 4 Effects) that 
the Hunter can use independently or all at once. Advantages 
can be inspired by all the other Archetypal Effects. The use 
of each must be tied to a specific action.
Keywords: resources / versatile / variety / multifunction.
Tips: This Effect is especially good for Gifts that do many 
different things. In this way, it is possible to wrap different 
abilities within the same Gift. It is important that all of the 
Gift’s options are consistent with its description. 
Multiple Targets
Effect: Using the Gift allows the Hunter to hit more 
than one target. However, a different Position Check for 
each target is required. Alternatively, the Hunter can 
automatically eliminate an NPC with a single Wound 
without going through a Check.
Keywords: bloodlust / impetuousness / alone against all / 
aimed shot.
Tips: This Effect makes the Hunter more fearsome against 
multiple opponents. The most suitable Cost is Increment 
Expenditure, so that using the Effect is not tied down by 
more dire Costs, but is still linked to the success of a Check. 
Against minor enemies with a single Wound, consider 
using this Effect as a secondary bonus to another action. 
Obtaining Information
Effect: Provides useful information to the Hunter on 
one or more specific topics. The Hunter gets information 
useful in the continuing Scenario or suggestions from the 
Storyteller on a possible choice to make.
Keywords: wisdom / intuition / clairvoyance / travel.
Tips: This Effect is a great complement to a Gift and grants a 
subtle advantage that can be used to mechanically represent 
a Hunter’s skills or expertise. An easy way to add to the 
fiction is to link the type of information obtained to talents 
and knowledge indicated by the Hunter’s Descriptors.

80
Recover Soma
Effect: The Hunter can recover 1 Soma by achieving 
a particular goal, such as demonstrating something or 
making progress towards a personal quest. In general terms, 
performing this action gives the Hunter inner strength.
Keywords: determination / milestone / inner strength / 
goal.
