Tips: Thanks to this effect, the Hunter can replenish their 
Soma reserve as a result of certain actions. To prevent 
abuse of this Effect, the specific actions required should be 
somehow intimately linked to the Hunter’s background. Be 
careful not to create a loop with Soma Expenditure, which 
is an unpleasant situation where a Hunter spends 1 Soma 
and then immediately recovers it. 
Recover Wounds
Effect: The Hunter can recover, or help someone recover, 
1 or more Wounds over the course of the Scene. This effect 
can also represent a regeneration power or immortality.
Keywords: healing / regeneration / taking a breath / 
endurance.
Tips: Thanks to this Effect, the Hunter can heal themselves 
or others. It is good to limit the use of this Effect so as not 
to make Wounds management during a Scene superfluous. 
On the other hand, if the idea is to reproduce a regeneration 
power, the recovery can be faster and free of additional 
costs. 
Replication
Effect: The Hunter can copy a Gift or Descriptor they have 
seen in action. Alternatively, they can have access to several 
Gifts for a limited time.
Keywords: adaptation / ace up your sleeve / replicate / 
imitate.
Tips: This Effect is highly versatile. For this reason, it 
must be limited in its use with relevant costs, such as Soma 
Expenditure or Once per Scene. It can be a good idea to 
limit the Gifts a Hunter can replicate to those which are 
consistent with their Descriptors.
Reshaping the Environment
Effect: The Hunter can impose conditions and changes 
to their environment, creating a dedicated Descriptor. 
Normally the Effect lasts a Scene, but it can also be 
permanent. It isn’t possible to directly harm someone 
by Reshaping the Environment. The Storyteller can rule 
that someone is at risk of injury if it makes sense in the 
circumstances. However, in that case the Storyteller may 
request a Position Check from the Hunter using this Gift.
Keywords: nature / light / dark / passage.
Tips: The significant difference from the Create a 
Descriptor Effect is that this Gift does not act on the Hunter 
but on the surrounding environment. It can still be a good 
idea to link related Descriptors to the narrative theme of 
the Gift, such as manipulating nature, a specific element, or 
other themes suggested by your Hunter’s concept.

81
Resources
Effect: The Hunter has or can easily procure whatever 
resources are needed. This includes special items that can 
grant 1 Additional Success.
Keywords: wealth / travel bag / contacts / organized.
Tips: As with the Allies Effect, you may want to consider 
where the Hunter draws their resources from and what they 
can do to recover access to them if the narrative separates 
them from the main source.
Support
Effect: Using this Gift allows the Hunter to grant 1 or more 
Additional Successes to an ally. If this is applied to an NPC, 
the Gift allows them to increase their Opposition Level. 
It is also possible to bestow other benefits such as Soma, 
Wounds, or temporary Descriptors. The Effect is active for 
the duration of a Check in the case of Additional Successes, 
or for a Scene in the case of Descriptors. The Storyteller can 
evaluate other cases. 
Keywords: team play / advice / leader / strategist.
Tips: This Effect is a good way to empower Hunters related 
to teamwork or leadership. The Once per Scene limitation 
is a good Cost here, so as not to make support for other 
Hunters a constant.
Archetypal Costs
Archetypal Costs represent a series of limits to prevent a Gift 
from being used excessively during the game. Thanks to the 
Archetypal Cost, the use of a Gift will be more focused and 
distinctive, and its use more consistent with the Hunter’s 
backstory. The Dark Ego Gift must always have Activator 
as Archetypal Cost. This Cost compensates for the fact 
that the Gift can contain more than two Archetypal Effects 
without having to worry too much about balancing Costs.
Activator (Dark Ego Gift only)
Effect: The activation of the Gift is dependent on the 
Descriptor, which acts as a trigger and represents the 
link between the Hunter now and what they were in their 
fairytale of origin. Alternatively, the Hunter can suffer or 
self-inflict 1 Wound to activate the Gift.
Keywords: Dark Ego / the original fairytale / true self / 
revealing what one really is.
Tips: The Activator must always be an action that connects 
a Hunter to their villainous forebears in their original 
fairytale. To outline it, think about what the Hunter has to 
do in the narrative to highlight their connection with the 
fairytale character they were. When the Hunter activates 
their Dark Ego, what they used to be must unambiguously 
re-emerge in the fiction.

ARCHETYPAL COSTS
82
Check Limit
Effect: The Gift can only be activated during a certain type 
of Check: either Position or Defense. 
Keywords: specialty / perfect defense / perfect action / 
unrivaled.
Tips: This is a great way to limit Effects that grant 
Additional Successes or that, in general, can be used too 
frequently. Linking a Gift to a type of Check is also a good 
way to make it more distinctive, with a Position Check 
implying a more active role and Defense Check suggesting 
a more passive one.
Checks Penalties
Effect: After the activation of the Gift, the Hunter receives 
1 or more Penalties for Position or Defense Checks, either 
for an action or the whole Scene.
Keywords: guard down / distraction / obsession / 
confusion.
Tips: This Cost, when applied to the whole Scene, is a 
serious problem and can be a good way to balance a Gift  
that is otherwise too strong. Conversely, a Penalty applied 
to the following action allows you to balance a Gift with a 
small disadvantage. It is easy to justify the Penalty in the 
fiction by tying it to the Hunter’s emotional state, such as 
being in a frenzy or confused by what happens after using 
the Gift.
Drawback Descriptor
Effect: Activating the Gift allows the Storyteller to create a 
related Descriptor that highlights a flaw or loss of control. 
The Descriptor remains active throughout the Scene.
Keywords: loss of control / unexpected problem / attracting 
attention / flaw.
Tips: This Cost is perfect for providing narrative hooks 
that the Storyteller can exploit to take the story in new 
directions. The Hunter avoids immediate, direct costs in 
exchange for risking potential long-term complications.
Drawback Token
Effect: The use of the Gift grants the Storyteller 1 or more 
named Token linked to the Gift itself. The Storyteller spends 
the Tokens, later on, to represent a long-term cost to the 
Hunter, creating a negative Descriptor, assigning 1 Penalty, 
or other effects. As a minor Effect, this Cost allows you to 
get rid of accumulated Drawback Tokens with a specific 
action during an Interlude. The action must be linked to 
the Hunter’s backstory.
Keywords: ignore the consequences / insanity / 
unpredictable / promise.
Tips: This Cost represents a mix between spending 
resources and creating long-term consequences in the 
Scenario. The Hunter gets an immediate advantage, with 
the Storyteller promising to make them pay for it at a 
later time. It’s an excellent Cost when representing special 
powers that aren’t fully under the Hunter’s control.

ARCHETYPAL COSTS
83
Increment Expenditure
Effect: The Gift can be activated as an Increment. 
Keywords: mastery / superiority / show / combination.
Tips: This Cost is particularly suitable for Gifts related to 
combat or fast actions. The frequency of use depends on 
the number of Checks made by the Hunter. 
Narrative Limit
Effect: The Gift can only be activated when a specific 
condition manifests in the narrative, such as during a one-
on-one challenge or in the presence of a specific object. 
Alternatively, the Gift has the obligation to introduce a 
specific Descriptor representing the limit of what the Gift  
can do.
Keywords: basic rules / tools / situation / routine.
Tips: This is one of the most versatile and characteristic 
Costs because it allows you to limit the use of a Gift by tying 
it to its narrative component. Narrative Limit is a great way 
to push the narrative directly towards what you expect 
your Hunter to do in play. In general, a good place to start 
is to analyze how the power of a Gift is activated. Often, 
you’ll notice that the Narrative Limit is already in the Gift’s 
description.
Once per Scenario
Effect: The Gift can be used once per Scenario.
Keywords: twist / changing the cards / great power / 
absolute protagonist of the moment.
Tips: This Cost should be reserved only for Gifts with a 
great impact on the narrative or which allow the Hunter to 
perform incredible actions. If the idea of the Gift is out of 
the box, like resurrecting or forever changing the mind of 
a minor NPC, the limit of Once per Scenario is optimal to 
give the right emphasis to the Gift and make it a real twist. 
This Cost strongly limits the use of the Gift, so consider 
applying it only to one of the Archetypal Effects. In this way, 
a very powerful Effect can be contained without completely 
impeding the Gift’s usage.
Once per Scene
Effect: The Gift can be used once per Scene.
Keywords: distinctive action / moment under the spotlight 
/ marked / regular cost.
Tips: This is by far the most versatile Cost. During each 
Scene it creates a moment in the spotlight for the Hunter’s 
Gift, preventing abuse. 
Once until the Next Interlude
Effect: After being used, the Gift recharges after an 
Interlude. 
Keywords: recharge / rest / preparation / limited.
Suggestions: This Cost strongly limits the use of the 
Gift and it may be best to apply this Cost to only one of 
the Archetypal Effects associated with a Gift. In this way, 
a very powerful Effect can be limited without completely 
impeding the Gift’s usage.

84
Random Effect
Effect: The activation of the Gift is linked to a die roll. 
When the chosen numbers are rolled the Gift may not 
work at all, or does work but with a serious drawback, or 
something totally unexpected may happen. Alternatively, 
you can create a list of six random minor Effects that 
accompany the Gift’s use.
Keywords: random / malfunction / try your luck / bet.
Tips: This Cost can be a good substitute for set Costs such 
as Soma or Wounds, but should be used sparingly. Take into 
account that with Random Effect a Gift could, with certain 
rolls, always be activated for free or always have a Cost. In 
the long run, that might prove frustrating for the Hunter. 
Reaction to an Event
Effect: The Gift automatically activates or stops when a 
specific event occurs.
Keywords: defense / reaction / contrast / preparation.
Tips: This Cost is great for encouraging the Hunter to 
avoid certain actions with consequences that effectively put 
an end to the Gift’s use. This is a Narrative Limit variant 
relying on external circumstances rather than the Player’s 
choice.
Sacrifice
Effect: Activating the Gift requires a special focus or the 
sacrifice of an expensive resource. Each time or just once, 
at the discretion of the Storyteller.
Keywords: essential components / ritual / pledge / specific 
equipment.
Tips: This Cost is perfect for Gifts related to magic or that 
require a focus to manifest. The sacrifice may not always be 
easily within the Hunter’s grasp, or the Hunter may have to 
come up with imaginative ways to satisfy the requirement 
for a focus.
Soma Expenditure
Effect: Using the Gift requires the investment of 1 or more 
Soma points.
Keywords: energy expense / limited resources / commitment 
/ manifesting power.
Tips: This is the best Cost to limit Gifts that can be used 
multiple times over the course of a Scene. It leaves the 
Hunter free to decide when to use the Gift while imposing 
a hard limit.
Wound Expenditure
Effect: Activating the Gift inflicts 1 or more Wounds to 
the Hunter.
Keywords: effort / exposed / adrenaline / punishment.
Tips: Paying 1 Wound is a very heavy Cost. For this reason, 
it is suitable for Gifts that are extremely versatile or have a 
significant impact on the narrative.

85
Give a distinctive name to 
the Gift
Even if it seems trivial, the name of a Gift reveals a lot about 
the Hunter who uses it. I Will have my Satisfaction, James 
the Swordsman’s Gift, immediately clarifies the feeling of 
revenge that moves him. If Y ou Don’t Stop, I’ll Call the 
Bogeyman! immediately makes us understand the true 
nature of Babai the Judge. Choosing a good name for a Gift 
must not be underestimated.
Example: Hook’s Gifts
The first Gift highlights the fact that Hook isn’t the only 
survivor of the sea monster: his crew has returned from the 
dead with him to serve him. Hook can then call his men 
back to service at any time, resurrecting them from 
the ground itself.
First of all, to build the Gift it’s a good idea to check 
for a similar one among the Hunters’ ready-made 
ones. We find a suitable one on Regina the Thief of 
Hearts’ sheet.
The Queen: During a Scene you always have a 
couple of trusted servants with you, ready to execute 
your every order. Create a Descriptor for each, 
which represents the skill for which you hold them 
in your service. Your servants will do their best to 
satisfy your every wish. Decide how you pay them. 
The Storyteller, when and if they deem it necessary, 
will ask you to honor their services so as not to lose 
the use of this Gift for the rest of the Scene. You can 
check this Descriptor multiple times to get XP .
Hook will have the crew around him and they will 
be undead too. The first part of the Gift is therefore already 
perfect for our purpose. On the other hand, it isn’t consistent 
with Hook’s backstory that he has to “pay” for the services in 
some way. After all, they are gallows’ material! We replace the 
Archetypal Cost (Sacrifice, in the case of Regina) with Soma 
Expenditure. We’re also expanding the Archetypal Effect of 
Allies, adding the ability for Hook to shield himself with his 
men. Since the Gift seems stronger than Regina’s, we add an 
additional Archetypal Cost in the form of a Narrative Limit. 
Hook’s followers are always undead, limited in their actions 
by being corpses.
Summarizing, Hook’s Gift will be:
The Captain: During the course of a Scene you can spend 
1 Soma to summon a couple of trusted sailors, who will 
emerge from the ground to help you. Create a Descriptor for 
each, representing the skill for which you hold 
them in your service. Remember to use the 
word walking dead and that, as versatile as 
you wish them to be, they are still pirates. 
Your subordinates will carry out your every 
order, even the most dangerous ones. They’re 
already dead, so things can’t get much worse. 
Once per Scene, when you suffer a Wound 
you can sacrifice one of your subordinates 
to suffer it in your place, leaving them in a 
thousand pieces.  
The base for the second Gift will consist of the 
Keywords skillful, duel, and win. Hook is a 
formidable opponent and his sword skills are 
unmatched. To make him properly heroic, 
he’ll be one of the best swordsmen in the 
world.
The basis will be the Archetypal Effect 
1 Additional Success , renamed Win

86
Every Challenge. As Archetypal Cost, we apply Narrative 
Limitation. As a second Effect we pick Additional Action, 
but another relevant Effect is 3 Additional Successes. Being 
3 Archetypal Effects, a good way to have them all is to balance 
them with 2 Costs represented by 2 extra Narrative Limits.
The second Gift of Hook becomes:
Winning Every Challenge: When you fight, few opponents 
can stand up to you. Get 1 Additional Success when you 
perform a Check to attack or disable an opponent. Once per 
Scene, when you eliminate a Minor NPC with 1 Wound, you 
can immediately take another action. Once per Scene your 
bonus increases to 3 Additional Successes, but you cannot 
use this Gift against an enemy who has already suffered a 
Wound.
The third Gift is the Dark Ego one and, in this case, it is 
possible to combine multiple Archetypal Effects without 
limits. The important keywords are: immortal and awe-
inspiring.
Again, a Gift we already worked out for a Hunter serves as 
a great foundation: Babai the Judge already has a fear-based 
Gift.
Dark Ego - If Y ou Don’t Stop I’ll Call the Bogeyman!
You are like a sinuous shadow that can reach anywhere. 
During a Scene, you can arrive at a location by covering 
any distance and overcoming any obstacle if one of your 
allies or an NPC calls your name. When you manifest your 
true nature, everyone who witnesses it will be frightened 
and will not act against you unless you threaten them first. 
The Storyteller may ask you for a Position Check to frighten 
particularly powerful characters. Receive 1 Additional 
Success on all Defense Checks you make against the actions 
of someone who is now fearful.
Activator: Enter or exit a shadow by manifesting your true 
nature and scaring someone.
The first part of the Gift (related to moving in the shadows) 
is not a good fit for Hook, while the part where he frightens 
everyone is exactly in theme with the Keyword awe-
inspiring. It will therefore suffice to simply use the Archetypal 
Effect Recover Wounds, linking it to the Keyword immortal.
The Dark Ego Gift will be:
Dark Ego - I’m the Fearsome Captain Hook!
Your body is immortal and therefore you cannot be destroyed 
by conventional means. Once per Scene, you can spend 1 
Soma to immediately regenerate a Wound you have suffered. 
You can move the severed parts of your body for a short 
period of time. When you manifest your true nature all the 
witnesses will be frightened and will not act against you 
unless you threaten them first. The Storyteller may ask you for 
a Position Check to scare off particularly powerful characters. 
Receive 1 Additional Success on all Position Checks you make 
to interrogate or intimidate someone.
Activator: Flaunt your cadaverous body and show everyone 
that you have conquered even death.

87
Determine the reserve of Soma
A Hunter’s Soma reserve starts at 6 points and can be 
reduced or increased in relation to the power of their Gifts.
 ◆ If a Gift has many Archetypal Effects, it has a strong 
influence on a Scene when it is activated, or can be used 
with a very high frequency, you may want to consider 
reducing the Hunter’s Soma reserve by 1.
 ◆ If a Gift is very situational, confers a very specific 
advantage, or is rarely used consider raising the 
Hunter’s reserve by 1.
During the course of the game, don’t hesitate to adjust the 
Soma supply if you find that a Gift is too strong and versatile 
or not strong enough. As mentioned earlier, you don’t have 
to categorically limit power, just make small corrections to 
make a Hunter as balanced as possible. Y ou will realize that, 
depending on your table’s style, the same Gift can have very 
different applications in play!
A combat-based Gift will be enormously useful if your 
playstyle points to very action-packed storytelling. On 
the other hand, if you love investigations and social 
confrontation, it could appear in the Scene with much less 
frequency.
Example: Hook’s Soma reserve.
Hook has three Gifts that, from a first evaluation, are within 
the average. Winning Every Challenge has 3 Archetypal 
Effects but also 3 Archetypal Costs, and that should even 
out the score. As a result, it is optimal for Hook to start 
with a reserve of 6 Soma points. For comparison, James the 
Swordsman has the Gift Tick Tock, Here Comes the Beast, 
which is not fully under his control and can only be used in 
open spaces. Because of these limitations, we have estimated 
that its use is much more situational and therefore James’ 
Soma reserve is 7.
Detail the equipment
All that remains to do is to detail the equipment and any 
other possessions based on the Descriptors.
The story told so far and the Descriptors are the basis for 
defining the equipment of the Hunter.
Even if the rules don’t focus on equipment as a game bonus, 
this doesn’t mean that it’s not important to outline a Hunter’s 
possessions, especially in order to describe something about 
them. Each Player must outline the Hunter’s possessions, 
with the help of the following questions.
 ◆ What did the Hunter do before joining the Order? Do 
they have a home and allies to return to?
 ◆ Does the Hunter bring with them a present from a 
loved one? Is it something they would never part with?
 ◆ How does the Hunter deal with danger and combat? 
With what weapons and means?
 ◆ What items and possessions are essential for the Hunter 
to do what their Descriptors suggest?
 ◆ Does the Hunter have something rare and unique or 
of great value with them? What advantages does this 
possession confer?
 ◆ Does the Hunter have an object, a coat of arms, or a 
symbol with them that gives them social importance?
Finally, try to give the Hunter at least one item that is a 
reference to their original fairytale.

88
Example: Hook’s equipment.
The old ship Jolly Roger /A crew ready to serve me / My 
trusted dueling blade / A tooth of the monster that devoured 
me / Fine clothes for every occasion / A bag full of treasures 
and riches of my raids /A book of poems.
The Hunters’ creation: 
wrapping it up
At this point, it is good to stop for a moment and describe 
what has been created. Each Player must portray the 
character that has emerged from the Hunter’s Descriptors. 
The Storyteller, meanwhile, can compile information 
relating to the Order and the world of Broken Europe, 
based on the same Descriptors. 
For each Descriptor created, the Storyteller can ask one of 
the following questions:
 ◆ Where in the world is it characteristic to have this 
Descriptor? 
 ◆ Who created the equipment, technique, or ability 
outlined in the Descriptor? 
 ◆ Are the abilities of the Descriptor unique or do other 
subjects possess them as well? 
 ◆ Why is it that the Descriptor is so important to the 
Order? 
 ◆ Is the Order aware of the Descriptor? 
 ◆ How does the Hunter keep the Descriptor hidden from 
the eyes of ordinary people? 
 ◆ Who allowed or taught the Hunter the power of the 
Descriptor? 
 ◆ Why is the Descriptor so important to them?
Each takes turns describing the Hunter quickly and 
answering any doubts and questions. Having done this, the 
Player expresses an idea linked to their Hunter’s role in the 
Order and how they ended up in the service of the Papacy. 
This step is useful for creating Hunter-related situations 
and NPCs.
As in the creation of the Descriptors, in this phase, the 
Storyteller’s task is to connect to the players’ ideas and, 
above all, to ask questions to obtain useful elements for 
the first Scenario and suitable material to outline Broken 
Europe.
The Players decide if the Hunters have already had the 
opportunity to carry out missions together, or if the first 
Scenario is the first time they meet. In Broken Tales, the 
Order glues the Hunters together and the quick setup of the 
game purposely avoids creating overly detailed backstories. 
During Interludes, there will be the chance to ask more 
questions about the other characters. This will allow Players 
to add details of the evolving stories of the Hunters’ time 
together. However, if the Players find it interesting, nothing 
prevents them from deepening their Hunter’s backstory at 
this juncture as well.

SCENARIOS
89
ScenariosEach Scenario of Broken Tales is inspired by a 
classic fairytale, “breaking” it into a new story of mystery 
and horror set in Broken Europe. Scenarios can be played 
as single episodes, choosing a group of Hunters from the 
pre-generated selection, or can be included in a long-term 
Campaign as explained on page 243.
When the Storyteller selects a Scenario, they must read 
it from top to bottom in order to have a clear idea of the 
connections between the Main NPCs, their Agenda, and 
the Events that are presented. Broken Tales’ emerging 
play style will make each story feel different, thanks to the 
possible combinations of Scenarios and Hunters. When 
deciding which Scenario to suggest, the Storyteller must 
keep in mind the following considerations.
Safety Keywords
Each Scenario presents a series of Keywords related to 
sensitive topics that the plots will focus on. Depending on 
how well they know their fellow players, the Storyteller will 
thus be able to evaluate whether to list the Keywords to 
the Players before starting to play, or possibly just mention 
the themes that could come up. Knowing the Keywords 
can suggest to the players what can happen during the 
story and create a “spoiler” that may not be appreciated. 
However, it is advisable to share them with groups of players 
you don’t know or when in doubt, as not doing so could 
make someone uncomfortable. The gaming table must be 
a safe and fun place for everyone to enjoy the experience. 
Some Scenarios are more adventurous and fantastic than 
others and choosing them can be a good way to avoid 
potentially awkward situations. However, it is always good 
to highlight to all participants that Broken Tales is a game 
that uses (even extreme) horror as one of its foundational 
characteristics, and this aspect must be understood and 
accepted by everyone sitting at the table.
Scene Tree
In each Scenario, the information provided is limited 
to what the Storyteller really needs to manage the game 
without setting up a plot in which the choices of the Hunters 
would have little weight. The Scene Tree, on the other 
hand, offers a plot “to follow” and is an excellent system 
for a group of newbies or to run a single Session. Even if 
the Storyteller is not planning on using the Scene Tree, the 
information provided there makes for useful additions and 
can help bring focus to various parts of the story such as 
places and characters. The Scene Tree can therefore also be

90
used as inspiration to support what the Storyteller will have 
to establish on the fly during the Session in response to the 
choices of the Hunters.
Choosing the Hunters
After reading the Scenario, the Storyteller could opt to 
exclude some Hunters from the roster if their Gifts would 
cause problems in a Scenario, even if Players are otherwise 
free to choose. To give an example, Bluebeard’s Gift, The 
Key to Every Door, allows you to read the memories of a 
corpse. This can make the Storyteller’s life difficult if the 
Scenario opens at the scene of the crime, with the dead 
person being immediately “interrogated” by Bluebeard 
thanks to his Gift.
Even if Broken Tales is based on the absolute choice of the 
Hunters, who hold the reins of the story, the game must also 
be fun and interesting for the Storyteller to run. Excluding 
a Hunter who would create difficulties because their Gifts 
would too easily overcome the challenges in the Scenario is 
absolutely an acceptable choice.

91
Scenario Creation
A Broken Tales Scenario is, at its core, a set of 
information that the Storyteller prepares before the Game 
Session, including events, characters, and other details. 
A good Scenario introduces an interesting mystery for the 
Hunters to investigate, leaving a lot of room for the Players 
to “maneuver” between one Scene and the next. Although 
it is possible to improvise everything on the spot, we don’t 
recommend it: you risk losing the plot thread and may end 
up with an incoherent and confusing Session. By nature, 
Broken Tales references classic fairytales. Improvising a 
Scenario that draws from a fairytale while twisting it into 
a Broken Tale is a tall order. Instead, the Storyteller should 
do some prep work before the game.
This book presents many ready-made Scenarios, inspired 
by as many classic fairytales, which the Storyteller can use 
to dramatically cut the preparation time. 
Y ou might want, however, to break a fairytale not featured 
in this book or break one that we’ve covered, but put your 
own spin on it. This section is designed to explain in detail 
the procedure to do so.
Step 1 – Choose a fairytale to break
Each Broken Tales Scenario is an investigation involving a 
group of Hunters. Although the inciting incident can vary 
(the Hunters arrive at someone’s request, or they happen 
upon a mystery by chance or, again, they follow a lead from 
a previous Scenario), the events will always be linked to a 
story that unfolds through locations and NPCs inspired by 
the fairytale we’ve chosen to break. 
To begin, the Storyteller will have to choose the underlying 
fairytale. As for the creation of the Hunters, in addition 
to books, the web is full of sites and sources about classic 
fairytales. The basic assumption is that starting from a 
story, its broken version will be much darker, most likely 
featuring elements of horror, and its protagonists will be 
transfigured into something new. 
The Scenario itself doesn’t have to be the inverted version 
of the original story. What matters is that its core elements 
are present and used in original ways. A good idea for an 
interesting story to play is more important than consistently 
following the fairytale’s story. If you creatively use Keywords 
for inspiration, you’ll see situations and characters emerge 
organically, already linked to their counterparts in the 
original story.

92
We will use Red-Hood Iskra (inspired by Little Red Riding 
Hood) as an example Scenario. You’ll find the full version in 
this book, on page 118.
Step 2 – Jot down the Keywords
Once the fairytale has been chosen, it’s time to read it again 
with an eye for the elements that interest you. Keywords can 
be created by jotting down key phrases or concepts at the 
core of the fairytale. As mentioned, Keywords will inspire 
the creation of elements that are effortlessly consistent with 
the original story.
Example: The Keywords of Little Red Riding Hood.
Little house near the wood / Little girl / Mother / Sick 
Grandmother / Forest / The mother advises being careful 
during the journey / Wolf in the woods / Deception / The Wolf 
presents himself deceitfully / Disguise / The Hunter notices 
what has happened / Opening the Wolf ’s belly and filling it 
with stones / The Wolf decides to take a walk in the woods / 
The Wolf is breathless and dies.
Step 3 – Define the Opponents, the 
other Main NPCs, and their Agenda
Before writing a Scenario, it is good to define the “main 
actors” on the stage. First of all, choosing the main 
antagonist of the Scenario is crucial, since many of the 
challenges the Hunters will face center on their opponent.
In Broken Tales, parts are switched. 
The “bad guys” are now Hunters, while the “good ones” 
become the Broken Tales at the center of the Scenarios. 
However, casting the original protagonist as the one who 
pulls the strings can become boring fast. It will often be 
more interesting to assign this role to a secondary character 
from the original fairytale. Many players approach the 
game taking for granted the classic twist of good people 
turning bad and will surely be surprised if the real enemy is 
not who they expect.
Every single character in the fairytale can be a good 
antagonist, provided they have the right motivations. 
Considering that the “original villains” of a fairytale often 
become Hunters, it is not necessary to include them in the 
Scenario if an interesting role doesn’t naturally emerge in 
the story. It is much more important to add depth to all the 
other characters.
If inspiration doesn’t strike, roll several times on the 
following table, once for each column. The results will give 
you some hints regarding how to pick the right villain.

93
Roll Who What do they 
want Why By what means
1 An ally of the protagonist. Redemption. To live a better life. Deception.
2
Someone the protagonist encounters during the 
story. Power. To stop suffering. Magic.
3 The protagonist of the story. Wealth. To receive recognition. Servants.
4 Someone connected to the story's protagonist. Expertise. To bask in success. Resources.
5
Someone the protagonist helps throughout the 
story. Revenge. To teach a lesson. Determination.
6 A neutral character who appears in the story. Control. To preserve themselves. Skill.
In the following paragraphs, we will show the step-by-step 
process of creating the Scenario of Red-Hood Iskra as an 
example. Spoilers ahead: read only if you intend to play 
the role of the Storyteller.
Example: Iskra’s main opponent.
We immediately decided that the little protagonist of 
Little Red Riding Hood would not be the real “villain” of 
the Scenario. The following options remained among the 
Keywords: Mother, Sick Grandmother, and Hunter. 
By opting for Mother (a very marginal character in the 
original fairytale and therefore great for taking the Players off 
guard), we rolled on the table to determine her motivations, 
obtaining: 3 for What does she want, 1 for Why , and 3 for 
By what means. As a result, Little Red Riding Hood’s Mother 
tried to get rid of her daughter in order to regain a comfortable 
life (Wealth and To live a better life) as the wife of a naive 
noble. She also manipulates those around her with the help of 
her trusted Servants. These followers are Vincent the Chief of 
the Guards (inspired by the Hunter in the original fairytale) 
and little Anne.
Each NPC in the Scenario must have an 
ambiguous role.
While we are discussing villains and switching roles, note 
that it would be a mistake to think that all NPCs have to 
be evil. Frequently, an opponent with logical motivations 
is more interesting and charismatic than those who are 
evil just for the fun of it. Make sure that different NPCs 
types are represented in the Scenario, ranging from those 
with noble purposes to those who are driven only by their 
own base instincts. The protagonist of the original fairytale 
should have a prominent role in the plot, even if they’re 
not the main villain. They could be, for example, someone

94
the real villain uses against the Hunters. In general, a good 
Scenario should contain at least three types of NPCs:
 ◆ NPC with an Agenda that is opposed to the Hunters’ 
purpose: if not a direct follower of the main villain, 
they could be someone who takes advantage of the 
current situation or who generally does not appreciate 
the Hunters’ presence.
 ◆ NPC with an Agenda that supports the Hunters’ 
purpose, or that simply has a good reason to be angry 
with the main opponent.
 ◆ NPC with a non-aligned Agenda: they can be both an 
enemy or an ally, based on the choices that the Hunters 
will make during the Scenario.
Example: Red-Hood Iskra’s other NPCs.
In Red Hooded Iskra we have Vincent, Former Huntsman 
and Chief of Guards (inspired by the Hunter in Little Red 
Riding Hood) as an NPC opposed to the Hunters, Anastazia 
the Sorrowful Spirit (inspired by Little Red Riding Hood’s 
Grandmother) as an NPC ally, and Greskar the Big Black 
Wolf (inspired by the Big Bad Wolf) as a neutral NPC. Iskra 
herself is an NPC with a neutral Agenda, since even if she 
acts with the right motivations, she does it violently and 
uncontrollably, which will ultimately lead to a confrontation 
with the Hunters.
For inspiration regarding an NPC’s Agenda, you can roll 
multiple times on the following table.
Roll Acts like this because... Opposed Agenda Favorable Agenda Non-aligned Agenda
1
Unaware of what they are 
doing.
Hates the Order and the 
Hunters.
Would give their life for the 
one they love. Is looking for something.
2
Feels deep, ingrained 
hatred.
Is the trusted right hand of 
an opponent. Wants truth to triumph. Wants to get rich.
3
Is driven by greed and a 
thirst for power.
Takes advantage of the 
situation.
Wants to ingratiate 
themselves with the Order.
Wants to replace the 
antagonist.
4
Is seeking acceptance and 
understanding. Shares a dark secret. Owes someone. Defends a place or a person.
5 Is forced. Enjoys manipulating others. Seeks redemption. Wants to subvert a 
situation.
6
Is related to someone with a 
strong influence. Is deeply evil. Is pure of heart. Seeks revenge.

95
Step 4 – Give the Scenario a 
backdrop
All the stories and events of Broken Tales are set in the late 
1700s of an alternative Europe. More precisely, the year 
1780 is used as a reference for events and places. This date 
is not binding and events may span a few decades forward 
or backward to include a place or a historical event useful 
to contextualize the Scenario. Doing a little research can 
provide useful inspiration. The eighteenth-century is a 
period full of events and changes that complement the 
rural and dark context of the Broken Tales.
Example: The setting of Red-Hood Iskra and the legend 
of Gévaudan.
For the Scenario of Red-Hood Iskra, the historical connection 
is with the legend of the Gévaudan Beast, which in 1764 
terrorized many small villages in that wooded area of France. 
This choice immediately provides a useful backdrop from 
which to draw references for locations and NPC names. As 
in the original legend, Iskra and her pack harass the village 
of Durfort, where Elizaveta (Iskra’s mother) took refuge after 
abandoning her daughter in the woods at the mercy of wolves.
Step 5 – The story so far
Now that we have a starting point, some Main NPCs, and 
a location where the Scenario is set, it’s time to write what 
happened before the Hunters arrived. In this process, some 
Keywords will have to be flipped, to create the Breaking 
Point of the Scenario.
In the following example, you will find the Keywords that 
we consider useful for the next Steps highlighted.
Example: Red-Hood Iskra’s background story.
Elizaveta of Volhynia (the main antagonist, inspired 
by Little Red Riding Hood’s mother) has always been an 
attractive woman, accustomed to the rich salons of the gentry, 
and the pomp of nobles and courtiers. Becoming pregnant 
with Iskra, her eldest daughter, was a curse for her. 
Abandoned by her wealthy lover, Elizaveta was impoverished 
for years, with few skills, little work, and only the help of her 
old mother Anastazia (inspired by the sick Grandmother 
of Little Red Riding Hood). A chance encounter with 
Gerard Dubois (Minor NPC), who is ignorant of her past, 
has rekindled Elizaveta’s ambition for a life of comfort and 
decadence as the future wife of a nobleman. 
Elizaveta, determined not to miss such an opportunity, 
sent her daughter Iskra alone to visit her grandmother. 
Elizaveta knew that she was exposing her to the dangers 
of the dense forest of Białowieża, a place full of beasts and 
bandits, and deadly to the unwary. Ten-year-old Iskra trusted 
her mother and disappeared forever into the darkness of the 
forest. Anastazia had always treated Iskra with love and 
kindness and, overwhelmed by the grief of her granddaughter’s 
sad end, died soon after. Free and unattached , Elizaveta 
fled to Gévaudan  with her new husband, unaware of her

96
daughter’s real fate. In fact, Iskra did not die, but joined 
Greskar’s wolf pack (inspired by Little Red Riding Hood’s 
Wolf), becoming a wolf herself...
Step 6 – The story when the 
Hunters arrive
The background details of the story will lead to the 
intervention of the Hunters. The next step is to briefly 
describe the situation that will unfold from the first actual 
game Scene. This is important to clarify the Storyteller’s 
ideas and to detail the Main NPCs’ Agenda. The NPCs’ 
Agendas are key to the Scenario, because they guide the 
Storyteller’s decisions, particularly when deciding how 
NPCs react to the Hunters’ actions. 
As we compile the NPC statistics, this paragraph will help 
us define the Agenda of each one of them. In the example 
below, we have highlighted the Keywords that will come in 
handy.
Example: The story when the Hunters arrive in Red-
Hood Iskra.
Iskra didn’t die in the forest of Białowieża. Instead, she faced 
every trial with a fierce tenacity and eventually prevailed, 
defeating Greskar. A huge, ferocious werewolf, Greskar 
infected Iskra with the wolf ’s curse. Becoming a member 
of the pack and the favorite of the alpha Greskar, Iskra set 
off for the Gévaudan, following the scent of her mother, 
determined to get her revenge. Thanks to the fury of the wolf 
pack, Iskra has thrown the city of Durfort into disarray. Now, 
she waits in the shadows for the right opportunity to enter 
the palace and devour her unworthy mother. Elizaveta has 
realized what is happening, for Iskra left a pendant she had 
once made for her on the windowsill, a memento of a rare 
moment of happiness. Elizaveta, a cunning manipulator , 
got rid of the token and did not panic or lose her temper. After 
convincing Gerard to turn to the Order, she will seek to move 
the pieces on the chessboard, using them to rid herself of 
her unwanted offspring once and for all...
Step 7 – Define all the Scenario 
Parts
Now that we have good foundations for the story and its 
main actors, it’s time to define the “stage” for the Hunter’s 
investigation. This process is open-ended and does not 
follow a strict list of instructions. In brief, this phase consists 
of creating Places, Minor NPCs, and finally Events, which 
are particularly useful in managing the Session. Y ou’ll often 
go back and forth between all the various elements. This is 
perfectly normal. Indeed, it is much easier to create hooks 
and connected situations as you add details to other things, 
rather than planning everything in advance.
When creating a Scenario, always keep in mind how much 
you want to plan and how much of the story you want to 
emerge from the Players’ choices. There are two main ways 
to play Broken Tales.

97
Emerging story
In this mode, Broken Tales’ Scenarios don’t have a 
pre-built plotline, nor Scenes or situations that are 
mandatory steps for the Hunters (with the exception 
of the First Scene). Writing the background, the 
story when the Hunters arrive, detailing the NPCs’ 
Agenda, and eventually adding Information to 
Places, will create a network of intentions and 
situations that will put obstacles in the Hunters’ 
way. A sandbox plot is key for a good game of 
Broken Tales. The Hunters are individuals out of 
the ordinary and their Gifts can easily break Scenes 
that are overplanned. The Scenario must therefore 
provide the Storyteller with all the Events and 
Agendas necessary to allow the world to respond to 
the Hunters’ actions.
Scene Tree
If you and your table enjoy a more guided game, the 
last part of this chapter will cover how to create a 
Scene Tree that gives you a more defined plotline 
to follow. If the storyline of the Scenario is heavily 
focused on investigation, a good option is to detail 
all obtainable Information found in a Place and be 
very explicit about it while playing. The Storyteller 
will then have a series of narrative hooks useful to 
connect the situations emerging at the table.
Defining Places
Each Scenario should have at least five or six Places to set 
a “stage” for the different Scenes. The best way to select 
Places to prepare is to think about the context in which the 
Main NPCs move. It is essential to always have a village 
or a city context to refer to. The Hunters may move their 
investigation into the wilderness, but having a place to 
return to is always useful in the course of a Scenario. One of 
the key themes of the game is how the Broken Tales move 
at the edges of society. An urban environment is useful 
in creating social connections and providing “ordinary 
people” who can experience the horror of the revealed 
Broken Tales.
Keywords for an urban area:  A small village on the 
edge of the forest / A remote mountain community / A small 
seaport / A village along a major road / A remote military 
outpost.
Y ou can get more granular for other types of Places, in 
order to have more control over the range of action that 
NPCs and Hunters will have in the Scenario.
Keywords for locations:  The mansion of a wealthy 
family / An old abandoned tower / A natural cave complex / 
A mine / A clearing in the woods with a strange magical aura 
/ A cemetery / A large stable / A farmhouse on the edge of the 
village / A secret tunnel / A small workshop / A deconsecrated 
church / A hunting lodge / A craftsman’s shop / A warm and 
welcoming tavern / The heart of a large forest / A small house 
in the woods / A treehouse.

98
Each Place must have a Descriptor that outlines its 
characteristics. The Place may also feature one or more  
Threats and a List of Related Situations.
We have seen how to define Descriptors and Gifts. Now, 
let’s spend some words on the List of Related Situations, 
which are a good method to give variety and depth to 
the Places of a Scenario. This list features elements that 
can enter the Scene randomly, giving the Storyteller new 
ideas to reinvigorate the plot when Players are stuck or 
undecided. Make a List of 3 or 6 items, then freely assign 
each to the results of a d6.
Using the List is never mandatory, but can often be a 
valuable aid. The same result cannot be used more than 
once in the course of a Scenario to avoid repetitions. Lists 
of Related Situations mainly serve two purposes:
 ◆ Manage encounters: if a Place is occupied, or an area 
NPCs are likely to pass through, each option would 
determine who will be present when the Hunters 
arrive.
 ◆ Bringing clues into play: if the location contains 
Information or areas to explore, a roll can determine 
what the Hunters discover when they arrive.
Y ou can then consider +1 or -1 modifiers to make some 
results more likely or link them to external elements, such 
as arrival during night or day.
Example: The Oaks Glade.
In Red-Hood Iskra, the Oaks Glade is an area Iskra’s pack 
may pass through. The List of Related Situations is:
1-3: The glade is empty, but there are traces of wolves and 
the bones of devoured prey. After ten minutes, 8 Black 
Wolves arrive and ambush the Hunters. 
4-5: A pack is at the glade: Greskar + 8 Black Wolves. 
6: Iskra is at the glade, alone if it is daytime or with the pack 
(4-5 result, but without Greskar) if it is nighttime.
As a modifier: if it is night, add 2 to the roll.
Minor NPCs
While Major NPCs emerged in the initial planning 
phase, when detailing Places and Situations it is good to 
draw up some Minor NPCs connected in some way to 
the plot. Minor NPCs will often be ordinary humans, but 
nothing prevents you from using secondary characters 
from the original fairytale that have not found space yet. 
Each Minor NPC must be linked to a Main NPC or a 
specific storyline situation. They should be considered 
as a narrative hook that will be useful to give depth to 
what happens in the Scene. If you reach a dead end during 
the Session, introducing a Minor NPC will open up new 
avenues for the Hunters.
Example: Red-Hood Iskra Minor NPCs.
There are seven Minor NPCs in the Scenario. Anne, the 
Little Chatterer (linked to Elizaveta) who acts as an eye on 
the village for her mistress. Nicholas, the Hunter  (related 
to the wolves in the forest) who can lead the Hunters, as he 
knows the forest. Geneviève, the Chemist  (connected to 
Iskra’s Grandmother) who can communicate with spirits and 
contact Anastazia. Corinne, the Housekeeper (connected 
to the village) who can provide information and rumors of 
various kinds. Marcel, the Time-Waster (connected to Iskra) 
who can make the Hunters aware of Iskra’s presence. Didier, 
the Grumpy Old Man (linked to Elizaveta) who can provide 
information about Gerard’s new wife and her dubious past.

99
Finally, we have Gerard Dubois (linked to Elizaveta), the 
noble who requested the Hunter’s intervention and makes 
decisions for the village, but who is easily manipulated.
Review the Agenda of the 
Main NPCs
Each Main NPC must have an Agenda which, in general, is 
defined at the beginning of the Scenario’s creation process. 
Now that the Scenario is almost complete, review all the 
Agendas of the Main NPCs to ensure they fit with all the 
other elements. Minor NPCs may (but don’t have to) have 
an Agenda. Often, defining everyone’s relationship with a 
Main NPC is enough to get started.
Events of the Scenario
At this point, it is a good idea to come up with a list of 
Events that “could happen” when a specific situation occurs. 
Because Events are an extension of the NPCs’ Agendas, 
managing the plot’s progress will be much easier if you 
have a practical method of tracking when triggers occur, as 
well as their associated Events. As with Minor NPCs, each 
Event must be linked to one of the Main NPC’s Agenda, 
to represent the effects of the NPC’s will on the Scenario. 
Some Events may also be an inevitable occurrence (for 
example, the eruption of a volcano).
To create an Event, you need to write a short sentence 
containing an Activation Keyword, a trigger that will 
communicate when the Event will occur. As soon as 
this happens, the Storyteller can decide to bring the new 
situation into play, possibly taking everyone by surprise. In 
any case, it is good to leave clues along the course of the 
Scenes to make the Hunters understand what could happen 
or, alternatively, to give them time to prepare for an Event 
that they have intuited and for which they started investing 
time and resources. 
Example: The Events of Red-Hood Iskra.
There are six Events in the Scenario. Here are a couple of 
examples highlighting the Activation Keyword, with the 
linked Agenda in brackets.
If a Hunter finds Iskra’s pendant in the well, Anastazia 
will contact them, showing them a vision of Iskra as 
a child drinking at the Oaks Glade. The spirit of Iskra’s 
grandmother, while unable to communicate directly, will try 
to make the Hunters understand that Iskra is not a monster. 
(Agenda of Anastazia, Iskra’s Grandmother)
If Elizaveta goes out of town for any reason, Iskra will 
immediately smell her scent and rush to eliminate her 
regardless of any risk. Confronted with her mother, the 
little girl will lose all restraint and refuse to listen to reason. 
(Agenda of Iskra)

100
Step 8 –  The First Scene
Now that all the elements of the Scenario are defined, 
all that remains to do is to detail the First Scene, which 
will introduce the situation to the Hunters and start the 
investigation. As described in previous chapters, each 
Scene consists of 4 elements: a Place , a Time, an Initial 
Situation, and Information. Make sure to draw up a list of 
Information, which will be the basis for the Hunters’ choices 
in the following Scenes. Opening an investigation without 
useful elements to start from would make little sense. 
Example: The First Scene of Red-Hood Iskra.
The Scenario begins with the Hunters being invited for dinner 
by a tense Gerard Dubois, who can’t wait to put an end to the 
beasts that plague the Gévaudan.
Place: The ceremonial hall of the Dubois family’s grand 
palace. A richly furnished room with an enormous painting 
of Patrick Dubois, Gerard’s late father. Stern and sullen-
looking, Patrick seems to judge his son’s incompetence from 
beyond the grave with ill-concealed contempt.
  
Time: The sun set more than two hours ago and after a long 
journey, it is now dinnertime.
Initial Situation: Gerard is anxious for the Hunters to get 
to work. Elizaveta studies the situation in silence, trying 
to figure out how she can turn the Hunters’ arrival to her 
advantage, using them as tools for her own purposes. Choose 
one of the villagers to be her dinner guest, or create one of 
your own. Vincent, her trusted head of the guards, will also 
be present, of course.
Information: The following information/clues can be 
obtained from the interview with Gerard.
 ◆ The monsters that scourge the Gévaudan are ferocious 
beasts, as can be seen from the state of the victims’ bodies.
 ◆ The first two victims were Guy and Ivette, two young 
lovers who used to meet secretly in the old cemetery just 
outside the city. It is there that what remains of their 
bodies are buried.
 ◆ It is strictly forbidden to leave the city after sunset. The 
fall of night now brings with it the mournful song of 
howling wolves.
Step 9 – Scene Tree
The rules presented so far are optimal for the management 
of a game with an emerging narrative. If you intend to 
create a 100% complete Scenario, even if only to make it 
available to the Broken Tales’ community, it’s good to also 
detail a Scene Tree. This mode of play is not designed to 
cover every possible choice the Hunters might make, but 
provide Storytellers and novice players with a familiar way 
to play. The use of Information at the beginning and end 
of each Scene will therefore be more explicit, so that the 
Storyteller can present the choices available to the Hunters, 
and will keep them on the “line” drawn by the Scene Tree. 
It’s inevitable that the Hunters’ actions will stray from what 
the Scene Tree has foreseen. However, for the most part, 
this mode will make it easier for the Storyteller to keep 
hold of the reins of the story. Playing in this way requires a 
conscious agreement between Storyteller and Players that 
the goal is experiencing a good story together, following the 
flow of clues presented in each Scene.

101
A Scene Tree consists of a series of Scenes linked 
according to this basic structure:
The First Scene leads to the Second Scene, then there is a 
choice between two options for the Third Scene, Option 
A and Option B. The Fourth Scene leads to a Fifth Scene 
and then there will be either a new fork or the Epilogue, 
depending on the Scenario’s duration. Five Scenes with 
a fork in the middle are optimal for playing a one-shot 
Session of about three hours. Additional forks and Scenes 
will increase playtime by approximately thirty minutes per 
addition.
The First Scene must be structured exactly as we’ve already 
described, but with the addition of a new item: End of 
Scene, which roughly describes which new Scene the 
action will switch to.
The new Scene structure is therefore:
 ◆ A Place where the Scene takes place.
 ◆ A Time, to know when it takes place with respect to 
the previous Scene.
 ◆ An Initial Situation  that defines what happens and 
what to describe to the Hunters before starting the 
Scene.
 ◆ Information, which can be obtained from the Scene. 
Information will act as a “guide” to the Hunters’ choices, 
so it must be explicit. Don’t be afraid to railroad the 
game too much, that’s exactly what we’re looking for!
 ◆ End of Scene , which is a brief description of the 
consequences of the current Scene. The Storyteller will 
use it to direct NPC actions and bring other elements 
to the attention of the Hunters.
Example: The Second Scene of Red-Hood Iskra.
Place: The old cemetery of Durfort in the middle of the large 
Gévaudan forest. It is reached by a narrow dirt path that 
winds its way through the woods from the main road.
Time: It is early morning, the sun is high on a clear winter sky.
Information: In the cemetery, there are many wolf tracks. 
In the middle of them are the prints of two small bare feet, 
almost certainly a little girl. At the bottom of the old well 
can be found the pendant that Elizaveta tried to make 
disappear. The wolves’ tracks lead deep into the forest, to 
their den. Anastazia will contact the Hunter who recovered 
the pendant, sending them a vision of Elizaveta trying in vain 
to wash her bloodstained hands.
End of Scene: Hunters can decide whether to return to the 
town to report (THIRD SCENE OPTION A), or follow 
the trail to the wolves’ lair (THIRD SCENE OPTION B).   
Step 10 – Scenario Gifts
As a final touch to the Scenario, you can create a list of 
Scenario Gifts. This step is not mandatory.
Exclusive Gifts can be of two types:
Scenario Gifts: Can be acquired during an Interlude, if 
the Hunter has fulfilled certain storyline conditions. These 
Gifts represent knowledge and skills that persist only for 
the duration of the Scenario itself and are an addition to 
the three Gifts the Hunter already possesses. The Gift must 
be built with the rules presented in the Hunter’s Creation 
chapter and the narrative requirement for having it can be 
considered as an Archetypal Costs. It is not possible to have 
more than one Scenario Gift at a time.

102
During a game Campaign, a Hunter can use a 
previously acquired Scenario Gift in another 
Scenario if this fits the context and events. 
If this occurs, the Player must bring this 
possibility to the attention of the Storyteller, 
who will decide (evaluating the impact on the 
current Scenario) whether to allow the Hunter 
to use what they have learned in their previous 
adventures. Even in this case, however, the 
limitation remains 1 Scenario Gift per Hunter.
Example: A Scenario Gift from Red-Hood Iskra.
Wolf-killer
Requirement: Having faced the wolves in a fight at least once.
You know how to deal with the fiercest of wolves and are careful 
not to be surrounded. When you obtain at least a Standard 
Outcome on a Position or Defense Check against a pack of 
beasts, you can immediately eliminate 1 Minor animal NPC 
with 1 Wound.
Special Gift: This is a Gift reserved only for the Hunter who 
is the villain of the original fairytale that inspired the Scenario 
being played if present among the Hunters. As soon as the 
requirement is met, the Hunter will automatically gain the 
Gift for the duration of the Scenario, in addition to the ones 
already owned and without having to spend XP . Whether they 
like it or not! The Gift must be built with the rules presented 
in the Hunter’s Creation chapter. Bear in mind that the Gift  
needs to represent a connection between the Hunter and the 
Scenario and not necessarily a mere advantage! When creating 
this Gift, don’t worry as much about balancing the Archetypal 
Effects and Costs. Y ou can even have more Costs than usual.
Example: A Special Gift from Red-Hood Iskra
Where are you going all alone, sweetie?
Hunter: Garou the Old Wolf.
Requirement: To meet Iskra alone in the forest.
You are attracted to the little girl who pretends to be a wolf and 
you dream of devouring her. Once per Scene, you can spend 1 
Soma to know exactly where she is. If you anticipate her and 
arrive somewhere before her, you get 1 Additional Success on 
every Check you make to hurt or devour that little morsel. 
You can’t wait to swallow that tidbit and it makes you let your 
guard down. As long as she is present on the Scene, you receive 
1 Penalty in all Defense Checks.

103
Broken Europe
Once upon a time…
Year 1780. There are strange, half-sensed presences moving 
at the edge of our reality. Beings once relegated to the realms 
of make-believe have assumed nightmare forms, no less real 
for being fantastic. Alarmed by the constant calls for help in 
dealing with supernatural evil, Pope Clement approves the 
creation of the Order: a secret group consisting of the Hunters, 
led by the mysterious Guardians. The Hunters accept into 
their ranks anyone who has demonstrated qualities outside 
the norm, revealing a connection to the world beyond the 
threshold of reality. The Hunters’ task is to seek out and defeat 
any supernatural threat. But aren’t the Hunters themselves 
children of the twilight at the edge of reality?
The world of Broken Tales
Broken Tales is set in a fictional 18th century Europe, a 
continent made up of a patchwork of small, rural locales. 
The exploits of the Order, to which the Hunters belong, 
happen in the shadows of everyday life. They investigate 
rumors and mysteries unimaginable to the common minds 
of the time. While art and culture are discussed in drawing 
rooms, the dark shadows of the Broken Tales lurk in small 
villages, waiting to snatch unsuspecting victims. The world 
of Broken Tales is but a darker and more fairytale version of 
1780’s Europe, a theater made up of small villages and large 
cities, where superstition and shadows move on the edges 
of what the common man defines as “normality. ”
In this chapter, we will further illuminate facts and 
characteristics that are helpful for managing the environment 
around the Hunters during their investigations. The world 
of Broken Tales is entirely an invention and this game makes 
no claim to historical accuracy. In fact, the fundamental 
idea is to add as many fantastic elements as possible, while 
keeping them hidden in the shadows, at the edge of what 
humans can perceive. The Europe of Broken Tales is not 
even a cohesive and “linear” world. Each Scenario is set in a 
different place, with unique rules and situations. What this 
chapter tries to provide is a series of useful details for the 
Storyteller to add color to the journeys and adventures of 
the Hunters. 
If you look at the Broken Europe Map, you’ll see many 
places and cities. Anything that has a name on the Map 
has a Scenario connected to it (either in this book or in 
The Broken Ones expansion) or is a starting point for new 
stories and situations. These elements are detailed in the 
chapter Playing a Broken Tales Campaign on page 243.

BROKEN EUROPE
104
Overall mood
To better get into the mood of the Broken Tales setting, 
keep these simple points in mind:
 ◆ Rural life is the backdrop for the Hunters and the 
Broken Tales they investigate. Hunters have to deal with 
small villages and simple-minded people. Superstition 
and fear of the supernatural surround the Hunters. 
 ◆ When the horror of the Broken Tales is revealed, most 
people cannot help but be terrified. 
 ◆ Common people are poor, living on what little they 
earn with back-breaking labor. The nobility lives safe, 
opulent lives in big cities. Social differences are clear.
 ◆ Children see what the adults refuse to understand. 
The elderly have the wisdom of a lifetime and the 
experience that darkness really exists. Adults are 
crushed by a world that rejects what is considered 
strange and unusual.
 ◆ Large ancient forests, shadowed labyrinths, lonely 
cottages in the woods, and all the abandoned places 
of the world. The Broken Tales live freely in places 
humanity has forsaken. In the midst of society, they 
have to hide their true nature.
Keywords
This section is entirely based on the use of Keywords, a 
series of terms that reveal information related to Broken 
Europe. For example, if the Hunters set out to reach the 
next destination, the Storyteller, scrolling through this 
section, could stop at the keyword TRAVEL to get useful 
information about it. That way, you can expand on an 
aspect of the setting only when it is really necessary.
Armors & Weapons
While bulky, full suits of armor are but relics of a bygone era, 
steel breastplates are still in use among the cavalry corps of 
the Ottoman army and knights in the service of the Papacy. 
For ordinary people, wearing armor is a very strange thing. 
But despite this, many Paladins remain within the Order 
who still wear their sacred armor.
For those who follow the sword as a profession, padding 
prevents dangerous, deep wounds during training, but in 
everyday life, armor remains very impractical to use.
As far as sidearms are concerned, swords are still the overall 
favorite. The most common examples have straight and 
thin blades. More practical and concealable blades such 
as stilettos and daggers are common as well. Large two-
handed blades are a thing of the past, and only in remote 
areas such as Scotland is the claymore still used, which is a 
kind of hand-and-a-half sword. Eastern influences in the 
Balkan area favor the spread of curved and light blades 
like sabers. Usually, only the rich, the nobility, or military 
officers possess proper swords or sidearms (and the nobles 
often have them custom-made at very great expense). On 
the other hand, a good handcrafted dagger is extremely 
simple to make and available to anyone.
Additionally, any pointed or heavy work tool carried by 
a farmer can in fact be a weapon. Imagine a pitchfork, a 
scythe, or a blacksmith’s hammer being used with violent 
intent.
Putting aside cannons, which are used only on the battlefield, 
the most popular firearms are pistols and flintlock rifles. 
Wheel locks and matchlocks have been out of use for about 
seventy years. Despite being the most advanced technology

BROKEN EUROPE
105
of its time, various elements such as humidity, jamming, 
and flint breakage mean that firearms aren’t particularly 
reliable. Although powerful, firearms are inaccurate due 
to the smooth bore inside the firing barrel. Beyond three 
hundred feet, it’s challenging to shoot accurately. The 
exceptions are dueling pistols and well-made shotguns 
which, thanks to a rifled barrel, are incredibly accurate, but 
also infinitely more expensive.
Magical weapons related to the Broken Tales are highly 
sought after among senior army officers. They represent 
trophies to show off and brag about in war councils. The 
Order actively monitors new discoveries, replacing the real 
weapon with a copy at an opportune time and taking the 
original for safekeeping in the underground chambers of 
Valea Viilor.
Cities, towns and villages
Big cities are a safe place for the whole range of social 
classes. Nobles and wealthy people live in beautiful houses 
and large palaces, artisans, and merchants of the petty 
bourgeoisie can afford a modest home in the suburbs, 
perhaps on the second floor of a terraced house. The further 
you go towards the slums, the harsher life is, down to the 
poorest and most desperate, forced to sleep on the street. 
The law of the state is paramount everywhere, both in the 
countryside and in the main cities. The primary difference 
is that, in a large city, the representatives of the law and of 
the state are physically present, often in good numbers. 
Meanwhile, small villages rely on the ruling of the elders 
and religious authority. The most prominent and serious 
crimes are usually tried in city courts. The cities can’t 
afford to spread their men-at-arms thinly across both rich 
and poor neighborhoods, and the rulers don’t intervene 
to prevent unrest because soldiers are much more useful 
on battlefields. For the simplest tasks, local or city militias 
are used, called up to help only when requested by the 
authorities. If a serious crime occurs in a small village, it is 
often necessary to contact the authority of the nearest city, 
hoping that someone there has the time and willingness to 
intervene...
The Broken Tales fear cities, but often the dark feelings 
that animate them are strong enough to push them into 
unreasonable risks.
Countryside
In almost all of Europe, the majority of people live off  
agriculture, following the cycle of the seasons. In spring 
and summer, the land is worked to care for the fields and 
the crops. Winter months are dedicated to repairing homes, 
stables, granaries, and tools. Some peasants own the land 
they work, but most are just laborers. In many regions, 
agriculture as an industry has become highly developed, 
made up of wide pastures and all the buildings associated 
with animal husbandry (such as stables, granaries, ovens, 
mills, fields). Laborers can also live by forming small 
communities. 
For the Broken Tales, the countryside is the best place to live 
and thrive, safe from the crowds of big cities. Small houses 
in the woods, natural caves, and old abandoned mills are 
great places to live without completely losing contact with 
mankind.

106
Education
Public education doesn’t exist, only private lessons and schools 
are available. Basic education is provided by the family and, in 
fact, almost everyone outside the city is illiterate. For practical 
reasons, almost everyone learns some basic mathematics. 
Nobility and bourgeoisie may both vary widely in terms 
of culture and education. Some may barely know how to 
read and write, while others possess significant academic 
knowledge including history, philosophy, politics, classical 
Latin and Greek culture, linguistics, and music. In many 
countries, knowledge of the Holy Scriptures and the Bible is 
considered essential.
Fire
In every home there is a fireplace, kept constantly lit in winter. 
It is a vital source of heating and in simpler homes serves as 
a place for cooking food. In large residential homes, on the 
other hand, fireplaces are present in all rooms or in the most 
important rooms, including the bedrooms. In these buildings 
the kitchens are in a dedicated space, often on the ground 
floor where the servants’ areas are located. There, the cooking 
fires are almost always lit. The embers are kept warm to make 
it easy to start a fire. 
Candles are the most common lightsource. The rich use 
wax, while tallow is sufficient for the poor (the latter being

107
much more smoky). Sparking devices using flintlocks have 
become popular for lighting fires – they are so useful that 
some have begun carrying them on their person.
Food
By 1780, naval voyages connected every continent and 
the most powerful nations had vast colonial empires. 
As a result, it’s possible to find goods from all over the 
world in Europe. Obviously, some are much more 
exotic than others, such as the pineapples that can 
be found only at the tables of English nobles, 
but nowhere else in Europe. American 
products (potatoes, tomatoes, corn) are now 
available in markets and cultivated in crops. 
Tobacco and cocoa (only imported and not 
cultivated), however, remain reserved for 
the higher classes. Cocoa is consumed as a 
drink, while tea from India has long since 
become a tradition for the English nobility. 
The most popular products are grains and 
bread forms the basis of every meal, from 
the richest to the poorest. Wine and oil are 
very common products, as is beer (or cider in 
some regions). Butter or lard are used both for 
cooking and as condiments.
Government
Absolute monarchy, in its “enlightened” variant, is the 
most widespread form of government. The most powerful 
nations in the world such as France, England, the Austrian 
Empire, Prussia, Spain, and Russia, are all ruled by 
monarchs endowed with absolute power. Many allow 
parliamentary assemblies or Councils of State 
but keep them mostly powerless, except for 
occasionally being consulted. Each monarch 
is convinced that it is essential for a sovereign 
to hold the ultimate authority, in order to 
secure their people’s welfare, despite often 
disastrous results.
The one exception to royal authority is the 
Church. It remains independent and held 
in the utmost esteem thanks to the work of 
the Order.
Usually, only the nobility are allowed to 
participate in politics, representing solely 
the ruling class, including also members 
of the high clergy, such as the bishops. 
Immediately below them, however, 
intellectuals or wealthy citizens (merchants, 
artisans, notaries, bureaucrats, students and 
writers) are often actively involved in politics 
as well. Peasants have no say in who rules them, 
being mostly  poor, humble, and illiterate.
Information
Some newspapers are published regularly, but they are 
usually just a few sheets and are not released daily. In the 
most important public squares there are always posting 
poles or boards, where the government shares decrees or

108
information of great importance with the citizens. In many 
cities the doors of prominent churches are used for posting 
notices. Even “town criers” (people who shout the most 
important news out in the street) are still widely used. 
The Order controls information to the best of its ability, 
casting news of monsters and magic as simple superstition. 
It is a difficult and painstaking job, which requires them 
to downplay the threat so as not to frighten the common 
people. However, the Order does not stifle the stories 
completely, so that rumors still travel from one mouth to 
next, until they reach the ears of the Hunters.
Night
Only cities have walls and, consequently, gates that are 
closed at night and controlled by the patrols of the city 
militia. In small villages, you fort up at night, locking doors 
and windows while anxiously waiting for the daylight... For 
commoners, the night is a time of rest, to recover strength 
in anticipation of a new day of work. For the nobles, 
however, the night is dedicated to parties and lust. For the 
Broken Tales, night is the best time to act, safe from most 
prying eyes.
Nobility
One is noble by birth, by investiture thanks to merit earned 
by a particular family, or by pretending to be one. Since the 
use of documentation is not widespread, with enough spine 
and a lot of charisma it is possible to simply talk your way 
into the nobility.
Being noble grants many benefits including an active role 
in politics, and the possibility of aspiring to the highest 
military and judicial posts. Often, as in France, the most 
important noble families are entitled to an income paid 
directly from the state coffers. Even the most prestigious 
religious offices (cardinals, bishops of the largest cities, 
and abbots of the richest monasteries) are still largely 
the prerogative of aristocratic families. Even if treaties, 
deals, and contracts are written and signed, the word of a 
“gentleman” can often be a sufficient guarantee in itself.
The life of a noble is made up of large palaces, servants, 
sumptuous clothes, balls and masquerades. Lazing around 
at any time of the day is normal, because physical work 
is considered common and degrading. Extravagance is 
everywhere, depending on the actual wealth of the family. 
It is easy for wizards and witches to dupe a noble and thus 
obtain wealth and privileges. In the lounges and courtyards, 
however, gossip makes the rounds quickly, with a risk that 
the Order hears of it.
Order (The)
The Order represents the most powerful and extensive secret 
society of Broken Europe. Every member of the Church, 
from the humblest country priest to the richest bishop of 
Rome, is aware of the war that the Church has always fought 
with the Broken Tales. Although the Order is an established 
truth to all of them, far fewer know of the Hunters and their 
incredible abilities. The Order acts directly under the Pope’s 
authority, but in reality, it is the different groups of Hunters 
that make the difference. Depending on their choices and 
the results of their investigations, the Broken Tales are 
eliminated or absorbed into the Order, expanding the ranks 
of the Hunters themselves or the hoard contained in the 
fortress of Valea Viilor.
The Order has no ultimate purpose, other than to counter 
the Broken Tales that constantly come into being over 
time because of the One Who Plots in the Shadows. This 
mysterious figure seems to be deeply connected to the 
Broken Tales, but little is known about him except for

109
vague rumors and the few clues collected by the Hunters, 
despite years of research. 
The Order is not protected from palace intrigues and power 
games. Many bishops and others in the Church’s hierarchy 
yearn for its control or, simply put, to get their hands on 
the many magical treasures it protects. For this reason, the 
management of the Order changes constantly, with the 
Pope himself as the only true constant. Hunters therefore 
enjoy great freedom of action and, each having their own 
personal goals, they can take advantage of the protection of 
the Order to progress in their own quests.
The Order is a reality that many are aware of, but nobody 
really wants to talk about. It represents the dark side of 
the Church, which must be trusted to settle what normal 
people cannot deal with.
Ordinary people’s belongings
A pocket knife is always useful for many tasks, as is a pipe 
if you can afford to buy tobacco from time to time. A small 
necklace with a cross made of humble materials such as 
wood and iron is commonly kept for moments of prayer, 
especially among women. Clothes and accessories are 
simple and suitable for work, such as aprons so as not to get 
too dirty and handkerchiefs to clean your hands. In most 
houses, there are candlesticks for light, which are made of 
iron or wood, though the rich can afford silver. Families 
also need tools for everyday life, such as looms for weaving 
and kitchen utensils.
Personalities
The Eighteenth century is the century of the Enlightenment, 
which gave birth to a plethora of important historical 
personalities. Below is a brief, and obviously not exhaustive, 
roundup of historical personalities whom the Hunters 
could come across.
Artists
Gotthold Ephraim Lessing (1729 - 1781) was the great 
father of the German literary Enlightenment. Johann 
Christoph Friedrick von Schiller (1759 - 1805), scholar, 
poet, philosopher. Donatien-Alphonse-François de Sade 
(1740 - 1814), French writer, philosopher, poet, playwright, 
essayist, aristocrat and revolutionary politician, known 
for his numerous libertine and gothic-themed literary 
works, centered on a mixture of violence and sexuality. 
Carlo Goldoni (1707 - 1793), the great father of modern 
comedy. Giuseppe Parini (1729 - 1799), one of the leading 
exponents of the Italian Enlightenment and Neoclassicism. 
Johann Wolfgang von Goethe (1749 - 1832), a man of all-
around culture (philosopher, politician, writer, poet, art 
critic, music critic, etc.), the original inventor of the concept 
of Weltliteratur (world literature), due to his admiration 
and great knowledge of the national literatures of distant or 
foreign countries (English, French, Italian, Greek, Persian 
and Arabic). Christoph Willibald Gluck (1714 - 1787), 
composer and opera player, one of the major initiators 
of the Classicism movement. Franz Joseph Haydn 
(1732 - 1809), one of the greatest exponents of Viennese 
Classicism, is still considered the “father” of the symphony 
and the string quartet. Antonio Salieri (1750 - 1825), a 
great composer, who tutored many musicians who later 
became famous including Beethoven, Schubert, and Liszt. 
Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (1756 - 1791), a true genius of

110
classical music. Francisco Goya (1746 - 1828), the greatest 
Spanish painter of the 1700s. Sir Joshua Reynolds (1723 
- 1792), founder of the Royal Academy of Arts. Antonio 
Canova (1757 - 1822), the greatest European sculptor and 
the greatest exponent of Neoclassicism.
Scholars and scientists
Henry Cavendish (1731 - 1810), scientist. Jean Baptiste 
Le Rond D’ Alembert (1717 - 1783), mathematician 
and physicist (writer of the Encyclopédie with Diderot). 
Leonard Euler  (1707 - 1783), mathematician and 
astronomer. Daniel Bernoulli (1700 - 1782), physicist. 
Alessandro Volta (1745 - 1827), physicist. Luigi Galvani 
(1737 - 1798), physicist and anatomist. The Montgolfier 
brothers (1740 - 1810/1745 - 1799), inventors of the air 
balloon. Antoine-Laurent de Lavoisier (1743 - 1794), 
chemist. Georges-Louis Leclerc de Buffon (1707 - 1788), 
naturalist. Denis Diderot (1713 - 1784), author of the 
Encyclopédie together with D’ Alembert. Adam Smith 
(1723 - 1790), economist. Carl Linnaeus, also known as 
Linnaeus (1707 - 1778), physician, botanist and, above 
all else, naturalist. Carl Scheele (1742 - 1786), chemist, 
discoverer of oxygen. Jan Ingenhousz (1730 - 1799), 
botanist and chemist. Frederick William Herschel (1738 
- 1822), astronomer, physicist, and composer. James Watt 
(1736 - 1819), physicist, engineer and inventor of the steam 
engine.
Politicians
Cesare Beccaria (1738 - 1794), jurist, author of the essay 
“On Crimes and Punishments. ” Leopold II of Habsburg-
Lorraine (1747 - 1792), Grand Duke of Tuscany, a true 
enlightened ruler: the first monarch in the world to abolish 
the death penalty. Frederick II of Prussia, known as the 
Great (1712 - 1786), enlightened ruler par excellence, was a 
philosopher (he was a friend of Voltaire, with whom it was 
rumored he actually had a relationship), composer, jurist 
and great political innovator. Maria Theresa of Austria 
(1717 - 1780), the female Austrian equivalent of Frederick 
II, and Edmund Burke (1729 - 1797), English politician
