3-4 Ordog’s secret: The Knight with a Hundred Heads 
has always been accompanied by the strange talking horse 
Ordog. But what is this creature’s true nature? Arriving at 
the top of a rugged mountain, the Knight hopes that the holy 
man who lives there will have an answer.
5-6 The last line of defense: The Knight with a Hundred 
Heads wakes up in a small village in the Black Forest. As 
always, he does not remember how he got there, but he is 
“wearing” the head of someone whom all the inhabitants of 
the village are relying on to defeat an imminent dark threat.
Keywords: Former comrade in arms / The pact / Revenge / 
My lost family / The duty of a soldier / Barnabas my eternal 
rival / War wounds / Will beyond death / A promise / The 
Devil has many forms.

34
Iskra the Young Wolf
1-2 History repeats itself: Iskra returns to her old village 
to cherish the memories of her previous life. But Irena, her 
dearest childhood friend, has disappeared… lost in the great 
forest where she had ventured searching for herbs. 
3-4 The old woman in the woods: A small village has 
seen many of its children disappear. Desperate parents tell of 
an old witch who attracts innocent young people to her house 
in the woods and then devours them mercilessly.
5-6 The pack: Although no longer part 
of Greskar’s pack, Iskra cannot remain 
motionless to the news that her former 
pack has been targeted by a group of 
unscrupulous poachers.
Keywords: Poacher / Cottage in the woods / Grandmother’s 
smile / Young girls / The hidden predator / Fangs / The pack’s 
call / Tracks / Dark forest / The wolf among the lambs.
The Gang of Three Pigs
1-2 The wolf that threatens the houses: The Gang 
arrives in a small village harassed by a mysterious creature 
that, every night, destroys one of the houses and devours its 
inhabitants. The Gang decides to solve the problem, not so 
much because it is the duty of a Hunter, but because of the 
challenge that this phantom “house wrecker” represents. 
3-4 The new lair: In his travels, the youngest of the Gang 
has come across what could become the perfect secret lair. 
The only problem is that a whole crew of smugglers now lives 
there. 
5-6 The fortress: That damned nuisance, Saint George, 
has discovered the power of the flesh of the God Under the 
Mine and now wants to deliver it to the scholars of the Order. 
The Gang took refuge in an old, abandoned castle, preparing 
to repel the assault of Saint George and his Paladins. 
Keywords: To build / Fierce wolf / Traps / House / Shelter /

35
The Woman from the Wood
1-2 History repeats itself: The Woman from the Wood 
wakes up in the middle of the countryside, completely covered 
with blood. Near her, the dismembered bodies of five men and 
the bloody tracks of a couple of children heading for the nearby 
forest. What happened here? 
3-4 The abandoned house: A Hunter’s report to the 
Order reveals that, in the thick of the Black Forest, they have 
come across a haunted house. The Woman from the Wood feels 
a strange nostalgia hearing this. Are there hidden clues to her 
past within that house? 
5-6 The survivor: News has come of the eruption of the 
Laki volcano in the islands of the far north. Only one survivor 
has returned to the mainland, carried by the fury of the sea. 
Miraculously unharmed, the strange individual vanished 
without a trace, escaping from the hospital where he was 
hospitalized. The Woman from the Wood feels a strange 
excitement at hearing the man’s description, including physical 
traits similar to her own. Perhaps she is also native to those 
islands. Only by finding the fugitive will she get an answer.
Keywords: Lost memories / Smell of freshly baked cake / 
Little house in the woods / Children’s laughter / Fleeing from 
the past / The power to trigger mutations / Blood and sugar / 
Fire / Home sweet home / The sweet taste of raw meat.
The Immortal
1-2 The retrieval: After years of investigation, the Immortal 
is now convinced that there is a map in Valea Viilor that leads 
to the place where the needle storing his soul is kept. The 
objective of the old sorcerer is to make a copy of the map to 
retrieve the precious heirloom later when he is safe from the 
Order’s gaze.
3-4 The young Dragomira: The old Immortal has 
fallen in love with a young noblewoman from the East, but 
unfortunately he is no longer the great sorcerer who could 
make himself look handsome with magic. Dragomira came 
to Rome from Freiburg to marry a local nobleman, but the 
Immortal discovered that the young woman mourns another 
love, left behind in her native land. The Immortal must decide 
whether to help her escape, doing a good deed, or stop her in 
order to satisfy his own frustrated ego. 
5-6 A strange memory: Every now and then, some 
thousand-year-old memory resurfaces in the Immortal’s mind. 
There is a recurring one showing him naked among a circle 
of big menhirs while reciting a magic formula. What was he 
doing? And who were the other sorcerers and witches there 
with him? Finding the magic circle may yield some answers.
Keywords: Ancient magic / Beauty and youth / Distant 
memories / Wounded bodies / Fortified churches / Compelled 
obedience / Former glories / Selfishness and generosity / 
Atonement / Guilt.

36
Marina the Sea Explorer
1-2 The stolen treasure: Once upon a time, there was 
a fascinating merchant named Armand who acquired one 
of Marina’s treasures through deception. The treasure could 
allow the wielder to force others to agree with them, allowing 
for some very profitable business deals. Armand used the 
treasure against Marina allowing him to get away while 
paying next to nothing. But now the merchant is in Paris for 
a great fair and it is time for Marina to have him pay in full, 
with interest. 
3-4 Marina’s true form: A mysterious letter is delivered 
to the Order. Inside is the portrait of a woman. Marina is 
deeply affected by this because that portrait shows her true 
appearance. Who would know of her identity? And why 
would they want Marina to know they have such secret 
knowledge?
5-6 The call of the sea: A Sea Fairy sends a message to 
Marina. A fearsome monster is waking up in the abyss and 
will bring disaster to the depths. Now used to a comfortable 
life among humans, Marina is not very interested in the fate 
of the sea. But when the Fairy explains that the water will no 
longer be salty, Marina decides to leave at once, intent on not 
losing the very source of her magical powers.
Keywords: A good deal / A bad deal / Elixirs and potions 
/ Mirror / The sea / The magic of the sea / Sea fairies and 
creatures / The big market / Hidden treasure / Love can’t be 
bought.
Morgana of the Lake
1-2 The knight: A well-built young man named Accolon 
reaches out to Morgana claiming to be her lover. He says 
he never stopped searching for his lost lady. The young 
man looks familiar to Morgana but also evokes unpleasant 
emotions. Following Accolon could allow her mind to access 
lost memories, but at what price?
3-4 The mysterious island: More and more ships are 
disappearing into thin air off the coast of the island of Sicily. 
Morgana is sent by the Order because it is feared that a spell is 
at work there, cast a hundred years ago by a powerful fairy...
5-6 The delivery: Morgana is entrusted with the Old 
Sword, a relic of the Order that must be taken to safekeeping 
in Valea Viilor. But the more Morgana holds the weapon, the 
more she feels it is tied to her past.

Family feud / Merlin / Baba Yaga / Magic that changes reality

37
OZena the Afflicted
1-2 The first witch: Everything has a beginning. Dorothea’s 
story starts in the village of Schiltach, in the eastern part of 
the Black Forest. There Dorothea killed the witch Johanne 
and was reborn as OZena the witch killer. But how did things 
really go?
3-4 The gang: During her crusade against the witches of 
the Black Forest, OZena saved and took under her wing three 
faithful followers: Hirnloser the madman, Dose the leper, and 
Lowe the coward. This is the story of their meeting.
5-6 The only friend who ever loved me: Dorothea 
did not arrive alone from the future in 1780. With her was 
her faithful dog Toto, but he was lost in the magical vortex 
evoked by Henkle. During a traveling circus show, Dorothea 
recognizes her dog among the beasts that a fierce tamer uses 
to give a show. But what lies behind the creepy Emerald

Keywords: Lost / Respect / A common cause / Brain / 
Heart / Courage / Witchcraft / Solitude / Away from home / 
United in destiny.
Regina the Thief of Hearts
1-2 The price of power: Regina has trampled and 
destroyed anyone in her way, even her beloved sister, to reach 
the top. Now that she is undoubtedly the best, she suddenly 
regrets some of her actions, particularly consigning her sister 
to slavery in a fit of pique. After so many years, perhaps it is 
time to do something for someone else.
3-4 The unexpected legacy: For Regina, it seems normal 
to have a dozen husbands scattered throughout Europe, silly 
nobles that she left after having squeezed them properly. But 
now that one of them has passed away, a bunch of greedy 
relatives can’t wait to silence Regina forever and prevent her 
from collecting a notable inheritance.
5-6 You’ll die when I decide!: Jack Hearts, one of the 
most debauched servants of the Queen, was captured by a 
gang of crazy cutthroats who call themselves the Jabberwock. 
But Jack had a very important task, which was to bring 
Regina a precious box of her favorite chocolates. No one can 
play with the life of her servant, except Regina herself. It is 
time to save those precious chocolates from the cutthroats, 
and maybe even that disappointment Jack Hearts.
Keywords: The gang / Crushed feelings / Gold and 
pleasures / An order to execute / Obsession / The title of 
Queen / Former loves and broken hearts / The drive to excel 
/ The word given / Madness.
Verdoux Bluebeard
1-2 The lost bride: Strange news comes to Verdoux’s 
ear from one of his many informants. A beautiful woman 
named Adeline will soon be marrying an elderly and wealthy 
nobleman, an arranged marriage that has nothing to do with 
love. Perhaps a meaningless piece of news, except that the 
young woman is identical in appearance to Bluebeard’s sixth 
wife, who died during a shipwreck.
3-4 The secret of death: Strange disappearances are 
occurring within the slums of Amsterdam. Bodies with pierced 
skulls are sometimes found among the rubbish. It seems that 
a thriving trade has been born in the city, involving secrets 
and information which someone takes directly from the brain 
of the victims. And if there’s a mystery to figure out, Verdoux 
Bluebeard will solve it.
5-6 The house of a thousand doors: Verdoux got his 
magic bunch of keys on a business trip, exploring a cursed 
house that everyone calls the House of a Thousand Doors.

38
Every year, Verdoux needs to recharge the power of his 
magical keys. To do so, he must explore new rooms of the 
cursed house. 
Keywords: Unmentionable secrets / Lookalike / The room 
of memories / Ruthless / The price of truth / Love at first sight 
/ The missing key / Doors / Business and intrigue / Enemies 
everywhere.
Sherazade the Weaver of Tales
1-2 The djinn: Rumors come to Sherazade of a strange 
creature, a shadow with a turban that deceives people, 
promising to fulfill their wishes in exchange for their soul. 
Sherazade has used such a shadow before. Could it have 
escaped her control to roam free?
3-4 Traveling with Sinbad: At the court of a wealthy 
nobleman, Sherazade meets Sinbad the Traveler of a 
Thousand Seas who invites her to embark with him on his 
next journey. With the promise of learning an incredible 
story, Sherazade accepts, aware that the shrewd sailor hides a 
secret he does not want to reveal. 
5-6 The greedy nobleman: Sherazade is contacted by 
the messengers of a rich nobleman who has heard rumors of 
an incredible storyteller. The nobleman wants Sherazade all 
to himself, revealing himself as the same sort of man as the

Sherazade does not flee, instead imparting an exemplary 
punishment to the nobleman.

Sun Wukong the Amazing Monkey
1-2 Fight between spirits: A strange creature of 
incredible power spreads panic among the small villages on 
the Aneto Peak, in the Pyrenees. An ancestral call guides 
Wukong to the large snow-covered forests, in search of a spirit 
with which he feels he has a great deal in common.
3-4 Journey to the West: Wukong is assigned as a 
personal guard to a young novice nun. He must secretly 
escort the devout Sanzia from Rome to Westminster Abbey 
in England. But why does a common novice make a Hunter

5-6 Human cubs: Three little brothers roam the streets

a ship to Paris. Entertained by the three little “cubs, ” Sun

their mother. 
Keywords: Dangerous journey / Compulsion / Awakening 
/ Spite / Someone to defend / Wild animals / Rebellion / 
Understanding humans / Rough / Spirits of nature.
Talia the Sleeper
1-2 A prince’s dream: While she was secluded in her 
castle, Talia had recurring visions of a young nobleman in 
the same situation. Now that she’s a Hunter of the Order, it’s 
time to go and find him. 
3-4 The miracle: One morning, Talia suddenly opens her

normal person. But how long will this miracle last? And who 
made it happen?

39
5-6 The last link: Talia found a diary among the treasures 
of her ancestral castle belonging to an aunt she did not know

younger sister and that she was the one who incited them

Yukie Onn the Traveling Artist
1-2 Bloodlust: A strange creature spreads terror through

Intrigued by a monster that could have similar powers to

alleys.
3-4 The suitor: A scion of a noble family fell hopelessly

out to be a madman obsessed with the beautiful artist. Yukie

5-6 The lake of spirits: A legend says that lost in the

Keywords: Love tears / Warm blood / Snowstorm / 
Vampire / Toxic love / Spirits / Vanity / Spilled blood / A 
great show / A gallant encounter.
Yvonne of the Dark
1-2 Orphans: Yvonne has grown fond of a group of 
Parisian foundlings, living hand-to-mouth on the streets. But 
winter is coming and the ghost must find accommodation for 
her young friends so that they do not succumb to the cold and 
hardships in the catacombs. 
3-4 The witch stakes: In a small country village, an 
overzealous prelate started his own witch hunt, setting two 
young girls ablaze. Yvonne has to find out where the real guilt 
lies.
5-6 Exploitation: Jerome, a powerful lord of the 
underworld in Paris, exploits young orphans to gather money

Keywords: Fire / Abandonment / Alms / Society’s outcasts 
/ Cold / Selfishness / No consideration / Church / Grace of 
God / Injustice.

40
Managing a 
Session
An Untold Stories Session unfolds broadly like a regular 
Broken Tales Session, with a few changes due to the solo 
game mode:
 ◆ The Player plays both the role of Hunter and of 
Storyteller, using a series of tables to randomly 
determine some events and to get guidance in times of 
uncertainty. The Initial Situation Table will be used 
to determine if what happens during a Scene will bring 
into play a Keyword, which will significantly alter the 
plot towards the Hunter’s goal.
 ◆ Each Scene is set through a series of rolls on various 
tables, after which the Player describes the actions 
of the Hunter and imagines how the surrounding 
environment could consistently react. In case of doubt 
about how a situation can be resolved, formulate a 
simple Yes/No question.
Whenever a question would normally be asked of the 
Storyteller, the question must instead be put so that the 
answer can only be Y es or No:
The Hunter scans the alley. Is someone lurking here? After 
seeing the Hunter’s magic in action, is the NPC frightened?
The Player rolls 2 dice and adds the results. Consulting 
table on page 43 will reveal the answer, sometimes with 
variations.

41
No, and...  In addition to denial ( no), something goes 
wrong and the situation turns against the Hunter.
No. The answer to the question is no.
No, but...  The answer is no, but an element can be 
introduced in the Scene that, if dealt with, could transform 
the answer into a yes.
Maybe. The Player can decide between the Yes or No 
effect, but each time after that, they will have to choose the 
option they didn’t choose on the previous occurrence.
Yes, but…  The answer to the question is yes, but like 
an Outcome with a Cost, a negative element of some kind 
must be introduced.
Yes. The answer to the question is yes.
Yes, and... The answer to the question is yes, but like an 
Outcome with an Increment, a further positive element of 
some kind can be introduced.
On the side of the Question Table you’ll find two lists of 
Keywords that can be used to inspire additional elements 
for No/Yes, but... and No/Yes, and... 
Clearly depict each Scene
Just like in a Scenario, each Scene should have:
 ◆ An Initial Situation.
 ◆ A Place.
 ◆ A list of NPCs (if any).
 ◆ A clear set of information for the Hunter. 
It is possible to determine each of these elements through a 
dedicated table, or by choosing options that are consistent 
with the developing story.
In addition, it is useful to determine the Purpose of the 
Scene, which is decided by the Player. This will be the 
“guideline” to decide who or what to include in the Scene, 
according to the final goal of the Hunter.
A Scene must be played until the stated Purpose is achieved 
or until it is clear that it is no longer achievable. Keeping 
track of the Scenes will help keep track of the mood and the 
playing time, and it is also critical to the use of some Gifts. 
If an Interlude is called, after resolving it you must roll on 
the Complication Table, which will add a future problem 
for the Hunter (page 48). 
Managing Scene consistency
Obstacles, clues, and reactions of the NPCs will emerge 
naturally from the Player’s decisions and from the random 
elements generated by the tables. A fundamental task is to 
call the Position and Defense Checks each time the most 
plausible resolution to a situation involves a potential 
failure. 
Consistency, however, is more important than introducing 
randomness. If during a Scene, it occurs to you that “it 
would be nice if X happened right now” , it makes sense for 
X to happen, period. There is no need to be afraid to lead 
the story, introducing an event, NPC, or a Threat.
As a general rule, each Scene should engage the 
Hunter in at least a couple of Position or Defense 
Checks.

42
The easiest way to do this is to never take the result of an 
action for granted. There isn’t a Storyteller to manage NPC 
Descriptors, Gifts, and Agendas or use them to oppose 
you. Instead, it makes more sense to rely on Checks when 
determining if an action is successful or which option an 
NPC may choose.
Typical actions that require a Check can be:
 ◆ Convince, get attention from, or scare 
someone. Unless the introduced NPCs openly fear 
the Hunter, important NPC interactions require a 
Check to determine the outcome. However, it’s often 
wise to leave open an alternative that doesn’t require a 
Check. Frequently, the Hunter must fulfill a request to 
earn the NPC’s favor .
 ◆ Fighting or using force.  Fights will feel less 
like a natural part of the narrative, since there is no 
Storyteller making decisions for the NPCs. With a 
couple of adjustments, it is still possible to generate 
interesting situations. The Player must roll or decide 
a result from the Opponents’ Strategy Table and 
the Opponents’ Objective Table. These tables will 
provide a course of action and a purpose to the NPCs, 
allowing for more interesting situations than a mere 
exchange of blows.
 ◆ Noticing a clue or a detail. Unlike with the normal 
rules (which never include a Check to get information), 
in this solo game mode it is necessary for the Hunter 
to perform a Check to notice details or gather 
information. In most cases, the Question Table will 
provide something for your Hunter to discover in a 
Scene, but you may need a Check to better understand 
the situation. Hunters’ Gifts that provide information 
should be used to provide information to the Hunter’s 
benefit, without going through a Check.
 ◆ Overcoming an obstacle or facing a Threat.  As 
per the rules, any situation with a risk of failure and that 
does not directly involve an NPC must be formalized 
into a Threat. The Player can create one or roll on the 
Danger Table.
Story Tables
The Story Tables cover the most varied situations and 
events. While setting up a Scene, it is always good to 
keep the tables close at hand so you can determine any 
unexpected elements on the fly.
Getting to the epilogue
After experiencing at least 3 Keywords, the story can move 
towards its conclusion. The sum of the 3 Keywords allows 
the Player to set a final Scene that solves the Scenario’s 
objective. If some issues are still unsolved, they can all be 
used during the epilogue.

43
Question Table
Whenever you need an answer to determine how the world 
around you behaves, set up a question that you can 
answer with a Yes or No. Roll 2 dice and follow the 
result.
2: No, and...
3-4: No.
5-6: No, but...
7: Maybe (The first time this is rolled, choose No, but... or 
Yes, but... In the course of a single Scene, each time this 
happens, you have to choose the opposite result to the 
previous one).
8-9: Y es, but...
10-11: Y es.
12: Y es, and...
Additional positive elements
Notice a hidden detail / Drop something / Uncover something 
about yourself / Find a secret / Somebody arrives / Change 
perspective / A new path is revealed / Introduce a new NPC 
/ Introduce a new place / Gain time / Get a future advantage 
/ Break a promise / Reveal a Gift / Reveal a Treasure / It is 
linked to a Dark Presence / Someone is watching the scene / 
They are willing to accept a deal / Gain a resource.
Negative additional elements
Reveal something about yourself / Reinforcements are coming 
/ Someone is coming / Something remains unsolved / Change 
perspective / Introduce a new NPC / Introduce a new place / 
Waste time / A guarantee is needed / Make a promise / Reveal 
a Gift / Reveal a Treasure / It is linked to a Dark Presence / 
You went back on your trail / Someone is watching the scene 
/ They see you / They hear you / Lose resources.

44
Story Tables
The first four tables are the basis on which to create and set 
the Scenes. All the other tables are to be considered optional. 
If the story seems to come to a halt during the Session, or 
you are up a blind alley, you can roll the dice for each of the 
tables and try to integrate the result with the information 
already in place. This way, you will get unexpected or 
inspirational elements, or sense of the situation that could 
give new life to the story (a new NPC appears and opposes 
the Hunter, an old friend runs to help, etc.)
Story Table 1 - Initial Situation
Choose according to the plot or roll 1 die to determine 
which Scene is presented to the Hunter. If you rely entirely 
on chance, add +1 to the roll for each Scene you play until a 
Key Scene comes out, then reset the bonus count.
1-2 Agitated or dramatic Scene: Things get worse 
for the Hunter, something gets in the way and hinders 
them. Y ou can roll on the Danger Table or the Event 
Table to figure out what.
3 Unexpected Scene:  The situation is not as the 
Hunter expected. Y ou can roll on the Event Table.
4-5 Connection Scene:  The story follows its natural 
course in line with the previous Scenes, you can roll on 
the Table of Encounters  and the Table of Typical 
Situations.
6 Key Scene: The story follows its natural course and 
will introduce a Keyword. After collecting 3 Keywords, the 
next Scene can be the Epilogue. 
Story Table 2 - Place
Choose according to the plot or roll a die 2 times to 
determine the positive and negative characteristics of a 
location the Hunter visits.
URBAN PLACES
1: Secluded, away from prying eyes / A potential danger to 
the Hunter is lurking.
2: Rich in resources and benefits / Controlled by someone 
who decides everything.
3: A mystery is linked to the place or to someone who inhabi-
ts it / A Broken Tale lives in or uses this place.
4: Crowded, is home to parties or gatherings / Someone takes 
advantage of the confusion to commit illicit acts.
5: Fortified and defended against danger / Well guarded and 
secure, it is not easy to enter and exit without being seen.
6: Comfortable and optimal for rest / Very difficult not to 
leave tracks and traces of your presence.
WILD PLACES
1: Isolated and far from human presence / Natural hazards 
and predators.
2: The “home” of a Broken Tale / The territory of an entity 
that does not want intruders.
3: No-man’s land, where anyone can walk around without 
worry / A crucible of magical power.
4: Devastated by a menace / Intricate, where one can easily 
get lost.

45
5: Abandoned and lost / Point of passage where it is easy to 
run into a Threat.
6: Unknown and full of secrets / Full of traps.
Story Table 3 - List of NPCs
Y ou can roll several times on the tables to generate details 
of an NPC and formalize a Descriptor. For Minor NPCs, it 
is not advisable to waste too much time on the details, apart 
from choosing a Quality and an Attitude, which are the 
most useful pieces of    information to include in the story. 
More than in other cases, you will have to thoroughly 
evaluate what is useful to establish before rolling to keep 
the narrative consistent.
DISTINCTIVE ELEMENT
Roll one or more dice to determine distinctive elements in 
the various categories of an NPC.
Physique - Hair - Eyes - Face - Clothing 
1: Tall / Thick hair / Tired eyes / Well-kept teeth / Poor and 
worn appearance.
2: Small / Bald / Sincere Eyes / Dirty and broken teeth / A 
symbol of belonging.
3: Overweight / Gray hair / Inquiring eyes / Well-kept beard 
and mustache / A religious jewel.
4: Underweight / Strange hairstyle / Sad eyes / A big scar / 
Clothes typical of a trade.
5: Handicapped / Hair braids / Cynical eyes / Scent of oil and 
essences / Fine clothes.
6: Trained / Dirty and ruffled hair / Lustful eyes / Scent of 
dirt and sweat / Armed. 
Defects - Voice - Benefits - Attitude - Quality
1: Nervous tics / Calm voice / Multilingual / Available to tho-
se in need / Skilled in exploiting weaknesses.
2: Fearful / Thunderous voice / Master of an art / Busy sol-
ving a problem / Stubborn and ready to fight.
3: Poor memory / Whispering voice / Affinity to a Broken 
Tale / Desperately searching for something / Benefit derived 
from knowledge. 
4: Shy / Strong voice / Master of a particular weapon / Ap-
prehended by someone / A useful resource. 
5: Rude / Hoarse, coughing voice / Knows the area / Seeking 
a challenge / Protected by someone who matters.
6: Greedy / Melodious voice / Loved by all / Trusted / Strug-
gling to handle a situation / Accurate and able to notice de-
tails.
PROFILE
If necessary, you can generate an NPC profile.
1-2 Weak: OL Easy 3 – 1 Wound – 0/1 Gift
3-5 Capable: OL Medium 5 – 1 to 3 Wounds – 1/2 Gifts
6 Paragon: OL Difficult 7 – 3 to 6 Wounds – 2 Gifts

46
AGENDA
It is not necessary for every NPC to have an Agenda, simply 
because there is no Storyteller to manage it. However, 
it may be useful to have a general idea of what the most 
important NPCs would like to achieve, in relation to the 
Hunter’s objective. Roll a die:
1: The NPC’s Agenda is in total contrast with that of the Hun-
ter, and achieving one excludes the other. The NPC can be 
considered a main opponent of the Scenario and will be wil-
ling to do anything to stop the Hunter.
2-3: The NPC’s Agenda is opposite to the Hunter’s, but only 
due to a connection to other NPCs and situations. The NPC 
hinders the Hunter, but they do so in the name of the bond 
they hold and not out of direct hatred for the Hunter. 
4-5: The NPC’s Agenda intersects with that of the Hunter. 
The NPC may be an ally or an enemy, in relation to what 
happens/what they crave for themselves. The objective of the 
NPC is connected to the story of the Hunter and is influenced 
by the decisions of the latter.
6: The NPC’s Agenda is aligned with that of the Hunter. The 
NPC will be a genuine ally, to the best of their ability.
Story Table 4 - Clear information for 
the Hunter
To get useful information to progress in the story, you often 
need a Check. Y ou can roll on this table to understand 
where the information is and who holds it.
1: The information is in the hands of an NPC who intends to 
share it at a high price.
2: The information is contained in something (a tome or in 
possession of an NPC) that anyone can reach. First come, first 
served.
3: The information is protected by several Threats.
4: The information is in the hands of an NPC willing to pro-
vide it and unable to defend themselves from threats.
5: The information is hidden and should be sought with time 
and attention.
6: The information can be accessed by killing a very dange-
rous creature/NPC.
Once the information is obtained, it will often open the way 
to the next Scene. Sometimes it will instead act as a bridge 
to the resolution of the Scene itself.
As an optional rule, you can add unpredictability with false 
information. 
When you find a new piece of information, you don’t 
always know if it’s accurate until you follow where it leads. 
To provide that ambiguity in a solo game, you can use a 
die, a deck of cards, or another tool. Roll a die, then hide 
the result without looking. Alternatively, draw a card from 
the deck and don’t look at it. When you follow up on the 
information in the next Scene, you can decide that an 
even roll means it was true, while an odd roll means the 
information was false, or vice versa. Alternatively, a red 
suited card could mean correct information while a black 
suit means it was a false lead. Y ou can then reveal the result 
(flipping the card over or uncovering the die) to discover if 
the information was true or false. If it was true, the Scene 
continues. If the information was false, you can introduce 
an unexpected element or roll on the Event Table.

47
Additional Tables
All the tables below are not a must for the construction of 
the story but can provide interesting ideas to enrich each 
situation.
Danger Table
Dangers are NPC and Threats that will try to harm the 
Hunter.
1: A group of Minor NPCs on mission on behalf of an NPC / 
A natural phenomenon that puts everyone in danger.
2: A group of Minor NPCs led by a Main NPC / A skillfully 
set trap.
3: A Dark Presence / A crowd out of control.
4: A Hunter with opposite or intersecting objectives / One or 
more dangerous animals.
5: A Main NPC prepared to deal with the Hunter / A threat 
based on a natural element (e.g., a fire).
6: A Main Broken Tale NPC / Someone (to whom the Hunter 
is bound) whose life is at stake. 
Event Table
Events are sudden incidents that overturn the status quo of 
the Scene.
1: A group of NPCs takes control of the situation by force.
2: The arrival of a new faction in the Scenario with a neutral 
Agenda.
3: A Dark Presence that crashes into the Scene, roll 1 die (1-3 
They seek revenge against the Hunter on behalf of someone 
else, 4-6 They are intrigued by the Hunter and their abilities).
4: An incident that attracts general attention.
5: An NPC that is eliminated in a mysterious way, roll 1 die 
(1-3 an allied NPC, 4-6 an opponent NPC).
6: A Broken Tale manifests itself for the first time.
Typical Situations Table
Situations are great introductions to a Scene with which 
to integrate the Initial Situation. It is possible to generate a 
Scene opening and a situation or place.
1: Someone on the run / A village festival.
2: A request for help / A mass.
3: A tournament / A market day.
4: A search / A night round.
5: A looming threat/ A place considered cursed.
6: A summons / At sunset.

48
Encounter Table
The encounters are great for introducing new NPCs into 
the story. Y ou can determine who you meet and a potential 
situation that affects them.
1: A Broken Tale / Wounded.
2: An old person / In pursuit.
3: A person / Fleeing.
4: A young person / In danger.
5: A couple / On a mission.
6: A group / Deceased recently or long ago.
Opponent’s Strategy Table
During a fight, you can change up the NPCs’ actions 
by rolling on this table. The first result is for individual 
opponents, the second for groups, and the third for the 
Broken Tales. During a fight, only apply each result once, 
rerolling the die if necessary.
1: They try to exploit the environment to attack / Exploit 
numerical superiority / They reveal a new Gift and use it 
against the Hunter.
2: They try to take advantage of the environment to defend 
themselves / Only one engages the Hunter, while the others 
block every escape / They go straight for the kill.
3: They try to hit an ally of the Hunter / They threaten so-
mething the Hunter cares about to distract them, while some 
companions attack / They use a Gift.
4: They pretend to be struggling, then counterattack / They 
surround the Hunter / They call for an ally. 
5: They call for one or more underlings / They call for their 
leader / They try to understand the Hunter’s intentions while 
fighting them.
6: They use a Gift / They attack all together without conside-
ring the risk / They move away to attack at a distance.
Complication Table
After an Interlude, roll twice on this table to find out 
what happened while the Hunter rested. Each result has a 
narrative effect and a possible mechanical effect.
1: An NPC, resource or situation that the Hunter cares about, 
ends up at the mercy of an opponent / What the Hunter ye-
arns for is used as bait to distract them and force them to 
perform Checks to keep it safe while the enemy acts.
2: The Hunter arrives late on the Scene, leaving time for their 
opponents to prepare / Add or introduce Minor NPCs as op-
ponents.
3: The enemy has time to devise a strategy / Give a new Gift  
to an opponent.
4: The place where the Hunter arrives turns out to be a trap / 
Delete one or more piece of the Hunter’s equipment. 
5: The enemy has learned something about the Hunter / In-
troduce something that hinders the use of one of the Hunter’s 
Gifts.
6: The enemy has become more powerful / Improves the pro-
file of an enemy by increasing the Opposition Level or their 
Wounds.

49
Discovery Table
Y ou can determine a finding or discovery and how it affects 
the situation.
1: A valuable object / Alter the status quo.
2: A Treasure of the Order / Procures wealth.
3: A building / Brings desolation.
4: A secret / Generates interest.
5: A document / Removes limitations of some kind (prohibi-
tions, laws, accusations).
6: A substance / Corrupts.
Traveling Table
If the Hunter covers long distances from one Scene to 
another, you can roll to see what happens along the way.
1: An enemy hired by a rival NPC intercepts the Hunter to 
stop them.
2: A group of NPCs in a perilous situation they can’t cope 
with.
3: Lost along the way, the Hunter comes to a mysterious pla-
ce that hides something.
4: A Dark Presence crosses the path of the Hunter.
5: The journey continues smoothly.
6: The Hunter arrives early, gaining time to plan and prepare 
before enemies and troubles arise.
In Media Res Table
If there are no particular ideas on how to set up a Scene it 
is possible to start it “in media res, ” meaning “in the middle 
of things”: the Hunter is already engaged with something 
and what happened before will be determined by the tables. 
This is a great way to create twists and turns in the story.
1: The Hunter is hunted by an enemy or a multitude of thre-
ats. 
2: The Hunter is trapped in a place by a threat or ended up 
there by mistake.
3: The Hunter is lost and far from what was their original 
destination, thrown into a completely new situation.
4: The Hunter is hiding while watching some NPC plot so-
mething.
5: The Hunter is interviewing an important person who can 
help them.
6: The Hunter is at the gates of a mysterious place full of 
dangers.

51
The Village is a variant of the game that will put 
the Players in the shoes of ordinary people who have 
crossed paths with the Broken Tales. Scarred by their 
dark encounter, regular people can no longer live a normal 
existence. Instead, they fall into a spiral of confusion, 
driven by a fascination with the macabre to investigate the 
mysteries of Broken Europe.
This mode is designed for a game with a strong OSR (Old 
School Renaissance) atmosphere, a game philosophy 
movement that highlights groups of less heroic protagonists, 
often desperately poor, who try to survive in a dangerous 
and hostile world. The protagonists will find themselves 
investigating the same Scenarios as the Hunters of the 
Order but, because their abilities are not the same as the 
Hunters’ , their experiences will be profoundly different. 
Rule Variations
The Village is played exactly like a normal Broken Tales 
Session, but with some tweaks both in the organization of 
the game and its rules.
A reason to investigate the 
shadows of the Broken Tales
Based on the chosen Scenario, the Storyteller decides why 
a group of Villagers gets involved in the investigation. If 
the idea is to play a continuous Campaign, the group of 
Villagers could be formed at the request of some authority 
or a Hunter. Given the shortage of Hunters of the Order, 
someone else has to step in. In the preparation phase, the 
Storyteller will have to consider rewriting the background 
of the Scenario to adapt it to the Villagers.
The Horror of the Broken Tales
The Villagers’ abilities are nothing like those of the Hunters 
of the Order. Any Position or Defense Check against a NPC 
or Threat that is clearly linked to the Broken Tales will have 
its Opposition Level increased by 1. This disadvantage can 
be eliminated in future Checks as soon as a Villager is able 
The Village

52
to inflict 1 Wound to whatever is threatening them, or 
overcome it entirely. This simple change significantly shifts 
the level of difficulty of each Check, putting the Villagers 
in a constant state of insecurity. The application of this rule 
must be strongly anchored to what happens during the 
Scenario. The Storyteller can decide when not to apply it. 
If, for example, the Villagers face a creature for the second 
time, it is plausible that its sight no longer rouses such 
horror in the Villagers as to affect their actions.
Mortality
When a Villager suffers their last Wound, they will die or, 
more rarely, be put aside for the rest of the Scenario. The 
Player can immediately create a new Villager by choosing 
them from a Bond on the Sheet, or by creating them from 
scratch if the Scenario offers interesting ideas from among 
the NPCs they have encountered. The Storyteller will 
arrange for the new Villager to enter the Scene as soon as 
possible.
Resting and experience
Villagers can ask for Interludes as described on page 11 of 
the Broken Tales core book. 
In the case of a Campaign, as with Hunters, it isn’t possible 
to advance a Descriptor into a Specialization during 
Scenario Interludes.
During the Scenario’s end Interlude, they recover every 
Wound suffered and every Soma point spent. Experience 
can be spent on:
 ◆ Acquiring a new Gift: (a Villager can never have more 
than 3 Gifts) 5 XP .
 ◆ Evolve a Descriptor into a Specialization: Granting 
1 Additional Success once per Scene, when the 
Descriptor is used. 5 XP .
 ◆ Obtain additional Soma for the following Scenario: 
1 point per XP spent. Soma obtained in this way can 
exceed the Villager’s normal maximum threshold.

53
Roll Age Wounds Gifts Soma Possessions
1 Child (up to 16 years) 1 1 6 +1
2-3 Y oung adult (up to 22 years) 2 2 4 +2
4-5 Adult (up to 42 years) 2 2 3 +3
6 Elderly (over 42 years) 1 2 5 +4
Creation of 
the Villager
The creation of the Villager is different 
from that of the Hunters and follows a 
series of steps to establish the Villager’s 
past and abilities.
2 - Role
Roll two dice to create one of the combinations available 
from page 60, determining the Role assigned to 
the Villager. Each Role provides guidance for creating 
Descriptors, a specific Gift that can be selected, a list of 
Possessions, and a Bond.
Optional rule: To give more choice to the Players, you can 
have them roll twice and choose between the two results.
3 - Descriptors
Each Role provides a list of 3 possible options for initial 
contact with the Broken Tales. These represent an event in 
the past of the Villager that has led them to the fringes of 
society, in search of what is hiding in the darkness. Each 
Player rolls a die to determine the past event (or, as an 
optional rule, chooses the one that inspires them the most) 
and marks it on the Sheet along with the Role Keywords. 
As a last option, each Player may decide to roll on the 
Distinguishing Features table one or more times.
At this point, it is necessary to create the 2 Descriptors that 
characterize the Villager:
 ◆ The first Descriptor must concern the past of the 
Villager, which must always contain elements of 
darkness and a connection with the Broken Tales. The 
Villager is an outcast, or, at least, someone who has 
missed their shot at having a normal life. 
 ◆ The second Descriptor must be concerned with how 
the Villager fights the Broken Tales and what abilities 
they can rely on in their fight against darkness. This 
Descriptor can be created by mutual agreement 
between the Players so that a motivation that keeps the 
group of Villagers together is outlined.
1 - Age
Roll a die to decide the Villager’s Age. Their age group will 
determine how many Wounds they can endure, their Soma 
point pool, and their number of Gifts and Possessions.

54
4 - Bond
Each Role provides a list of three possible Bonds that 
represent an NPC connected in some way to the Villager 
who will worry about their fate. In game, if the Villager dies 
or is knocked out for the rest of the Scenario, the Player 
controlling them will be able to immediately create a new 
Villager using one of the Bonds from a Sheet, either theirs 
or another Player’s. This will immediately provide a new 
protagonist with a valid and reasonable connection to the 
Scenario. 
During the Session, it’s possible to create additional Bonds 
with the NPCs encountered. The Storyteller has the final say 
on whether or not to create a Bond based on the purpose of 
the NPC in the story.
Note: During the Session, it may happen that a Villager 
forges a Bond with a Hunter of the Order. If this happens 
and the Villager is eliminated, the Player can select the 
Hunter and bring them into play. In the case of a Campaign, 
the Hunter will exit the game at the end of the Scenario and 
the Player will have to create a new Villager afterward.
Some suggestions for group motivation: At the service of an 
agent of the Church / At the service of a Hunter of the Order 
/ A company that solves mysteries for reward / A group of 
devotees, sponsored by an anonymous client who presents 
mysterious cases to be solved / At the service of a wealthy 
nobleman who wants to manage mysteries without drawing 
the attention of the Order.
Each Descriptor must have its own Drawback, as usual. 
For the creation of the Descriptors, each Player can use as 
inspiration the Keywords present in the Role, their contact 
with the Broken Tales, any Distinguishing Features, and the 
name of the Role itself which, in fact, gives a clear idea of 
what the Villager does for a living.

and decide which one to keep.

The available equipment will be much more important for 
the Villagers than for Hunters. Their possessions confer 
useful narrative possibilities to aid in Checks. They can also 
supplement the use of resources such as Wounds and Soma, 
which are generally very limited. Each Role provides a list 
of possessions. The Player will add to those a number of 
Possessions equal to the bonus in the Age Table, generating 
them from the Table of Possessions.

6 - Gifts
Each Villager obtains the number of Gifts indicated in 
the Age Table. Gifts can be chosen from the generic list in 
addition to the exclusive Gift conferred by the Villager’s 
Role. 
GENERIC GIFTS
Accustomed to Horror:  Y ou have already fought the 
horrors that hide in the shadows several times. Ignore the 
penalties of the Horror of the Broken Tales rule. Reduce 
your Soma cap by 1.
Animal Friend: An animal is faithful to you and follows 
you wherever you go. Y our Animal Friend is a Minor NPC 
under the control of the Storyteller, but it does its best to 
help you. Create a Descriptor to characterize it.
Die Hard: It’s not easy to knock you out. Once per Scenario, 
you can return at the end of a Scene after you suffered your 
last Wound. Tell everyone how you survived and recovered 
from your Wound.
Lucky: Y ou’ve done well more than once thanks to your 
incredible luck. Once per Scene, when you don’t have any 
Descriptor that fits the action you are doing, gain 3 Base 
Successes.
Old Friendships:  When you arrive in a new place, 
you can spend 1 Soma to create a Bond with an NPC (in 
agreement with the Storyteller) that your Villager knows 
and with whom you have some kind of relationship. The 
nature of the Bond is decided by the Storyteller, or chosen 
by you if you pay 1 additional Soma.
One-Time Remedies: Y ou know how to treat wounds 
and ailments with simple means. Once per Scene, if you 
take enough time to treat a wound, roll 1 die. 1-2: Y ou have 
the wrong remedy and nothing happens. 3-4: Y ou do not 
cure the problem, but you lift the spirits of your patient, 
who recovers 1 Soma. 5-6: Y ou got it right and cured 1 of 
your patient’s Wounds. Once per Scenario, you can decide 
the outcome without rolling the dice.
Savings: Y ou have saved up a decent amount and use it 
to the fullest. Once per Scene you can take out or procure 
an object you need, simply by spending a portion of your 
resources. Get 2 additional Possessions.
Sixth Sense: Y ou can easily sense when the situation is 
going south. Receive 2 Additional Successes for the first 
Defense Check you make against a sudden threat.
Status: Y ou are related to some important organization, 
such as the Papacy or a noble family. Once per Scene, you 
can appeal to your connections to receive 1 Additional 
Success when you deal with or ask for favors from high-
ranking people. Once per Scene, you gain 1 Additional 
Success when you threaten or manipulate low-ranking 
people.
Veteran: Y ou have many years of experience on your 
shoulders. Once per Scene, you can raise the Outcome of 
an action by one rank. Y ou can also turn a Failure into an 
Outcome with a Cost, but you must spend 1 Soma to do so.

57
Tips for the 
Storyteller
A game in Village mode varies considerably in tones and 
atmospheres compared to one involving the Hunters of 
the Order, who can guide the situation and turn the tide of 
each story with their skills. Villagers are the exact opposite: 
ordinary people who have to endure the power of the Broken 
Tales without really having the skills. Given the total reversal 
of perspective, here is a list of useful tips to manage the 
Session.
Use a Check only when you really need it
Villagers, compared to a Hunter, are definitely at a 
disadvantage when it comes to Position and Defense Checks 
because they will face increased Opposition Levels most of the 
time. It is good to point out to the Players that changing the 
narration can lead to avoiding a Check, and that studying 
a situation might reduce the opponent’s Opposition 
Level by 1 precious point. They don’t want to be heroes 
and fewer Checks mean a smaller chance of getting 
hurt. However, the Storyteller must not hesitate to 
call for a Position or Defense Check when necessary. 
They must strike mercilessly even using Wounds as 
possible Costs. A Scenario played by Villagers must 
always maintain a sense of hardship and insecurity.
Intelligence and preparation
Clever use of the Villagers’ Possessions must be 
rewarded. Each Check is a situation of 
potential danger, which is why it’s 
a good habit to always pay attention to the “alternative” 
plans of the Villagers. It’s rarely a good idea to face the most 
dangerous situations head-on. A skirmish for a group of 
Hunters could become a genuine slaughter for the Villagers. 
Avoiding a problem often means solving it later, when you 
have brought resources to spend.
Shrouding all in darkness
In this mode, the perception of the Broken Tales is very 
different from the usual. The Hunters are aware and prepared 
for the dark part of the world, while the Villagers have only 
tasted a small sample of it in their past. Giving a different 
vibe to the descriptions can significantly vary the overall 
mood of the Session. A group of hungry werewolves is 
simply a problem to be managed for a group of experienced 
Hunters. To Villagers, they would be a nightmare made flesh. 
The Horror of the Broken Tales rule serves to highlight this 
aspect, which can be reinforced by the Storyteller giving 
weight to descriptions and dwelling more on the monstrous 
aspects of the Broken Tales and how different they are 
from what is conceived as normality.
Hunters as NPCs
In addition to rewriting the background of the 
Scenario to suit Villagers, a good option may be 
to drop in one or more Hunters as NPCs. Even 
if they have other things to do, Villagers may 
ask the Hunter for help at certain times in the 
Scenario and even create a Bond of some kind. 
The presence of a Hunter in the Scenario can 
act as an anchor of salvation for a distressed 
group of Villagers.

LIST OF ROLES

among people, while the 
wilderness feels like a 
much nicer place. Your 
strange appearance leads 
others to distrust you in a 
way that makes you feel 
not completely human. 
In the Broken Tales, you 
may be looking for that 
part of you that you can’t 
find among ordinary people. 
Keywords: Forest Spirit / Animal Friends / Unearthly 
appearance / Sixth Sense / Mutation / Unnatural Charm / 
Fleeing / House in the Woods / Fairy Blood.
Possessions: A stick that I found in the woods / a large 
cloak with which to hide from the sight of others / stones with 
strange engravings that I found by chance / a wooden mask 
that a poacher gave me / a small animal that follows me as a 
trusted companion.
Gift - Fae Blood:  Y our body and appearance have some 
special qualities to them. Create a Descriptor that represen-
ts your mutation, such as Luminescent Eyes or Pointy Ears. 
Non-human creatures (including animals) never consider 
you a threat and will be well disposed towards you, not 
targeting you for their actions if you do not threaten them 
first. Y ou can communicate crudely with animals, plants, 
and non-human creatures.

74
YOUR CONTACT WITH THE BROKEN TALES
1-2: A Hunter of the Order has discovered you and clearly 
told you not to be caught again if you do not want your life 
to take a bad turn.
3-4: Strange creatures live in the forest, but they do not 
reveal themselves to you, perhaps because they fear your 
human part. 
5-6: Y ou have seen a group of Hunters of the Order capture 
and kill someone like you. They called them fae offspring.
YOUR BOND
1-2: A weird artist who loves to use you as a model for their 
paintings (Mad Artist).
3-4: A young hunter with whom you have become friends 
(Poacher).
5-6: A Hunter of the Order who kept an eye on you. 
Special: With this result, you can bring into play and use a 
Hunter of the Order for the rest of the Scenario.
