any reason, though the suspicion that they are a place of 
dark mysteries has long been held by Hunters. 
Possible situations: 1-2: During full moon nights, strange 
rituals are performed in the Farnese Gardens. The Order has 
always frowned upon the situation, but the strange priests 
wearing wolf masks are protected by a powerful merchant 
association in the city. 3-4: Word has spread among the 
city’s youth of a mysterious cult that holds parties and eve-
nings of dancing, good food, and wine in the Farnese Gar-
dens. 5-6: A Dark Presence, hunted by the Order, has taken 
refuge in the ranks of the Bacchanalian Priests.
5: The Tiber Island
On Tiber Island, in the middle of the Tiber River, stands an 
ancient temple, abandoned since Roman times, that is said 
to be home to mysterious creatures. 
Possible situations: 1-2: The ancient temple has become a 
refuge for a Dark Presence sought by the Order. 3-4: Hid-
den in the old temple is a Treasure of the Order, long sought 
by many Hunters. A Dark Presence lives in the temple and 
does not want visitors. 5-6: The old temple is actually a 
magical door connected to the many Devil’s Bridges scat-
tered throughout Broken Europe. The key to open the door 
is in the hands of a Dark Presence who knows its value and 
will sell it for a high price. 
6: The Colosseum
Rome’s most famous site hides many secrets, factions, and 
misdeeds in the shadow of the great arena.
Possible Situations: 1-2: An underground fight club run by 
a Hunter of the Order forces some of the NPCs defeated in 
past Scenarios to fight each other to entertain the rich peo-
ple of the city. The Hunter has a secret deal with the Order’s 
prison guards and picks up the prisoners one night a week 
to fight. 3-4: A group of conspirators gathers around the 
Colosseum to make an attempt on the Pope’s life. The con-
spirators are led by a Dark Presence hired by the One Who 
Plots in the Shadows. 5-6: Ancient inscriptions on the walls 
of the Colosseum point the way to an ancient Treasure of 
the Order. Translating them, however, requires the inter -
vention of a Dark Presence.

70
Leaving Rome
When the Hunters leave Rome, each member of the party 
must roll 1 die, plus 1 additional die for each Unexpect-
ed Event Token accumulated. Those rolling at least a 6 will 
encounter an Unexpected Event, which the Storyteller will 
either roll at random or choose from the list that follows.
Unexpected Events 
1: The Trial
A Dark Presence is put on trial by the Order, and the Hunt-
er is called upon to serve as judge. The Storyteller sets up 
a trial Scene in which the Hunter is the judge. One Hunter 
NPC will be the prosecution and another Hunter NPC will 
be the defense. If the Dark Presence is known to the Hunter 
group, the roles of prosecution and defense can be assigned 
to other Hunters controlled by a Player. 
The Storyteller presents the crime of which the Dark Pres-
ence is guilty, and then the Scene is played out, with the 
prosecution and defense alternating in presenting their 
cases. The judge may ask any questions they wish and will 
ultimately rule on innocence or guilt. However it goes, the 
judge will displease one side and satisfy the other. These are 
good hooks for the Storyteller to use to build relationships 
with the NPCs involved. 
2: The Revenge
An NPC from a previously played Scenario returns to take 
revenge on the Hunter. The Storyteller must introduce a 
Scene in which the Hunter is ambushed by the NPC.
3: An Old Acquaintance
The Hunter encounters an old acquaintance, and the Sto-
ryteller must decide whether this NPC will be a friend or 
an antagonist for the Hunter. It will be up to the Hunter to 
decide how to deal with this NPC. 
4: On the Trail
An Opponent Dark Presence is on the Hunter’s trail. The 
Storyteller selects the Dark Presence and creates an Agenda 
opposing the Hunter. The Dark Presence will appear as an 
enemy in the next Scenario.
5: The Lost Object
The Hunter realizes too late that they have left one of their 
pieces of equipment in one of the Order’s locations. They 
can only retrieve it when they next return to Rome. The 
Player chooses which piece of equipment to lose.
6: The Escort 
The Hunter is summoned for a special assignment: to es-
cort a young recruit of the Order to the place the Hunters 
have designated as their next destination. The person being 
escorted is very important to the delicate political balance 
that the Papacy seeks to maintain, so the Hunter must en-
sure that the journey is as safe as possible. The Storyteller 
creates a Minor NPC who will be the subject to be escort-
ed. One or more traits leading the NPC to get into trouble 
should be highlighted in the Descriptor and their Agenda. 
These reasons can be a rebellious character, an irrepressible 
addiction, or any other motivation.

NEMESI
71
Once upon a time...
Once upon a time, there was a mad writer who created his 
own reality to escape a world that could not understand him. 
In his strange creation, good and evil had swapped places: 
villains were heroes, and heroes indulged in the worst sort of 
wickedness.
The One Who Plots in the Shadows, as the writer called 
himself, weaved the threads of all the characters’ destinies, 
creating stories that were dark and bloody, but where justice 
ultimately triumphed. But in the process, his own hands were 
left dripping with blood.
As the years passed, the new world was filled with life, pop-
ulating itself with dark and bizarre characters created by the 
twisted mind of the One Who Plots in the Shadows.
Then it happened: the villains he had given a chance at re-
demption joined the Order, an organized group serving man-
kind. Investigation after investigation they sought to unravel 
the great deception, to reveal the identity of the One Who 
Plots in the Shadows himself.
And, after the abandonment of childhood, after the rejection 
and mockery of adulthood, a new emotion found its 
way into the heart of the One Who Plots in the Shadows: 
disappointment that his children didn’t recognize all he 
had done for them.
Once again, he had dedicated his life to someone, and once 
again, he had been repaid with betrayal. First, the readers 
who mocked his stories, and now the Hunters who had be-
come evil in his vision of the world.
Desperate and in pain, the One Who Plots in the Shadows 
wept inky tears that stained his hands until they turned black 
as night.
And at the height of his despair, he realized what he had to 
do. 
If no one would be on his side, if no one could understand 
him, he would create his own companions: a group of loyal 
allies united by the same disappointment that had broken 
his heart. 
Each would be a Nemesis for the Hunters, extensions of his 
work so that the whole world would remain locked forever in 
his perverted vision. 
In the emptiness of his vast mansion, by the light of a few 
faint candles, the One Who Plots in the Shadows laughed out 
loud as he had not in a long time. All he had to do was what 
he was born to do: create stories, create characters, create 
someone who could understand him.
And as he laughed, he began to write, and the more the 
words took shape, the more each Nemesis formed from the 
ink-stained pages.
And as he wrote, tears began to fall. 
Bitter, cold tears. Tears of loneliness.
Nemesis

72
The Nemeses
Nemeses are a new category of NPC that can be 
introduced in the Broken Tales Campaign or in a single 
Scenario to increase its difficulty. Nemeses are special 
Dark Presences, always linked to the One Who Plots in 
the Shadows, ready to unfold his mad plans to control 
Broken Europe.
The Nemeses will add depth to the world of Broken Tales, 
introducing a group of recurring enemies that will creep 
into the Hunters’ stories. Unified by their hatred for the 
Hunters of the Order, a Nemesis is also a good way to 
continue a Broken Tales Campaign after the confronta-
tion with the One Who Plots in the Shadows, as they are 
extremely powerful NPCs and have all the makings of the 
Hunters’ new “epic enemies. ” 
Nemesis creation follows the pattern of Dark Presences 
but with some significant differences.  
Step 1 Bring the character to their Breaking 
Point
Nemeses follows the method for creating Dark Presenc-
es presented on page 42 of the Broken Tales Core Book, 
the only difference being the Nemesis’ motive for acting 
against the Order. In breaking their story, we must con-
sider how to include an event that deeply disappointed the 
Nemesis and caused them to hate the world and every-
thing the Hunters protect.
The five Nemeses presented in this chapter are good ex-
amples of what we mean.
Step 2 Assign the characteristics
All Nemeses have a Descriptor that outlines their primary 
abilities, which should be on par with Dark Presences. Since 
these are characters created with the specific intention of be-
ing champions of the One Who Plots in the Shadows, the 
composition of the Descriptor can be very powerful and 
more effective than that of normal NPCs.
A Nemesis must have a minimum of 4 Wounds and a max-
imum of 6. On average, 5 Wounds is best to give the Neme-
sis the proper narrative resilience to the actions of an entire 
group of Hunters. 
The Opposition Level of a Nemesis is always Medium 5.  It 
should be noted that thanks to the Nemesis Gift (common to 
all Nemeses), the Opposition Level against the Hunter whom 
the Nemesis has marked as a sworn enemy increases to Hard 
7. This dynamic is important to encourage teamwork among 
the Hunters, as it makes it easier for that Hunter’s compan-
ions to succeed in Checks against the Nemesis.

73
Step 3 Create the Gifts
Each Nemesis possesses three Gifts:
 ◆ The first Gift represents their abilities, powers, or special 
talents. Follow the Core Book rules for this Gift.
 ◆ The second Gift is always called “Nemesis, ” and is the 
same for all Nemeses. This Gift confers a number of spe-
cial powers that the Nemesis can use as an NPC of great 
power and ability.
 ◆ The third Gift is always the “Investiture” , a powerful spe-
cial item given to the Nemesis by the One Who Plots in 
the Shadows to fight the Hunters.
Gift - Nemesis: When the Nemesis enters the game, they 
choose a Hunter as their sworn enemy. Against them, the 
Nemesis is always considered OL Hard 7. The Nemesis has 3 
Nemesis Tokens, which can be spent during the Scenario to 
activate one of the following effects. Each effect can only be 
used once per Scenario. These Tokens do not regenerate at 
any point. 
1. Reinforcements: New Nemesis Minions will appear in 
the Scene, overcoming any obstacle. 2 Minions per Hunter 
will appear in the Scene.
2. Hostage: The Nemesis reveals that they are holding hos-
tage an NPC that the Hunters encountered during the cur -
rent Scenario or a past Scenario. The NPC is being held cap-
tive by some of the Nemesis’ Minions at one of the Places in 
the current Scenario.    
3. Sacrifice: When a Hunter targets the Nemesis with one 
of their Gifts, a Minion makes an extreme gesture to save 
their master or mistress’s life. Eliminate the NPC to nullify 
the effect of the Gift.
4. Trap: The Scene changes drastically because of a trap set 
by the Nemesis. “The floor collapses, ” “ A flood comes, ” or 
“The room is hit by a toxic cloud that spreads through the 
air” are all good examples of traps. The Hunters involved 
must make a Defense Check against the Nemesis’ OL to 
avoid falling into the trap. In addition to being in a new zone 
with different narrative conditions, each Hunter who fails the 
Check suffers 1 Penalty on their next action. After the trap is 
triggered, the Exchange is interrupted and a new one begins, 
with the Nemesis faction taking the first action. 
5. Concealed Truth:  The Nemesis knows some informa-
tion that is crucial to the Scenario or very important to one of 
the Hunters, and will try to use it as a bargaining chip to gain 
an escape route or safe passage.
6. You Won the Battle, not the War: The Nemesis leaves 
the Scene immediately. No one can prevent this escape, and 
it will be up to the Storyteller to stage it. Even if the Hunters 
see the Nemesis die before their eyes, they will find a way to 
escape. 
Gift - Investiture:  This Gift represents a powerful magi-
cal object that the Nemesis receives from the One Who Plots 
in the Shadows. The object is not a Treasure of the Order, 
nor does it need to be inspired by an object from a fairytale. 
Instead, it must represent an important characteristic of the 
Nemesis. All items tied to an Investiture Gift lose their power 
when the Nemesis is defeated: when the Nemesis is no longer 
a threat to the Hunters, the meaning of the investiture is lost, 
and so is the power that animates the object.

DELUSION
74
Step 4 Agenda
Each Nemesis must have 4 Agendas, one for each possible 
story phase in which they act. As with the common Agen-
das, these are a general indication of the Nemesis’ goals and 
must be updated based on the Hunters’ actions during the 
Scenarios. 
The Agendas for the five Nemeses presented in this guide 
can be used as guidelines for creating Agendas for other 
Nemeses.
The first Agenda represents why the Nemesis 
is interested in the Hunters
This is the initial Agenda of the Nemesis and is the most 
“passive” , meaning that it represents what the Nemesis does 
in Broken Europe when they are unaware of the Hunters’ 
actions. This Agenda establishes the focus of the Nemesis 
and what triggers their interest in the Hunters. The first 
Agenda is the main way to determine when the Nemesis 
comes into play.
Example: 
The first Agenda of the Relentless Beastslayer is: “Track down 
and eliminate any monster that threatens humanity. ” This 
Agenda can trigger in any Scenario where monsters are pres-
ent (including Hunters with monstrous traits!) and pique the 
interest of the Relentless Beastslayer, which will intervene to 
investigate and stop anything that he perceives as monstrous.
The use of this Agenda can be direct, with the Nemesis ap-
pearing during the adventure to oppose the Hunters (or even 
temporarily help them if it makes sense to accomplish the 
Nemesis’ goals). The use of the Agenda can also be indirect, 
with the Nemesis appearing earlier or later to influence loca-
tions and NPCs, or to gather information about the Hunters’ 
actions. 
The goal of the first Agenda is to introduce the Nemesis into 
the Scenario and show what makes them unique.
The second Agenda represents how the 
Nemesis helps the One Who Plots in the 
Shadows
This Agenda characterizes how the Nemesis operates be-
hind the scenes and how they use their resources to help 
the One Who Plots in the Shadows. This Agenda is respon-
sible for leaving traces of the Nemesis in the game world 
and introducing related resources and NPCs that can pro-
vide important information to the Hunters.
Example: 
One of the two items in the Wise Fairy’s second Agenda is 
to “Donate power at a dear price, always reminding us 
that good for its own sake does not exist. ” This Agenda can 
be implemented through NPCs connected to the Wise Fairy, 
who become more skilled and dangerous by receiving power 
from her. However, they will forever after be tied to the Fairy 
and may be forced to do her bidding.
The use of this Agenda can be direct, with the Nemesis sup-
porting other NPCs against the Hunters, or indirect, with 
the Nemesis approaching NPCs defeated by the Hunters 
after a Scenario to bring them into the ranks of their Min-
ions. 
The goal of the second Agenda is to leave traces of the Nem-
esis in Broken Europe for the Hunters to stumble upon and 
see the effects of their machinations.

75
The third Agenda represents how the 
Nemesis fights against the Hunters
This Agenda characterizes the overt actions of the Nemesis 
and outlines how they fight and confront the Hunters. This 
Agenda is meant to guide their actions in the Scene as they 
confront the Hunters.
Example: 
The two items in the third Agenda of the King of Crime are: 
“Use my resources and Minions to attack the Hunters 
without exposing myself on the front lines / Use deception 
and blackmail to corner the Hunters. ” These items outline 
a talent for manipulation, which the Nemesis can use when 
attempting to turn the Hunters against each other through 
deception and social power. 
This Agenda is always direct, meaning it requires the pres-
ence of the Nemesis in the Scenario or during Encounters 
and Events. 
The goal of the third Agenda is to provide the Storytell-
er with good guidance for pitting the Nemesis against the 
Hunters.   
The fourth Agenda represents the goal of 
the Nemesis after the One Who Plots in the 
Shadows falls
This Agenda sets out a long-term goal that the Nemesis will 
seek to achieve as they replace the One Who Plots in the 
Shadows, while trying to remain true to his vision: to create 
dark stories featuring the many characters created over the 
years by the mad writer. Once he’s out of business, the Nem-
esis will attempt to turn the tables by involving the Hunters 
and Dark Presences scattered throughout the world.
Example: 
The two items in the fourth Agenda of the Valiant Princess 
are to: “Unite the weak and marginalized to create a net-
work to help others / Accumulate wealth to gain power in 
society and impose more just laws and rules, disrupting 
secret societies like the Order. ” The items in this Agenda 
define a long-term goal of the Valiant Princess, who deeply 
hates those who are able to decide the fate of others due to 
their privileged status and will seek to fight everything she 
deems wrong. The Order included.
This Agenda acts first in an indirect way, as a driving force 
behind the events that will involve the Hunters, and only 
later in a direct way, as the Nemesis lashes out at their ene-
mies to achieve their goal. 
The purpose of the fourth Agenda is to introduce a new 
main plot that will continue the Hunters’ adventure after 
the final confrontation with the One Who Plots in the 
Shadows.

77
Step 5 Minions
Each Nemesis may have Minions, which are related NPCs 
who help the Nemesis fulfill their Agenda. Minions must be 
created using the rules for various types of NPCs presented 
in the Broken Tales Core Book on page 31, with the addition 
of an Agenda that defines why they are in the service of the 
Nemesis.
For convenience, it is useful to define Minions in two cate-
gories:
The Followers: Represented by NPCs and Dark Presences 
drawn from the background of the Nemesis. These charac-
ters have been present since the creation of the Nemesis and 
represent the relationships and social fabric in which the 
Nemesis moves. These Minions should be characterized by 
Agendas and Descriptors that represent their history with 
the Nemesis. 
The Minions must be created along with the Nemesis, al-
though it is possible to update the members of the group to 
be consistent with what happens in the Hunters’ adventures.
The New Recruits: These are all the NPCs and Dark Pres-
ences that come under the influence of the Nemesis during 
the course of the game. Their motivations will often be a re-
sult of the Hunter’s actions in their Scenario.
New Recruits must be created between Scenarios, reflecting 
on what has happened and looking for points of connection 
with the Nemesis.    
Step 6 Connections
This step is not mandatory, but preparing for it can be a tre-
mendous help to the Storyteller. It involves making a list of 
Keywords related to the Nemesis, in order to more easily 
note possible connections with the Scenarios and Scenes that 
will be played out. 
Another step is to identify the most thematic Scenarios for 
the Nemesis and, in accordance with the main storyline, 
sketch out a range of useful information about how the Nem-
esis might be grafted onto the events presented. This step 
may take a lot of work, but it will provide a very useful set of 
information and new gameplay hints that will be valuable to 
the Storyteller. At the same time, these clues should address 
both what the Nemesis can do during the Scenario and what 
attracts the Nemesis to the Campaign after the Hunters re-
solve the Scenario.
Example:
The Connections of the Relentless Beastslayer for the Scenario 
Bloodwhite’s Heart, available in The Broken Ones expansion, 
are: “Finding a way to cure Bianca of her condition / Help-
ing Svenn preserve the Karenmann family secret. ” Bianca 
has the traits of a monster, but she does not act like one: she 
has a pure heart and would never hurt anyone. When outlin-
ing Connections, we also incorporate a reference to the orig-
inal fairytale, where the Huntsman is ordered to kill Snow 
White, but cannot find the courage to do so. 
In memory of the young woman he killed in the past, the Re-
lentless Beastslayer wants to find a way to save Bianca from 
her monstrous condition. Connections tell us how he would 
act during the Scenario, but with some interpretation, they 
will also tell us what happens if the Nemesis comes to Lohr 
before or after the Hunters.

78
If he arrives first: He could provide Svenn with resources and 
Followers to help him keep Bianca’s secret. He could even wait 
for the Hunters to arrive, and once they have the upper hand 
over the Hessian Mercenaries, he could kidnap Bianca and 
take her to safety. Finally, he could lurk by the river to am-
bush the Hunters before they reach the mine.
If he comes later: He could hire Svenn as a Minion and learn 
everything the man knows about the Hunters. The same 
strategy could apply to the Hessian Mercenaries. He could 
also track down Bianca and snatch her from the clutches of 
the Order. Finally, he could ambush the Hunters on one of 
their journeys (Event) to punish those who harmed Bianca. 
Each of the five Nemeses presented in Lost Stories has an 
extensive list of Connections that make their usefulness 
very clear. 
Introducing Nemeses into the game
Nemeses are not simply NPCs to be added as opposition to 
the Hunters, there are already Opponent Dark Presences 
NPCs for that. Each Nemesis will have a consistent impact 
on the Campaign or Scenario. It is a good idea to introduce 
the Nemesis gradually, starting with simple clues and small 
events that will evolve into more or less direct actions. The 
Nemesis Agendas, especially the first two, serve as a long-
term guide to the Nemesis’ behavior.    
Some tips to ease the introduction of a Nemesis:
 ◆ The first Agenda of each Nemesis gives important clues 
as to what the Nemesis is interested in. Linking it to the 
Scenarios already played gives consistency to their ap-
pearance in the game. It is better to delay the arrival of a 
Nemesis in order to integrate it well into the Campaign 
plot, rather than to drop them in as an opponent who has 
no real reason to resent the Hunters.
 ◆ Each Nemesis will eventually decide which Hunter is their 
sworn enemy, so it makes sense to have as many Nemeses 
in the Campaign as there are Hunters. Alternating the ap-
pearance of the Nemeses will make it easier to keep them 
from wearing out their welcome too quickly.
 ◆ A good alternative method of introducing the Nemesis 
can be through chance encounters. The Nemesis is pres-
ent in the Scenario, but with an Agenda that does not con-
cern the Hunters, and only tangentially touches on events. 
This will leave a trace of their passage for the Hunters to 
discover, which will then serve as a link in their future ap-
pearances. 
 ◆ Nemeses have great power even when they are behind the 
scenes. Gifts and Minions allow the Nemesis to influence 
the world around the Hunters without directly appearing 
on the Scene. These links should be outlined in Nemesis 
Connections. 
Don’t make the Nemeses the primary 
antagonist of a Scenario
The Nemesis’ job is to hinder the Hunters, but future con-
frontations will be more interesting if you don’t unleash all 
of their power against the Hunters right away. Nemeses can 
be really scary thanks to the Gifts of Nemesis and Inves-
titure, but they will lose their appeal if they play all their 
cards too soon. Each Scenario already has a plot and a cast 
of main NPCs that animate it, and replacing them with a 
Nemesis will only distract from the story. Therefore, do not 
make Nemeses the antagonist of a Scenario unless it is the 
final confrontation against them (see page 79).
Keep in mind that:
 ◆ The Nemesis must be a valuable aid to the Scenario’s 
NPCs, without replacing them in the main plot.
 ◆ Thanks to the Y ou Won the Battle, not the War Gift, 
a Nemesis can always make a run for it if the situation

79
takes a turn for the worse. For this reason, Nemeses 
can be useful as surprises that appear briefly during a 
Scene to add some excitement, then escape.
 ◆ Not every appearance of the Nemesis has to be a con-
flict. Clues, verbal confrontations, and Minions brag-
ging about their masters’ power are good systems for 
building up Hunters’ expectations for the arrival of 
these new adversaries.
 ◆ Encounters and Events are great ways to bring the 
Nemesis into the game. In these moments, the Nemesis 
can be the true antagonist, using Gifts and Minions to 
put the Hunters at risk.
 ◆ The Nemesis’ Gifts are meant to make them versatile, 
not overly powerful. Each encounter should bring out a 
different ability of the Nemesis so that their true threat 
level is gradually revealed to the Hunters.  
Defeating a Nemesis
Each Nemesis requires a unique approach to defeat, as-
suming that is the ultimate goal of the Hunters. As with 
all Broken Tales stories, it is the Player’s choices that create 
turning points in the plot. Like the NPCs presented in the 
Scenarios, the Nemesis isn’t a puppet with the sole purpose 
of harming the Hunters, but a well-rounded character who, 
if approached in the right way, may even understand and 
help the Order.
There are several ways to present the final confrontation 
with a Nemesis:
 ◆ Create a Scenario dedicated to the Nemesis, starting 
with the story in which they appear. If a Scenario based 
on that story already exists among the official Scenar -
ios, it is recommended that you don’t propose it to the 
Players and instead save it for the grand finale. Alter -
natively, it is possible to rewrite the Scenario, adapt it to 
what the Hunters have already done, and put the Nem-
esis in the role of the main antagonist.
 ◆ If the Hunters arrive at the final confrontation with 
the One Who Plots in the Shadows and the Nemeses 
are still in action, it is recommended that they be used 
during the Epilogue. One or more Nemesis could use 
Y ou Won the Battle, not the War during the con-
frontation, paving the way for the continuation of the 
game’s Campaign.
 ◆ If the Hunters are actively engaged in finding the Nem-
esis, it is only fair to reward them by creating a Sce-
nario that represents a confrontation with their enemy. 
However, in order to reach this Scenario, they should 
go through a series of encounters and a collection of 
useful clues to uncover the location of the Nemesis.
Nemeses in Single Scenarios
Although a Nemesis reaches their full potential during 
a Campaign, it is possible to include them when playing 
individual Scenarios. Before embarking on the adventure, 
the Storyteller should broadly introduce the Nemesis to 
the Hunters, specifying why they are on their trail. The first 
Nemesis Agenda provides good narrative hooks in this re-
gard. The Hunters will then be aware that an enemy is on 
their trail and could complicate their plans any moment.
Example:
The Storyteller has prepared the House of Roses Scenario, in-
spired by Beauty and the Beast and included in the Broken 
Ones expansion. The Hunter party consists of Garou the Old 
Wolf, James the Swordsman, Baba Yaga the Child Witch, and 
Niklaus von Krampus. With as many as three Hunters tied to 
something monstrous, and a main NPC with the character -
istics of a monster (Dionysus Gonsalva), the Storyteller iden-
tifies the Relentless Beastslayer as an excellent Nemesis for 
the game. The first Nemesis Agenda reads: “Track down and 
eliminate any monster that threatens humanity. ”

80
The Storyteller then introduces the Nemesis to the group of 
Hunters: They had previously encountered a strange figure 
cloaked in black who had tried to kill Garou and Niklaus sev-
eral times because of their “monstrous” appearance. This is a 
dangerous, merciless, and ruthless individual, a beast-killer 
who seems to be obsessed with monsters. 
 
Players are unaware that there is a monstrous NPC in 
the Scenario, and this combination of characters will 
certainly lead to interesting situations.
Even if introduced at the beginning, a Nemesis may need 
further hooks to the narrative:
 ◆ The Nemesis has allied themselves with an NPC in the 
Scenario to whom they provide support. They will not 
act directly except to help that NPC achieve their goals.
 ◆ Nemeses will come into play as the price for an Inter -
lude, giving players the distinct feeling that they have 
made things more difficult by wasting time. 
 ◆ The Nemesis waits for the right moment to strike with 
a direct attack. They will do so at a transitional mo-
ment in the Scenario’s plot when their appearance can 
seamlessly fit into events and will not interfere with the 
actions of the Main NPCs or even the events of the Sce-
nario itself. The Nemesis attacks the Hunters and will 
be able to escape through the Gift Y ou Won the Battle, 
not the War.
The Five Nemeses
Below are the five Nemeses created by the One Who Plots 
in the Shadows to fight the Hunters. Each Nemesis is de-
rived from a fairytale archetype:
 ◆ The Huntsman, on which the Relentless Beastslayer is 
based, represents the deus ex machina that intervenes 
to save the protagonists, a force of nature that punishes 
monsters and villains.
 ◆ The Lady of the Lake, the inspiration for the Wise 
Fairy, is the powerful fairy whose gifts give the heroes 
the power to win.
 ◆ Thumbling (inspiring the King of Crime) and Viola  
(the archetypal Valiant Princess) represent the young 
protagonists who manage to defeat the villains with 
cunning and courage.
 ◆ The Naked Emperor, a direct reference for the Mad 
Emperor, represents truth overcoming lies, which, 
when revealed, exposes deception.

81
The Relentless 
Beastslayer  
Inspired by the Huntsman of many fairytales
Once upon a time, there was a skilled Beastslayer with a good 
heart who traveled the world, offering his skills to small vil-
lages. The Beastslayer feared nothing, and more than once his 
courage had saved helpless people from the threat of monsters 
and wild beasts. One day, the Beastslayer was summoned to 
a mission by the queen. Rumor had it that the queen was 
actually a witch, but all the subjects were happy under her 
leadership, and the Beastslayer decided to trust her anyway. 
The queen had a very special request for him: to escort her 
stepdaughter into the forest and remove her heart, for she 
was actually a demon hiding in the guise of a maiden. The 
Beastslayer refused to believe the queen, but she cast a spell 
to deceive his eyes, and what the man saw was exactly what 
the witch wanted. Bewitched and unable to object, the Beast-
slayer took the young woman into the woods and cut out her 
heart with a precise stroke of his blade. When he came to 
his senses, he tried to take revenge, only to discover that he 
was wanted throughout the kingdom for the murder of the 
young princess. The queen witch had used him as a scapegoat 
so she could appear blameless before the king. Disappoint-
ed at his helplessness in the face of such evil , the 
Beastslayer fled far away to the shelter of the great forest, and 
with his hands still stained with blood, he finally came to a 
small cottage. Finding the door open, he entered to see who 
lived there, just in time to see a huge wolf devouring an old 
grandmother and her young granddaughter. The Beastslay-
er attacked the monster, which was still intent on chewing 
the little girl’s bones between its sharp fangs. After a furious 
struggle, the wolf prevailed, leaving the Beastslayer on the 
ground in the small yard of the house. With his belly ripped 
open, but still alive, the Beastslayer found himself cursing all 
monsters, in body and soul, who mercilessly slaughtered in-
nocent souls. And as his tears mingled with the rain that be-
gan to fall, he made an oath: he would become the scourge of 
every aberration, the shadow that would appear to mete out 
just punishment to every monster. Without mercy. 
Descriptor: I am a skilled huntsman and have all the 
means to kill any monster. I never say a word and try to keep 
myself aloof from everyone.
5 Wounds
Opposition Level: Medium 5 
Agenda 1:  Track down and eliminate any monster that 
threatens humanity.
Agenda 2: Set traps to kill monsters/Teach others how to 
kill monsters. 
Agenda 3: Kill the Hunters by taking them by surprise / 
Challenge the Hunters on the battleground most advanta-
geous to me.
Agenda 4:  Form the Beast Slayer Guild and recruit the 
most skilled individuals into its ranks / Capture and train 
monsters to use as weapons against other monsters. 
Gift - Hunt Master:  Once per Scenario, the Beastslayer 
can appear in the Scene from a shadowy area or patch of 
vegetation and act immediately in the Exchange. The Beast-
slayer is completely immune to all fear-inducing effects and 
any manipulation (magical or otherwise) that would dis-
tract him from his goal: killing monsters.    
Gift - Nemesis:  See page 73. Sworn Enemies: Hunters 
with monstrous traits or monsters.
Gift of Investiture - The Blade of Punishment:  
When the Beastslayer attacks a target with monstrous traits,

82
the blow always hits, with no chance to make a Defense Check. 
Wounds inflicted with this weapon are conspicuous and impossible 
to conceal, and cannot be healed in any way until the end of the 
Scenario.  
Minions:
Hounds
The Relentless Beastslayer has trained many hounds, teaching 
them to face monsters without fear.
OL Medium 5 - Minor NPC (1 Wound)
Descriptor: I am an agile hunting dog, ready to carry out any 
command of my master. 
Agenda: Follow my master on hunting trips and serve him 
even at the cost of my life. 
Gift - Perfect Training: As long as the dog is focused on 
its master, it cannot be distracted in any way. The NPC is 
immune to all fear and mind control effects. When focus-
ing on a target to restrain it and prevent it from escaping, 
the Hound uses OL Hard 7.

83
The Twelve Poachers 
Inspired by the Twelve Huntsmen [Opponent)
Once upon a time, there was a princess who was deeply in 
love with a prince. The two longed to marry, but the young 
man’s father had already promised him to another noble 
maiden. Unable to resist the last wishes of his dying father, 
the prince abandoned the princess and went through with 
the arranged marriage. Destroyed by grief, the abandoned 
princess hatched a terrible deception. Together with eleven 
other girls who closely resembled her, she pretended to be a 
huntsman in the service of the prince and accompanied him 
on every hunting trip. During one of these trips, the princess 
staged an accident and pierced the new queen’s heart with 
an arrow. That same night, she presented herself to her lov-
er and revealed what she had done, confident that he would 
understand. But he did not: he was horrified and ordered the 
guards to kill the princess who had posed as a huntsman. The 
young woman managed to escape with her companions and, 
convinced that she was in the right, took up poaching, wait-
ing for a new love to understand her heart.
OL Medium 5 
(1 Wound - 12 Gang Members)
Descriptor: We are skilled hunters and we disguise our -
selves as ordinary girls. We have no qualms about achieving 
our goal. 
Agenda: Serving the Relentless Beastslayer and dispensing 
punishment in the name of our warped sense of justice.
Gift - A Delicate Maiden:  Unless a Poacher unleashes 
her hunting weapons, she will look just like a normal vil-
lager. No NPC will consider her a threat. When a Poacher 
reveals her true nature, her OL increases by 1 for the first 
action she participates in.  
•
Connections: Monsters / Villagers in danger / Vicious 
beasts / Hunting / Traps / Defending a location.
Red-Hood Iskra: Assist Vincent Arn, an old hunting com-
panion / Help Elizaveta get rid of her monstrous daughter.
Hunger Island: Retrieve the Stone that Came from the Sky 
so that it cannot spawn new monsters / Prevent the inhabi-
tants and any visitors from leaving the island, as they may 
be infected.
Bloodwhite’s Heart: Finding a way to cure Bianca of her 
condition / Helping Svenn preserve the Karenmann family 
secret.
Nikola’s Pledge: Capture the fearsome Badalisk for later 
training. Use the village of Rovaniemi as bait to lure the crea-
ture / Help Nikola defend himself from intruders in exchange 
for his monstrous Reindeer, which could be trained for mon-
ster hunting.

84
The Valiant Princess 
Inspired by Tale of Tales
Once upon a time, there was a noble regent of Altomonte who became morbidly attached 
to a flea. The old man cared for the flea as if it were a daughter and allowed it to grow to 
the size of a large dog. The regent treated the flea like a member of his family, even going 
so far as to neglect Viola, his only real daughter. The young girl suffered the humiliation 
of being second in importance in her father’s eyes, and when a rich and ill-mannered 
suitor came to ask for the princess’ hand, the Regent of Altomonte gave his consent to the 
marriage, ignoring his daughter’s protests. The prospective bridegroom was a lecherous 
old man, and there were ugly rumors that he had been involved in the deaths of his 
previous brides. Disappointed by her father’s insensitivity and her suitor’s 
wickedness, Viola left for her new home with her future husband, despairing of the 
fate that awaited her because of men’s power games. But in the young princess’ heart 
burned the fire of courage, and when she arrived at her destination, Viola refused to 
participate in the marriage ceremony. As punishment, the old man locked her in the 
dungeon of his castle, where she lived on bread and water until she agreed to marry 
him. It was in the dungeons that Viola met seven brothers dedicated to smuggling 
and the use of magic so feared by the Church. With the help of the seven, Viola 
escaped from prison and that very night, she stabbed the old man in the heart 
as he lay in bed with two concubines. The seven brothers were so impressed by 
Viola’s courage that they swore allegiance to her. They had always lived to do 
wrong, but now they had an example of justice to follow. The young prin-
cess then returned to her father, hoping that he would come to his senses. 
Instead, he accused Viola of treason for breaking the sacred traditions 
of the nobility: a woman like her could not afford to decide for herself 
what was right or wrong.
With tears of grief in her eyes, Viola tried to leave, but the flea, 
as if sensing her pain, acted for her. The insect leaped to her 
father’s neck and cut his throat cleanly. The dying man 
waved his arms at Viola for help, but the girl had 
dried her tears and had no more words for her fa-
ther.

85
From that day on, Viola took charge, and with her trusty flea 
at her side, she rewrote all the laws of Altomonte so that no 
young woman would ever again have to endure the torment 
of a society run by the will of men alone.
Descriptor: I am a brave princess who fights for the free-
dom of all oppressed youth. The passion that drives me allows 
me to overcome any obstacle and ignore any pain.    
4 Wounds
Opposition Level: Medium 5
Agenda 1: Liberate all young people who are oppressed by 
society. 
Agenda 2: Support those who rise up against oppression / 
Make the evil of the oppressors known to all.
Agenda 3: Use good-hearted people to oppose the Hunters / 
Challenge the Hunters openly and boldly. 
Agenda 4: Unite the weak and marginalized to create a 
network to help others / Accumulate wealth to gain power in 
society and impose more just laws and rules, disrupting secret 
societies like the Order. 
Gift - Porziella the Flea: The Valiant Princess is always 
accompanied by her trusty flea, who guards her like a faith-
ful dog. Porziella is not considered an NPC but will act on 
Viola’s behalf a second time in the Exchange. Porziella’s 
skin is as hard as stone, so the bug cannot be removed by 
normal means.
Gift - Nemesis:  See page 73. Sworn Enemies: Hunters 
with malicious intent and men who represent authority.
Gift of Investiture - The Heart of Courage:  The Val-
iant Princess is considered to have OL Hard 7 against all 
actions designed to frighten her or restrain her. Once per 
Scene, the Heart of Courage can negate the effect of any 
Gift used against the Valiant Princess.
Minions:
The Seven Shady Brothers 
Inspired by The Flea (Opponent)
Once upon a time, there were seven brothers who dedicated 
themselves to a life of banditry and the reckless use of mag-
ic. After a robbery gone wrong, the seven were arrested and 
sentenced to the gallows. In prison, they met a brave prin-
cess who had been imprisoned not for a crime, but simply 
for wanting the freedom to choose her own life. Impressed by 
such courage, the seven brothers helped the princess escape, 
and once free, they swore allegiance to her. In her service, 
they would finally do something good to redeem themselves 
from a life of causing misery. 
OL Medium 5 
(1 Wound - 7 Gang Members)
Descriptor: We are young brigands who learned magic 
from our late mother, a witch. We hate law and religion, in-
stitutions that have always persecuted what they do not un-
derstand. 
Agenda: Help the Valiant Princess subvert the order and 
dogmas of society.    
Gift - Scoundrel Magic: Each of the Seven Shady Broth-
ers has a magical power they can use during the course of 
a Scene.
Mase: He has extraordinary hearing and can hear a 
speech even through a wall. All Checks performed to 
avoid him hearing something are done with 2 Penal-
ties.
Nardo: He can excrete soap from his skin and slip 
through any crevice. He can also automatically free 
himself from any physical obstacle.

86
Cola: He has a razor-sharp right hand and inflicts 2 
Wounds with all his attacks.
Micco: He can make the old wooden stick he always 
carries grow at will. The weapon can stretch up to sixty 
feet or expand to the size of a door.
Petrullo: He can create a water jet from his hands that 
can hit enemies and objects up to ten meters away. 
Ascadeo: He can throw one of the many rocks in his 
bag, always hitting the target. When he throws a stone, 
Ascadeo acts at OL Hard 7. 
Ceccone: He can magically guide the arrows he shoots 
with his crossbow, sending them on impossible trajec-

The King of Crime 
Inspired by Thumbling
Once upon a time, a terrible famine struck a remote village, so that a woodcutter and his wife could not feed 
their seven children. They made a fateful decision: to abandon the children in the forest. The youngest of them 
all was named Thumbling, and though he was tiny in stature, he possessed a cunning that would make the 
sliest of brigands envious. Once alone in the forest, Thumbling’s six siblings despaired, but the little one took 
charge and led them all to a small house whose light illuminated the darkness of the night. Throughout 
the journey, the brothers and sisters complained and lamented every misfortune, but Thumbling was 
undaunted. When they arrived at the house, the seven children were greeted by a kind woman who 
made them comfortable and served them a hot meal. All the siblings were happy to have found a new 
home, but Thumbling was wary of trusting such kindness. He warned his brothers and sisters, who 
mocked him. Tired and with full bellies, the siblings went to sleep, ignoring the doubts of Thumbling, 
who decided to stay awake to keep watch. At the stroke of midnight, a fearsome ogre returned to 
the house. Thumbling overheard him talking to his wife, complimenting her for providing him with 
juicy children to devour the next day. Hearing of the terrible deception, Thumbling devised a plan 
to save his brothers and sisters: at night, taking advantage of his diminutive size, he slipped out of 
the room and slit the throat of the ogre and his wife as they slept. Stained with blood and shaken 
by what he had done, Thumbling clung to the happiness of having saved his beloved brothers and 
sisters. When they awoke, they saw what Thumbling had done and immediately chased him out 
of the house, accusing him of killing out of spite the only people who had cared for them. Hurt 
by these words, Thumbling ran far away. Ten years passed, and Thumbling had become im-
mensely wealthy. Using his brilliant mind, the young man had risen through the ranks of the 
underworld to become one of the most powerful criminals in all of Europe. Loaded with coins 
and jewels, Thumbling returned to the ogre’s home, where he knew his siblings had settled. 
Confident that he would surprise everyone with the gifts he had brought, he approached 
the window...
In the festively decorated hall, he saw his six siblings toasting with their parents, gathered 
together like a real family, enjoying the riches found in the ogre’s house. A family por
-
trait towered over the richly decorated hall: father, mother, and six children. 
Thumbling’s eyes narrowed to a slit: they had forgotten him, after all he had done 
for them.

88
Disappointed at the abandonment of the only peo-
ple he had ever loved , Thumbling dropped a large dia -
mond on the doorstep, knocked, and left. He heard his father’s 
agitated voice when he opened the door and saw such a beau-
tiful gift abandoned. 
Returning to his hiding place, Thumbling wrote an open let-
ter to all the criminals of Europe: the famous Ogre’s Eye, the 
largest diamond ever seen in Europe, was in a small house in 
the woods, guarded by a gang of petty thieves. 
It took less than a day for the news to reach him: an entire 
family, father, mother, and six siblings, had been mercilessly 
slaughtered by an army of criminals who had come from far 
and wide. Almost nothing was left of the poor people’s bodies, 
dismembered in the course of the most unspeakable torture. 
As the most skilled of his assassins returned to the lair with 
the Ogre’s Eye, Thumbling sighed and then smiled at another 
successful plan.

The Raven Clan 
Inspired by the Seven Ravens [Opponent)
Once upon a time, there was a family in which only sons 
were born. The father and mother longed for a daughter

baby girl finally arrived, the family’s enthusiasm quickly fad-

over a piece of meat. The witch heard the man’s grief and,

forever condemned to a life as watchers, never to be shown 
affection again.

The Wise Fairy  
Inspired by the Lady of the Lake from the Arthurian Myth
Once upon a time, there was a powerful fairy whose birth was 
lost in the mists of time. Always driven by a kind heart and 
an altruistic spirit, the fairy gave her help to those in need 
without ever asking for anything in return. Soon, people from 
every land came to the lake where the fairy lived to ask for 
her help. No matter how much good she did, the pilgrims 
kept coming. So the fairy decided to give some of her magic 
to chosen people to travel the world and bring help to all who 
needed it. The fairy forged objects of great power, raised little 
orphans to become heroes, and taught her spells to wise old 
men so they could help humanity. After more than a hundred 
years of effort, the fairy found herself exhausted and almost 
completely drained of power. Weary but content, she let her-
self fall to the bottom of her lake and fell into a restful sleep. 
More than a decade passed before she awoke: she had slept 
peacefully, knowing that her chosen ones had fulfilled their 
mission to help others. But when she looked out at the world 
again, she was dismayed by what she saw. The champions she 
had armed, the heroes she had raised, and the wizards she 
had taught had all abandoned the path of benevolence to pur-
sue selfish personal gain. Disappointed by the darkness 
of the human soul , the fairy vowed that she would never 
again use her power to do good, but only to increase her own 
power and judge the world from the heights of her superiority.

91

icated. 
Agenda 3: Use my power to weaken the Hunters and turn

ness.
Agenda 4: Create an army of champions who yearn for

The Mad Emperor  
Inspired By The Emperor’s New Clothes
Once upon a time, there was a vain Emperor who loved 
to adorn himself with fine jewelry and dress in the latest 
fashions. The Emperor had little interest in governing his 
lands, leaving that task to his trusted advisors. This allowed 
him to devote his full attention to festivals and receptions, 
all good occasions to show off his best clothes. The Emperor 
was not an evil person, he simply preferred to entrust tasks 
to the most competent rather than the highest ranking. The 
Emperor was also fond of the arts, and under his reign, all 
people, even the poorest, had the opportunity to indulge 
their passions, receiving substantial donations from the Em-
peror himself. Happy to be able to give beautiful creations 
to the world, the Emperor did not notice that his advisors 
and subjects soon began to take advantage of his generos-
ity. Everyone complimented the Emperor: “What graceful 
movements His Majesty makes”, “What elegant clothes Your 
Grace wears”... even when he was barely awake and wearing 
only simple pajamas. Doubt began to creep into the naive 
ruler’s heart, and when he summoned the kingdom’s trea-
surer, the harsh reality gave shape to the prison of deception 
and self-satisfaction in which he had been caught. While the 
Emperor was preoccupied with beauty and art, his minis-
ters, with the consent of all the citizens, had plundered every 
resource. Disappointed by the lies and false compli-
ments, the Emperor summoned the general of the army 
and his soldiers. All the townspeople and ministers were 
herded into the main square, where, one by one, they were 
blinded by the soldiers’ blades. Naked, with only the crown 
on his head, the Emperor walked among the desperate and 
suffering people and asked, “How do you find me today? Do 
you like my elegant attire?” and everyone nodded, seeing 
nothing, in fear of losing their lives as well as their eyes.
